```{r}
#| label: triangle_visualization
triangle_df <- data.frame(
  label = c("Cognition", "Language", "Behavior"),
  x = c(0, 1, 0.5),
  y = c(0, 0, sqrt(3)/2),
  color = c("pastelblue", "pastelred", "pastelgreen")
)

# Edges of the triangle
edges <- data.frame(
  x = c(0, 1, 0.5, 0),
  y = c(0, 0, sqrt(3)/2, 0)
)

# Define pastel colors
pastel_palette <- c(
  "pastelblue" = "#A7C7E7",
  "pastelred"  = "#F4A7A3",
  "pastelgreen" = "#A8E6A3"
)

triangle_plot <- ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(
    data = edges, aes(x, y),
    fill = NA, color = "grey20", linewidth = 0.3
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = triangle_df,
    aes(x = x, y = y, color = color),
    size = 1
  ) +
  labs(
    x = " ",
    y = " ",
    color = NULL,                # legend title
    subtitle = "A"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = pastel_palette,
    labels = triangle_df$label   # <-- labels for legend, replaces geom_label text
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "left",           # put legend below plot
    legend.direction = "vertical",
    legend.box.spacing = unit(0, "pt"),
    legend.key.size = unit(6, "pt"),
    legend.text = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.9, color = "grey20"),
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks = element_blank(),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: example_beads_task
example_round_timecourses <- read.csv(file.path(root, "triangulation/example_timecourses_entropy_groups.csv"))

# Tidy labels: one series per (Participant_ID, round_idx)
df_plot <- example_round_timecourses %>%
  mutate(
    entropy_group = factor(
      entropy_group,
      levels = c("Low entropy", "High entropy"),
      labels = c("Low belief instability (low update entropy)", "High belief instability (high update entropy)")
    ),
    series_id = paste0(Participant_ID, "-r", round_idx)
  )

# 1) one series per (Participant_ID, round_idx), then pick 3 per facet
series_pool <- df_plot %>%
  distinct(entropy_group, series_id)

picked <- series_pool %>%
  dplyr::group_by(entropy_group) %>%
  dplyr::slice_sample(n = 3) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(example = paste0("ex", row_number())) %>%
  ungroup()

# 2) keep only the picked series, add example labels
df_small <- df_plot %>%
  inner_join(picked, by = c("entropy_group", "series_id")) %>%
  mutate(example = factor(example, levels = c("ex1","ex2","ex3")))

## 3) plot with SAME greys across facets (mapped by example)
p_example_beads <- ggplot(df_small, aes(x = draw_idx, y = True_Label, group = series_id, color = example)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_point(size = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~entropy_group, scales = "fixed", ncol = 1) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:8, limits = c(1, 8)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("ex1" = "grey30", "ex2" = "grey50", "ex3" = "grey70")) +
  labs(
    title = "D",
    subtitle = "Belief instability as a \nlatent cognitive phenotype",
    x = "Trial",
    y = "Belief"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
    legend.position = "none"
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: corr_pca_original_language_congition

all_corrs_entropy_pca <- read.csv(file.path(root, "triangulation/all_corrs_entropy_signed_pca.csv"))
df_pca <- all_corrs_entropy_pca %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Target = factor(Target, levels = c("True", "Predicted")),
    Dimension = as.character(Dimension),
    Dimension_display = forcats::fct_rev(factor(Dimension, levels = unique(Dimension))),
    Source = "PCA"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(Source, Dimension, Dimension_display, Target, r)

# --- Original questionnaire data ---
all_corrs_entropy_original <- read.csv(file.path(root, "triangulation/all_corrs_entropy_signed_original.csv"))
all_corrs_entropy_original$Dimension <- stringr::str_replace_all(
  all_corrs_entropy_original$Dimension, "\\-", " "
)

df_orig <- all_corrs_entropy_original %>%
  dplyr::filter(Metric == "entropy_signed") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Target = factor(Target, levels = c("True", "Predicted"))
  ) %>%
  dplyr::filter(is.finite(r))

dim_order <- df_orig %>%
  dplyr::filter(Target == "True", is.finite(r)) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(desc(r)) %>%
  dplyr::pull(Dimension)

df_orig <- df_orig %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Dimension = as.character(Dimension),
    Dimension_display = factor(Dimension, levels = unique(ordered_standard_dimensions)),
    Source = "Original"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(Source, Dimension, Dimension_display, Target, r)

# --- Combine for bar/linerange panel ---
df_all <- dplyr::bind_rows(df_pca, df_orig)

pc_levels <- c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3")
rest_levels <- ordered_standard_dimensions
rest_levels <- rest_levels[!rest_levels %in% pc_levels]
final_levels <- c(pc_levels, rest_levels)

df_all <- df_all %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Dimension_display = factor(as.character(Dimension_display), levels = final_levels)
  )

pd <- position_dodge(width = 0.75)

# Merge palettes so both panels resolve their named colors
palette_all <- c(pc_palette, custom_colors)

p_corr_bigrid <- ggplot(
  subset(df_all, Source == "PCA"),
  aes(x = Dimension_display, y = r, color = Dimension, shape = Target, linetype = Target)
) +
  geom_linerange(
    aes(ymin = 0, ymax = r),
    position = pd,
    linewidth = 0.5,
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  geom_point(position = pd, size = 1.2, alpha = 0.95) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(True = 16, Predicted = 15)) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c(True = "solid", Predicted = "dotted"), guide = "none") +
  scale_color_manual(values = palette_all, guide = "none") +
  labs(
    y = "R: Ground truth & MAILA ~ Belief instability",
    x = "PCA",
    shape = NULL,
    title = " ",
    subtitle = " "
  ) +
  guides(
    shape = guide_legend(
      ncol = 1,
      byrow = TRUE,
      override.aes = list(linetype = NA)
    )
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
    legend.position = c(0.4, 0.2),
    legend.justification = c(0.5, 1),
    legend.direction = "vertical",
    legend.box = "vertical",
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = NA, color = NA),
    legend.key = element_rect(fill = NA, color = NA),
    legend.key.width = unit(0.55, "lines"),
    legend.key.height = unit(0.55, "lines"),
    legend.spacing.y = unit(0.1, "lines"),
    legend.text = element_text(margin = margin(l = -1))
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off")

# --- Prepare data for scatter: include PCA + Original ---
scatter_corr_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(df_pca, df_orig) %>%
  dplyr::select(Source, Dimension, Target, r) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = Target, values_from = r) %>%
  dplyr::filter(is.finite(True), is.finite(Predicted))

# --- Scatter plot (True vs Predicted correlations) ---
library(ggrepel)

scatter_corr_plot <- ggplot(
  scatter_corr_df,
  aes(x = True, y = Predicted, color = Dimension)
) +
  geom_point(size = 1, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text_repel(
    aes(label = Dimension),
    size = 1.5,
    show.legend = FALSE,
    segment.size = 0.2,
    segment.alpha = 0.6,
    box.padding = 0.2,
    point.padding = 0.15,
    max.overlaps = Inf
  ) +
  geom_abline(
    slope = 1, intercept = 0,
    linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.3
  ) +
  stat_cor(
    aes(x = True, y = Predicted),
    method = "spearman",
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    label.x.npc = "left",
    label.y.npc = "top",
    size = 1.5
  ) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  labs(
    x = "R (Self-report)",
    y = "R (MAILA)",
    color = "Dimension",
    title = "C",
    subtitle = "Belief instability aligns with self-reported & predicted mental health"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = palette_all) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(1, "lines")
  )

STAT.cor_entropy <- cor.test(
  scatter_corr_df$True,
  scatter_corr_df$Predicted,
  method = "spearman",
  use = "complete.obs"
)
```

```{r}
#| label: cv_maila_language_congition

res_path  <- file.path(root, "triangulation", "entropy_signed_conditions_summary.csv")
perm_path <- file.path(root, "triangulation", "entropy_signed_permuted_rhos.csv")

COND_ORDER <- c("Self-report", "MAILA", "Self-report + MAILA")

entropy_prediction_real_df <- read.csv(res_path) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Condition = dplyr::recode(
      Condition,
      "MentalHealth" = "Self-report",
      "Language"     = "Self-report",
      "Cursor"       = "MAILA",
      "Combined"     = "Self-report + MAILA",
      .default       = Condition
    ),
    Condition = factor(Condition, levels = COND_ORDER)
  )

perm_df <- read.csv(perm_path) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Condition = dplyr::recode(
      Condition,
      "MentalHealth" = "Self-report",
      "Language"     = "Self-report",
      "Cursor"       = "MAILA",
      "Combined"     = "Self-report + MAILA",
      .default       = Condition
    ),
    Condition = factor(Condition, levels = COND_ORDER)
  ) %>%
  dplyr::filter(is.finite(Permuted_R))

entropy_prediction_real_long <- entropy_prediction_real_df %>%
  dplyr::transmute(
    Condition = factor(Condition, levels = COND_ORDER),
    Metric = "r",
    Value = rho
  )

entropy_segment_df <- entropy_prediction_real_long %>%
  dplyr::filter(Condition %in% c("Self-report", "Self-report + MAILA")) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from = Condition,
    values_from = Value
  ) %>%
  dplyr::rename(
    r_self_report = `Self-report`,
    r_combined = `Self-report + MAILA`
  )

SHAPES_COND_FILL <- c(
  "MAILA" = 21,
  "Self-report" = 22,
  "Self-report + MAILA" = 23
)

p_entropy_conditions <- ggplot() +
  geom_violin(
    data = perm_df,
    aes(x = Condition, y = Permuted_R),
    alpha = 0.10,
    width = 0.5,
    bw = 0.005,
    fill = "black",
    color = NA,
    trim = TRUE
  ) +
  geom_linerange(
    data = entropy_prediction_real_long,
    aes(x = Condition, ymin = 0, ymax = Value),
    linewidth = 0.5,
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = entropy_prediction_real_long,
    aes(x = Condition, y = Value, shape = Condition),
    size = 1,
    alpha = 0.95,
    stroke = 0.6,
    fill = "black",
    color = "black"
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.25
  ) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    breaks = COND_ORDER,
    values = SHAPES_COND_FILL
  ) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "R",
    title = "D",
    subtitle = "MAILA improves the \ndecoding of belief instability"
  ) +
  guides(
    shape = guide_legend(
      nrow = 1,
      byrow = TRUE,
      override.aes = list(
        color = "black",
        fill = "black",
        size = 1,
        stroke = 0.6,
        alpha = 1
      )
    )
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, vjust = 1),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(1, "lines")
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(drop = FALSE, limits = COND_ORDER) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.3, 0.3))

##
## Error triangulation (NRMSE)
##
error_comparison_triangulation <- read.csv(file.path(root, "triangulation", "error_comparison.csv"))

STAT.error_comparison_triangulation <- t.test(
  error_comparison_triangulation$NRMSE_Combined,
  error_comparison_triangulation$NRMSE_MH,
  paired = TRUE
)

error_comparison_triangulation_long <- error_comparison_triangulation %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("NRMSE_"),
    names_to = "Method",
    values_to = "Value"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::filter(is.finite(Value)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Method = dplyr::recode(
      Method,
      "NRMSE_MH" = "Self-report",
      "NRMSE_Cursor" = "MAILA",
      "NRMSE_Combined" = "Self-report + MAILA"
    ),
    Method = factor(Method, levels = COND_ORDER)
  )

error_comparison_triangulation_avg <- ddply(
  error_comparison_triangulation_long,
  .(Method),
  summarise,
  average = mean_ci_95(Value)$mean,
  error = mean_ci_95(Value)$error,
  n = sum(is.finite(Value))
)

p_error_comparison <- ggplot() +
  geom_linerange(
    data = error_comparison_triangulation_avg,
    aes(x = Method, ymin = average - error, ymax = average + error),
    linewidth = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = subset(error_comparison_triangulation_avg, Method == "Self-report")$average,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.25
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = subset(error_comparison_triangulation_avg, Method %in% c("Self-report", "Self-report + MAILA")),
    aes(x = Method, y = average, shape = Method),
    size = 1,
    alpha = 0.95,
    stroke = 0.6,
    fill = "black",
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = subset(error_comparison_triangulation_avg, Method == "MAILA"),
    aes(x = Method, y = average, shape = Method),
    size = 1,
    alpha = 0.95,
    stroke = 0.6,
    fill = "white",
    color = "black"
  ) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    breaks = COND_ORDER,
    values = SHAPES_COND_FILL
  ) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "e",
    title = "G",
    subtitle = "Highest accuracy for \nMAILA + Self-reports"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, vjust = 1),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(1, "lines")
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(drop = FALSE, limits = COND_ORDER)

```

```{r}
#| label: partial_corr_maila_language_congition
library(ppcor)
predicted_entropy = read.csv(file.path(root, "triangulation/entropy_signed_conditions_true_vs_predicted.csv"))

pred_wide <- predicted_entropy %>%
  dplyr::group_by(uid) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(
    True             = first(True),
    Pred_MentalHealth = Pred[Condition == "MentalHealth"][1],
    Pred_Cursor       = Pred[Condition == "Cursor"][1],
    Pred_Combined     = Pred[Condition == "Combined"][1],
    .groups = "drop"
  )

STAT.pcor_mh_given_cursor <- pcor.test(
  x = pred_wide$Pred_MentalHealth,
  y = pred_wide$True,
  z = pred_wide$Pred_Cursor
)

STAT.pcor_cursor_given_mh <- pcor.test(
  x = pred_wide$Pred_Cursor,
  y = pred_wide$True,
  z = pred_wide$Pred_MentalHealth
)
```

We next asked whether human-computer interactions can help predict cognitive markers of mental health. Specifically, we tested whether MAILA could improve the decoding of belief instability, a latent cognitive phenotype describing how readily individuals update internal models in response to new evidence [@so_jumping_2016]. Belief instability is typically inferred from controlled behavioral experiments in which participants integrate sequential noisy observations. Rigid updating has been linked to perseveration and compulsive control, whereas overly flexible updating may manifest as volatile, inconsistent inference associated with impulsivity or disorganized behavior [@so_jumping_2016]. In the MAILA dataset, 600 participants completed a gamified beads task that measured belief updating under uncertainty. On each round, participants inferred the hidden source of a sequence of colored beads and reported their confidence after each new observation. We quantified belief instability as the entropy of these confidence-weighted belief updates: low entropy reflected stable, conservative inference, whereas high entropy reflected reactive, variable updating in response to new evidence [@trepka_entropy-based_2021] (see Methods for details).

Our data confirmed that belief instability in the beads task mapped onto mental health along a continuum from reactive to rigid cognition [@so_jumping_2016], with positive associations with hostility and somatization, and negative associations with depression, obsession-compulsion, and interpersonal sensitivity (Figure 5C). These associations persisted when self-reports were replaced by MAILA predictions derived solely from cursor movements in held-out participants: from human–computer interactions alone, MAILA recovered the group-level association between mental-health dimensions and belief instability at R = `r STAT.cor_entropy$estimate` (p = `r STAT.cor_entropy$p.value`). Thus, latent cognitive traits typically inferred from controlled laboratory paradigms also left measurable signatures in everyday digital behavior.

MAILA’s movement embeddings predicted unique variance in belief instability over and above all available self-reports (cross-validated partial correlation controlling for self-reports: R = `r STAT.pcor_cursor_given_mh$estimate`, p = `r STAT.pcor_cursor_given_mh$p.value`). Self-reports also carried unique, albeit weaker, information about belief instability after controlling for cursor-derived predictions (R = `r STAT.pcor_mh_given_cursor$estimate`, p = `r STAT.pcor_mh_given_cursor$p.value`). Combining self-reports and cursor-based features achieved the highest performance (R = `r subset(entropy_prediction_real_df, Condition == "Self-report + MAILA")$rho`, p = `r subset(entropy_prediction_real_df, Condition == "Self-report + MAILA")$pval`) and significantly reduced prediction error relative to self-reports alone (p = `r STAT.error_comparison_triangulation$p.value`; Figure 5D).

Together, these results show that human–computer interactions encode cognitive information that is not fully captured by language-based mental-health assessments. Data-driven markers extracted from everyday digital behavior can therefore validate, refine, and extend established cognitive phenotypes of mental health, providing a step toward scalable, data-driven models of mental health.

## MAILA improves the ability of frontier AI chatbots to infer user mental health 

```{r}
#| label: maila_diagnostic_interview_setup
#| include: false
#| cache: false

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(stringr)
library(ggplot2)
library(lmerTest)

v3_analysis_dir <- "../language_mind"

assistant_condition_labels <- c(
  "maila_empirical" = "MAILA",
  "no_aux" = "No context",
  "maila_random" = "Random MAILA"
)

# Display order in the figure
assistant_condition_order <- c("Random MAILA", "No context", "MAILA")

assistant_shape_map <- c(
  "Random MAILA" = 17,
  "No context" = 16,
  "MAILA" = 15
)

prediction_files <- c(
  "no_aux" = "condition_turn_predictions__no_aux.csv",
  "maila_random" = "condition_turn_predictions__maila_random.csv",
  "maila_empirical" = "condition_turn_predictions__maila_empirical.csv"
)

assistant_predictions <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  lapply(names(prediction_files), function(cond) {
    read.csv(
      file.path(v3_analysis_dir, prediction_files[[cond]]),
      colClasses = c(
        "run_id" = "character",
        "profile_id" = "character",
        "aux_source_profile_id" = "character",
        "scenario_id" = "character",
        "scenario_title" = "character",
        "scenario_context" = "character",
        "scenario_use_type" = "character",
        "target_text" = "character"
      )
    ) |>
      dplyr::mutate(condition = cond)
  })
)

assistant_prediction_eval <- assistant_predictions |>
  dplyr::filter(condition %in% names(assistant_condition_labels)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    dimension = stringr::str_replace_all(dimension, "\\-", " "),
    true_value = as.numeric(true_value),
    predicted_value = as.numeric(predicted_value),
    abs_error = as.numeric(abs_error),
    norm_squared_error = ((predicted_value - true_value) / 100)^2,
    ConditionLabel = dplyr::recode(condition, !!!assistant_condition_labels),
    ConditionLabel = factor(ConditionLabel, levels = assistant_condition_order),
    condition = factor(
      condition,
      levels = c("maila_empirical", "no_aux", "maila_random")
    ),
    dimension = factor(dimension)
  )

assistant_final_turn <- max(assistant_prediction_eval$turn_index, na.rm = TRUE)

assistant_final_predictions <- assistant_prediction_eval |>
  dplyr::filter(turn_index == assistant_final_turn)

# Use the manuscript-wide dimension order.
assistant_dimension_order <- ordered_standard_dimensions[
  ordered_standard_dimensions %in% unique(as.character(assistant_final_predictions$dimension))
]

assistant_dimension_colors <- custom_colors[assistant_dimension_order]
assistant_dimension_colors[is.na(assistant_dimension_colors)] <- "#666666"

assistant_final_predictions <- assistant_final_predictions |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    dimension = factor(as.character(dimension), levels = assistant_dimension_order),
    ConditionLabel = factor(as.character(ConditionLabel), levels = assistant_condition_order)
  )

assistant_dimension_r_final <- assistant_final_predictions |>
  dplyr::group_by(condition, ConditionLabel, dimension) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    r = suppressWarnings(
      cor(predicted_value, true_value, method = "spearman", use = "complete.obs")
    ),
    n_profiles = dplyr::n_distinct(profile_id),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    dimension = factor(as.character(dimension), levels = assistant_dimension_order),
    ConditionLabel = factor(as.character(ConditionLabel), levels = assistant_condition_order)
  )

assistant_condition_r_summary <- assistant_dimension_r_final |>
  dplyr::group_by(condition, ConditionLabel) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    mean_r = mean(r, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_r = sd(r, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_dimensions = sum(is.finite(r)),
    se_r = sd_r / sqrt(n_dimensions),
    ci95 = 1.96 * se_r,
    ci_low = mean_r - ci95,
    ci_high = mean_r + ci95,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    ConditionLabel = factor(as.character(ConditionLabel), levels = assistant_condition_order)
  )

# LMM on per-dimension R. MAILA is the reference condition.
MODEL.assistant_r_condition <- lmerTest::lmer(
  r ~ condition + (1 | dimension),
  data = assistant_dimension_r_final |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      condition = factor(
        condition,
        levels = c("maila_empirical", "no_aux", "maila_random")
      )
    )
)

STAT.assistant_r_condition <- anova(
  MODEL.assistant_r_condition,
  type = 3,
  ddf = "Satterthwaite"
) |>
  as.data.frame()

STAT.assistant_r_condition$term <- rownames(STAT.assistant_r_condition)

STAT.assistant_r_condition_effect <- STAT.assistant_r_condition |>
  dplyr::filter(term == "condition")

STAT.assistant_r_condition_summary <- summary(MODEL.assistant_r_condition)

STAT.assistant_r_condition_coef <- coef(STAT.assistant_r_condition_summary) |>
  as.data.frame()

STAT.assistant_r_condition_coef$term <- rownames(STAT.assistant_r_condition_coef)

STAT.assistant_r_no_context_vs_maila <- STAT.assistant_r_condition_coef |>
  dplyr::filter(term == "conditionno_aux")

STAT.assistant_r_random_vs_maila <- STAT.assistant_r_condition_coef |>
  dplyr::filter(term == "conditionmaila_random")

# Positive values mean MAILA had higher R than the comparison condition.
assistant_delta_r_no_context <- -STAT.assistant_r_no_context_vs_maila[1, "Estimate"]
assistant_delta_r_random <- -STAT.assistant_r_random_vs_maila[1, "Estimate"]


assistant_dimension_e <- assistant_final_predictions |>
  dplyr::group_by(condition, ConditionLabel, dimension) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    e = sqrt(mean(norm_squared_error, na.rm = TRUE)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    dimension = factor(as.character(dimension), levels = assistant_dimension_order),
    ConditionLabel = factor(as.character(ConditionLabel), levels = assistant_condition_order)
  )

assistant_profile_e <- assistant_final_predictions |>
  dplyr::group_by(condition, ConditionLabel, profile_id) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    e = sqrt(mean(norm_squared_error, na.rm = TRUE)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    ConditionLabel = factor(as.character(ConditionLabel), levels = assistant_condition_order)
  )

assistant_e_summary <- assistant_profile_e |>
  dplyr::group_by(condition, ConditionLabel) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    mean_e = mean(e, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_e = sd(e, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_profiles = dplyr::n_distinct(profile_id),
    se_e = sd_e / sqrt(n_profiles),
    ci95 = 1.96 * se_e,
    ci_low = pmax(mean_e - ci95, 0),
    ci_high = mean_e + ci95,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::rename(e = mean_e) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    ConditionLabel = factor(as.character(ConditionLabel), levels = assistant_condition_order)
  )

assistant_mae_summary <- assistant_final_predictions |>
  dplyr::group_by(condition, ConditionLabel) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    mean_error = mean(abs_error, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_error = sd(abs_error, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_predictions = sum(is.finite(abs_error)),
    se_error = sd_error / sqrt(n_predictions),
    ci95 = 1.96 * se_error,
    ci_low = pmax(mean_error - ci95, 0),
    ci_high = mean_error + ci95,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    ConditionLabel = factor(as.character(ConditionLabel), levels = assistant_condition_order)
  )

# Main LMM on final-turn profile-by-dimension absolute error.
# MAILA is the reference condition. Positive beta means higher error than MAILA.
assistant_final_model_data <- assistant_final_predictions |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    condition = factor(
      condition,
      levels = c("maila_empirical", "no_aux", "maila_random")
    ),
    dimension = factor(dimension)
  )

MODEL.assistant_error_condition <- lmerTest::lmer(
  abs_error ~ condition +
    (1 | profile_id) +
    (1 | profile_id:dimension),
  data = assistant_final_model_data
)

STAT.assistant_error_condition <- anova(
  MODEL.assistant_error_condition,
  type = 3,
  ddf = "Satterthwaite"
) |>
  as.data.frame()

STAT.assistant_error_condition$term <- rownames(STAT.assistant_error_condition)

STAT.assistant_error_condition_effect <- STAT.assistant_error_condition |>
  dplyr::filter(term == "condition")

STAT.assistant_error_condition_summary <- summary(MODEL.assistant_error_condition)

STAT.assistant_error_condition_coef <- coef(STAT.assistant_error_condition_summary) |>
  as.data.frame()

STAT.assistant_error_condition_coef$term <- rownames(STAT.assistant_error_condition_coef)

STAT.assistant_error_no_context_vs_maila <- STAT.assistant_error_condition_coef |>
  dplyr::filter(term == "conditionno_aux")

STAT.assistant_error_random_vs_maila <- STAT.assistant_error_condition_coef |>
  dplyr::filter(term == "conditionmaila_random")

assistant_delta_error_no_context <- STAT.assistant_error_no_context_vs_maila[1, "Estimate"]
assistant_delta_error_random <- STAT.assistant_error_random_vs_maila[1, "Estimate"]

# Inline reporting objects.
assistant_final_maila_r <- assistant_condition_r_summary |>
  dplyr::filter(condition == "maila_empirical")

assistant_final_no_context_r <- assistant_condition_r_summary |>
  dplyr::filter(condition == "no_aux")

assistant_final_random_r <- assistant_condition_r_summary |>
  dplyr::filter(condition == "maila_random")

assistant_final_maila_e <- assistant_e_summary |>
  dplyr::filter(condition == "maila_empirical")

assistant_final_no_context_e <- assistant_e_summary |>
  dplyr::filter(condition == "no_aux")

assistant_final_random_e <- assistant_e_summary |>
  dplyr::filter(condition == "maila_random")

assistant_final_maila_error <- assistant_mae_summary |>
  dplyr::filter(condition == "maila_empirical")

assistant_final_no_context_error <- assistant_mae_summary |>
  dplyr::filter(condition == "no_aux")

assistant_final_random_error <- assistant_mae_summary |>
  dplyr::filter(condition == "maila_random")
```


```{r}
#| label: maila_diagnostic_interview_plot
#| include: false
#| out.width: "100%"

assistant_condition_order <- c("Random MAILA", "No context", "MAILA")

assistant_shape_map <- c(
  "Random MAILA" = 17,
  "No context" = 16,
  "MAILA" = 15
)

assistant_dimension_r_final <- assistant_dimension_r_final |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    ConditionLabel = factor(
      as.character(ConditionLabel),
      levels = assistant_condition_order
    )
  )

assistant_segment_r <- assistant_dimension_r_final |>
  dplyr::filter(ConditionLabel %in% c("No context", "MAILA")) |>
  dplyr::select(dimension, ConditionLabel, r) |>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from = ConditionLabel,
    values_from = r
  ) |>
  dplyr::rename(
    r_no_context = `No context`,
    r_maila = MAILA
  )

p_r_interview <- ggplot(
  assistant_dimension_r_final,
  aes(
    x = dimension,
    y = r,
    color = dimension,
    shape = ConditionLabel
  )
) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.25
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    data = assistant_segment_r,
    aes(
      x = dimension,
      xend = dimension,
      y = r_no_context,
      yend = r_maila,
      color = dimension
    ),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    linewidth = 0.45,
    alpha = 0.85,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_point(
    size = 1,
    stroke = 0.35
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = assistant_dimension_colors, guide = "none") +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = assistant_shape_map,
    breaks = assistant_condition_order,
    name = NULL
  ) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "R",
    title = "E",
    subtitle = "MAILA improves mental-state decoding by GPT-5.2"
  ) +
  guides(
    shape = guide_legend(
      nrow = 3,
      byrow = TRUE,
      override.aes = list(
        color = "black",
        size = 1,
        stroke = 0.35,
        alpha = 1
      )
    )
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(
      angle = 45,
      hjust = 1,
      vjust = 1,
      size = base_font_size * 0.70
    ),
    legend.position = c(0.98, 0.98),
    legend.justification = c(1, 1),
    legend.direction = "vertical",
    legend.background = element_rect(
      fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.75),
      color = NA
    ),
    legend.key = element_rect(fill = NA, color = NA),
    legend.key.height = grid::unit(0.25, "lines"),
    legend.key.width = grid::unit(0.65, "lines"),
    legend.text = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.70),
    legend.margin = margin(t = 1, r = 1, b = 1, l = 1),
    legend.box.margin = margin(t = 0, r = 0, b = 0, l = 0),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor.y = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2)
  )

p_delta_error_interview <- ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data = assistant_dimension_e,
    aes(
      x = ConditionLabel,
      y = e,
      color = dimension
    ),
    position = position_jitter(width = 0.08, height = 0, seed = 17),
    size = dot_size,
    alpha = 0.45,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_linerange(
    data = assistant_e_summary,
    aes(
      x = ConditionLabel,
      ymin = ci_low,
      ymax = ci_high
    ),
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.45
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = assistant_e_summary,
    aes(
      x = ConditionLabel,
      y = e
    ),
    shape = 21,
    size = 1,
    alpha = 0.5,
    stroke = 0.6,
    fill = "black",
    color = "black"
  ) +
  
  scale_color_manual(values = assistant_dimension_colors, guide = "none") +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "e",
    title = "F",
    subtitle = NULL
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(
      angle = 45,
      hjust = 1,
      vjust = 1,
      size = base_font_size * 0.80
    ),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor.y = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2)
  )
```

MAILA’s ability to decode mental states from non-verbal digital behavior may be particularly useful as context for frontier AI chatbots, where misunderstanding a user’s latent mental state in text-based interactions can amplify risk in vulnerable interactions [@weilnhammer_vulnerability-amplifying_2026]. We therefore tested whether MAILA can improve the ability of AI chatbots to infer a user’s mental state from ordinary assistant-facing language. 

We simulated short messages from 4,000 synthetic users (target agents, independent Claude-Sonnet-3.7 instances), each conditioned on the 13-dimensional ground-truth mental-health profile of one participant in the cursor-based MAILA dataset. Each target agent wrote a realistic message to a general AI assistant (GPT-5.2) about everyday topics such as planning, communication, decision-making, routines, social situations, work, family, household tasks, travel, health administration, finances, or situations that felt difficult to interpret. These messages were not framed as mental-health disclosures; instead, the latent profile could shape what the user wanted help with, how the situation was framed, and which concerns surfaced indirectly. GPT-5.2 inferred the target's mental-health profile under three conditions: text-only (message alone), with MAILA as context (message plus MAILA predictions for the same target participant), and with random MAILA context (message plus MAILA predictions sampled from a randomly selected other participant).

Across dimensions, profile recovery was highest when GPT-5.2 received MAILA's cursor-based predictions as context (R = `r round(assistant_final_maila_r[["mean_r"]][1], 3)` ± `r round(assistant_final_maila_r[["ci95"]][1], 3)`), intermediate from text alone (R = `r round(assistant_final_no_context_r[["mean_r"]][1], 3)` ± `r round(assistant_final_no_context_r[["ci95"]][1], 3)`), and lowest with random MAILA context (R = `r round(assistant_final_random_r[["mean_r"]][1], 3)` ± `r round(assistant_final_random_r[["ci95"]][1], 3)`; Figure 5E). Normalized prediction errors were lower with MAILA (e = `r round(assistant_final_maila_e[["e"]][1], 3)` ± `r round(assistant_final_maila_e[["ci95"]][1], 3)`) as compared to text alone (e = `r round(assistant_final_no_context_e[["e"]][1], 3)` ± `r round(assistant_final_no_context_e[["ci95"]][1], 3)`) and random MAILA context (e = `r round(assistant_final_random_e[["e"]][1], 3)` ± `r round(assistant_final_random_e[["ci95"]][1], 3)`; F(`r STAT.assistant_error_condition_effect[["NumDF"]][1]`, `r round(STAT.assistant_error_condition_effect[["DenDF"]][1])`) = `r round(STAT.assistant_error_condition_effect[["F value"]][1], 2)`, p = `r STAT.assistant_error_condition_effect[["Pr(>F)"]][1]`; Figure 5F). MAILA thus improved the ability of a frontier AI chatbot to decode mental health based on realistic single-message interactions with simulated users.

```{r}
#| label: Figure_5
#| include: true
#| out.width: "100%"
#| fig-cap: "**Figure 5. MAILA extends cognitive phenotypes of mental health and improves the ability of frontier AI chatbots to infer user mental health.** **A.** Each point is a participant shown in three interconnected spaces: human–computer interactions (behavior), self-reported mental health (language), and cognition.  **B.** Performance for self-report models, MAILA, and self-report models augmented with MAILA across the first 3 mental-health principal components. Horizontal separation indicates the gain associated with adding MAILA; violins show permutation-based null distributions. **C.** Belief instability showed aligned associations with self-reported and MAILA-predicted mental-health dimensions, with positive associations with hostility and somatization, and negative associations with depression, obsession-compulsion, and interpersonal sensitivity. **D.** Prediction of belief instability from self-report measures, MAILA, and their combination. Violins indicate permutation-based null distributions; points and line ranges show observed correlations (R). **E.** Mental-state decoding by a frontier AI chatbot (GPT-5.2) from single-message assistant-facing interactions with simulated users. Points show dimension-wise R between chatbot-inferred and ground-truth mental-health profiles under three conditions: text only, text plus MAILA context, and text plus random MAILA context; vertical lines connect the text-only and MAILA conditions for each dimension. **F.** Normalized prediction error (e) across the three context conditions; colored points show dimension-level error, black points and line ranges show mean and 95% CI across simulated users."

library(png)
library(grid)
img <- png::readPNG("cursor_mind_files/edit_rev_Figure_5A.png")
g_img <- rasterGrob(img, interpolate = TRUE)

lay1 <- rbind(c(1,2,2,2), c(3,4,5,6))
grid.arrange(g_img, p_add_maila, scatter_corr_plot, p_entropy_conditions, p_r_interview, p_delta_error_interview, layout_matrix = lay1, heights = c(0.8,1), widths = c(0.55,0.28,0.45,0.2))

g <- arrangeGrob(g_img, p_add_maila, scatter_corr_plot, p_entropy_conditions, p_r_interview, p_delta_error_interview, layout_matrix = lay1, heights = c(0.8,1), widths = c(0.55,0.28,0.4,0.2))

ggsave("cursor_mind_files/rev_Figure_5.svg", g, width = 9, height = 5, units = "in", device = "svg")
```

## Explaining MAILA with handcrafted features

```{r}
#| label: compare_handcrafted_to_mila_original
#| include: false
##
## 1. Load Regression Results
##

# Handcrafted feature regression results
handcrafted_cursor_regression_original <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'handcrafted', 'regression', 'original', 'cursor', 'summary_regression.csv'))
handcrafted_touch_draw_regression_original <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'handcrafted', 'regression', 'original', 'touch', 'draw', 'summary_regression.csv'))
handcrafted_touch_quest_regression_original <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'handcrafted', 'regression', 'original', 'touch', 'quest', 'summary_regression.csv'))

handcrafted_regression_original <- mean(c(handcrafted_cursor_regression_original$R,
                                 handcrafted_touch_draw_regression_original$R, handcrafted_touch_quest_regression_original$R))

# MAILA regression results
cursor_regression_pca <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'regression', 'pca_regression_results.csv'))
df_mila_cursor        <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'regression', 'predicted_vs_true_values_pca.csv'))

touch_quest_regression_pca <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'touch_regression', 'general', 'quest', 'pca', 'pca_regression_results.csv'))
touch_draw_regression_pca  <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'touch_regression', 'general', 'draw', 'pca', 'pca_regression_results.csv'))

# Mean regression performance across all MAILA models (PC1-PC3)
mila_regression <- mean(subset(real_joint_df, Source %in% c("baseline", "touch", "touch-d"))$R)


##
## 3. Load Clinical Classification Results
##

handcrafted_mdd_classification <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'clinical', 'classify_general_vs_mdd_summary_metrics.csv'))
handcrafted_ocd_classification <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'clinical', 'classify_general_vs_ocd_summary_metrics.csv'))


##
## 4. Load Explainer Results (feature contribution to prediction error)
##

# Individual prediction error summaries for cursor vs touch
handcrafted_cursor_ind_original <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'handcrafted', 'regression', "original", "cursor", 'cursor_comparison.csv'))
handcrafted_cursor_ind_original$type <- "cursor"

handcrafted_touch_ind_original <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'handcrafted', 'regression', "original", "touch", 'touch_comparison.csv'))
handcrafted_touch_ind_original$type <- "touch"

# Combine and normalize residuals
handcrafted_ind_original <- rbind(handcrafted_cursor_ind_original, handcrafted_touch_ind_original)

norm_resid_df_original <- handcrafted_ind_original %>%
  group_by(Dimension, type) %>%
  mutate(
    range_true = max(True, na.rm = TRUE) - min(True, na.rm = TRUE),
    nrmse_handcrafted = abs_resid_handcrafted / range_true,
    nrmse_mila = abs_resid_mila / range_true
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

STAT.error_mila_handcrafted_original <- t.test(norm_resid_df_original$nrmse_mila, norm_resid_df_original$nrmse_handcrafted, paired = TRUE)
STAT.corr_mila_handcrafted_original  <- cor.test(norm_resid_df_original$Predicted, norm_resid_df_original$Predicted_Label_Handcrafted)
```

```{r}
#| label: visualize_loadings
#| include: true

feature_labels <- c(
  speed = "Speed",
  kurtosis = "Kurtosis",
  jerk = "Jerk",
  area = "Area",
  idle = "Idle time",
  path_efficiency = "Path efficiency",
  avg_turn_angle = "Avg. turn angle",
  tortuosity = "Tortuosity",
  turn_rate_per_distance = "Turn rate / distance",
  horizontal_vertical_bias = "H/V bias",
  speed_variability_entropy = "Speed entropy",
  speed_fluctuation_rate = "Speed fluctuation rate"
)

movement_features <- names(feature_labels)
present_features  <- intersect(movement_features, names(norm_resid_df_original))

regression_results_by_original <- norm_resid_df_original %>%
  dplyr::group_by(Dimension, type) %>%
  dplyr::group_split() %>%
  purrr::map_dfr(function(df_pc) {
    pc <- unique(df_pc$Dimension)
    tp <- unique(df_pc$type)

    feats_here <- intersect(present_features, names(df_pc))
    purrr::map_dfr(feats_here, function(f) {
      x <- as.numeric(df_pc[[f]])
      y <- as.numeric(df_pc$Predicted)

      if (all(is.na(x)) || sd(x, na.rm = TRUE) == 0) {
        return(tibble::tibble(Dimension = pc, type = tp, Feature = f,
                              Beta = NA_real_, SE = NA_real_, t = NA_real_, p = NA_real_))
      }

      df_fit <- tibble::tibble(Predicted = y, x = x) %>% tidyr::drop_na()
      if (nrow(df_fit) < 3) {
        return(tibble::tibble(Dimension = pc, type = tp, Feature = f,
                              Beta = NA_real_, SE = NA_real_, t = NA_real_, p = NA_real_))
      }

      fit  <- lm(Predicted ~ x, data = df_fit)
      smry <- summary(fit)
      tibble::tibble(
        Dimension = pc, type = tp, Feature = f,
        Beta = coef(smry)[2, 1],
        SE   = coef(smry)[2, 2],
        t    = coef(smry)[2, 3],
        p    = min(1, coef(smry)[2, 4] * length(feats_here))
      )
    })
  }) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    CI_low  = Beta - 1.96 * SE,
    CI_high = Beta + 1.96 * SE
  )

# Order features by PC1 betas (cursor)
sort_order <- regression_results_by_original %>%
  filter(Dimension == "Overall Distress", type == "cursor", !is.na(Beta)) %>%
  arrange(Beta) %>%
  pull(Feature)

if (length(sort_order) == 0) sort_order <- present_features

regression_results_by_original$Feature <- factor(regression_results_by_original$Feature, levels = sort_order)

# Safe labeller for pretty axis names
pretty_feature_labeller <- function(x) {
  out <- feature_labels[x]
  out[is.na(out)] <- x[is.na(out)]
  out
}

regression_results_by_original$Dimension <- str_replace_all(regression_results_by_original$Dimension, "\\-", " ")
regression_results_by_original$Dimension = factor(regression_results_by_original$Dimension, levels = ordered_standard_dimensions)

regression_results_by_original$Questionnaire <- ifelse(
  grepl("Wellbeing", regression_results_by_original$Dimension),
  "Wellbeing",
  "Distress"
)

# --- Plot loadings ---
pd <- position_dodge(width = 0.6)

loadings_of_features <- ggplot(
  subset(regression_results_by_original),
  aes(x = Feature, y = Beta, color = Dimension, shape = type)
) +
  # geom_linerange(
  #   aes(x = Feature, xend = Feature, ymin = 0, ymax = Beta, linetype = type),
  #   position = pd,          # same dodge as points
  #   linewidth = 0.5,
  #   alpha = 0.7
  # ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = CI_low, ymax = CI_high),
    width = 0, position = pd, linewidth = 0.3
  ) +
  geom_point(position = pd, size = 1.2, alpha = 0.85) +
geom_line(position = pd) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  facet_wrap(~Dimension, ncol = 5) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = pretty_feature_labeller) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(cursor = 1, touch = 0)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = custom_colors, guide = "none") +
scale_linetype_manual(values = c(cursor = "dashed", touch = "dotted"), guide = "none") +
  labs(
    y = "Standardized Beta (±95% CI)",
    x = NULL,
    shape = NULL,
    title = "A", subtitle = "Loading of hand-crafted fearues on MAILA's predictions across dimensions"
  ) + scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(-0.025, 0.025),
    breaks = c(-0.015, 0, 0.015),
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  ) +
  coord_flip()

df_wide <- regression_results_by_original %>%
  #filter(Dimension %in% c("Overall Distress", "Overall Wellbeing")) %>%
  select(Feature, Dimension, type, Beta) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from  = type,
    values_from = Beta
  )

# Spearman correlation of feature-wise betas
STAT.handcrafted_feature_cor <- cor.test(
  df_wide$cursor,
  df_wide$touch,
  method = "spearman"
)

# Proportion of features with same sign
prop_same_sign <- mean(sign(df_wide$cursor) == sign(df_wide$touch), na.rm = TRUE)
```

```{r}
#| label: compute_handcrafted_MDD_OCD
#| include: false
# Load handcrafted features for general, MDD, and OCD groups
handcrafted_general <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'handcrafted', 'hcf_matrix_touch_general.csv')) %>%
  mutate(population = 'general')
handcrafted_mdd <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'handcrafted', 'hcf_matrix_touch_mdd.csv')) %>%
  mutate(population = 'mdd')
handcrafted_ocd <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'handcrafted', 'hcf_matrix_touch_ocd.csv')) %>%
  mutate(population = 'ocd')

# Combine all groups
df_all <- bind_rows(handcrafted_general, handcrafted_mdd, handcrafted_ocd)

# Identify feature columns
feature_cols <- setdiff(names(df_all), c("Participant_ID", "population"))

# Reshape to long format
df_long <- melt(df_all, id.vars = c("Participant_ID", "population"), 
                variable.name = "Feature", value.name = "Value")

df_long$group <- ifelse(df_long$population == "general", "general", "clinical")

df_long_z <- ddply(df_long, .(Feature), transform, Value = scale(Value))

summary_df <- ddply(df_long_z, .(Feature, population), function(df) {
  m <- mean(df$Value, na.rm = TRUE)
  se <- sd(df$Value, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(length(df$Value))
  data.frame(
    Mean = m,
    SE = se,
    CI_low = m - 1.96 * se,
    CI_high = m + 1.96 * se
  )
})

# Ensure consistent factor ordering + human-readable labels
summary_df$Feature <- factor(summary_df$Feature, levels = sort_order)
regression_results_by_original$Feature <- factor(regression_results_by_original$Feature, levels = sort_order)
feature_labels_ordered <- feature_labels[levels(summary_df$Feature)]
summary_df$FeatureLabel <- factor(summary_df$Feature, labels = feature_labels_ordered)
regression_results_by_original$FeatureLabel <- factor(regression_results_by_original$Feature, labels = feature_labels_ordered)


# Separate PC1-positive and PC1-negative features
pos_Distress <- subset(regression_results_by_original, Dimension == "Overall Distress" & Beta > 0)$Feature
neg_Wellbeing <- subset(regression_results_by_original, Dimension == "Overall Wellbeing" & Beta < 0)$Feature

# Test if clinical group differs from general on PC1-positive and PC1-negative features
STAT.mdd_ocd_pos_Distress <- summary(lm(Value ~ group, data = subset(df_long_z, Feature %in% pos_Distress)))
STAT.mdd_ocd_neg_Wellbeing <- summary(lm(Value ~ group, data = subset(df_long_z, Feature %in% neg_Wellbeing)))
```

```{r}
#| label: visualize_handcrafted_MDD_OCD
# 1) Build a (Feature, Dimension, Sign) map from loadings
loadings_touch <- regression_results_by_original %>%
  dplyr::filter(type == "touch", Dimension %in% c("Overall Distress", "Overall Wellbeing")) %>%
  dplyr::select(Dimension, Feature, Beta)

# Keep only features present in df_long_z
feat_in_data <- unique(df_long_z$Feature)
loadings_touch <- loadings_touch %>%
  dplyr::filter(Feature %in% feat_in_data) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Sign = dplyr::case_when(
      Beta > 0 ~ "Positive",
      Beta < 0 ~ "Negative",
      TRUE     ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) %>%
  tidyr::drop_na(Sign)

long_with_sign <- df_long_z %>%
  dplyr::inner_join(loadings_touch, by = "Feature") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Sign = factor(Sign, levels = c("Negative", "Positive")),
    Dimension = factor(Dimension, levels = c("Overall Distress","Overall Wellbeing")),
    population = recode(population,
                        "general" = "General",
                        "mdd"     = "Depression",
                        "ocd"     = "OCD"),
    population = factor(population, levels = c("General","Depression","OCD"))
  )

# 3) Participant-level aggregation: average across features within each (PC, Sign)
by_participant <- long_with_sign %>%
  dplyr::group_by(population, Participant_ID, Dimension, Sign) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(Score = mean(Value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# 4) Group-level summary (mean ± 95% CI across participants)
pc_posneg_summary <- by_participant %>%
  dplyr::group_by(population, Dimension, Sign) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(
    Mean   = mean(Score, na.rm = TRUE),
    SE     = sd(Score, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(dplyr::n()),
    CI_low = Mean - 1.96 * SE,
    CI_high= Mean + 1.96 * SE,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# 5) Plot: x-axis = population; color/shape = Sign; facet by PC (Dimension)
pd <- position_dodge(width = 0.5)

features_within_groups <- ggplot(
  pc_posneg_summary,
  aes(
    x = population,
    y = Mean,
    ymin = CI_low,
    ymax = CI_high,
    color = Dimension,
    shape = Sign
  )
) +
  geom_linerange(
    position = pd,
    width = 0.0,
    linewidth = 0.5,   # match linerange feel (was 0.3)
    alpha = 0.85
  ) +
  geom_point(
    position = pd,
    size = 1,
    alpha = 0.95,
    stroke = 0.6,      # visible outline like p_pca_values
    aes(fill = "white")# force white fill for 24/25
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.25
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ Dimension, nrow = 3) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "Average features with +/- loadings (z)",
    shape = "Feature loading",
    color = NULL,
    fill = NULL,
    title = "B",
    subtitle = "More distress- and less wellbeing-related \nfeatures in self-identifying groups"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = custom_colors, guide = "none") +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = c(Negative = 25, Positive = 24), # 25=down, 24=up (both fillable)
    guide = guide_legend(nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("white" = "white"), guide = "none") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),

    # If you want the same "legend inside plot" look:
    legend.position = c(0.02, 0.98),
    legend.justification = c(0, 1),
    legend.direction = "horizontal",
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.75), color = NA),
    legend.key = element_rect(fill = NA, color = NA),
    legend.margin = margin(2, 2, 2, 2),
    legend.text = element_text(margin = margin(r = -5)),
    legend.key.width  = unit(0.5, "lines"),
    legend.key.height = unit(0.3, "lines")
  )
```


```{r}
#| label: compare_handcrafted_to_mila_pca
#| include: false
##
## 1. Load Regression Results
##

# Handcrafted feature regression results
handcrafted_cursor_regression_pca <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'handcrafted', 'regression', 'cursor_test_pca_regression_results.csv'))
handcrafted_touch_regression_pca  <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'handcrafted', 'regression', 'touch_pca_regression_results.csv'))
handcrafted_regression_pca <- mean(c(handcrafted_cursor_regression_pca$Real_Rho,
                                 handcrafted_touch_regression_pca$Real_Rho))



mila_regression_pca = mean(subset(pca_real_joint_df, Source %in% c("baseline", "touch", "touch-d") & Dimension %in% c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3"))$R)
##
## 2. Load Change Vector Results (for change prediction analysis)
##

handcrafted_regression_change_pca <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'handcrafted', 'changes', 'pca','change_vector_results.csv'))
mila_regression_change_pca        <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'changes', 'pca','change_vector_results.csv'))

##
## 4. Load Explainer Results (feature contribution to prediction error)
##

# Individual prediction error summaries for cursor vs touch
handcrafted_cursor_ind_pca <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'handcrafted', 'regression', 'cursor_comparison.csv'))
handcrafted_cursor_ind_pca$type <- "cursor"

handcrafted_touch_ind_pca <- read.csv(file.path(root, 'handcrafted', 'regression', 'touch_comparison.csv'))
handcrafted_touch_ind_pca$type <- "touch"

# Combine and normalize residuals
handcrafted_ind_pca <- rbind(handcrafted_cursor_ind_pca, handcrafted_touch_ind_pca)

norm_resid_df_pca <- handcrafted_ind_pca %>%
  group_by(Dimension, type) %>%
  mutate(
    range_true = max(True, na.rm = TRUE) - min(True, na.rm = TRUE),
    nrmse_handcrafted = abs_resid_handcrafted / range_true,
    nrmse_mila = abs_resid_mila / range_true
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

STAT.error_mila_handcrafted_pca <- t.test(subset(norm_resid_df_pca, Dimension %in% c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3"))$nrmse_mila, subset(norm_resid_df_pca, Dimension %in% c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3"))$nrmse_handcrafted, paired = TRUE)
STAT.corr_mila_handcrafted_pca  <- cor.test(subset(norm_resid_df_pca, Dimension %in% c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3"))$nrmse_mila, subset(norm_resid_df_pca, Dimension %in% c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3"))$nrmse_handcrafted)

df_diff_original <- data.frame(
  Space = "Original",
  Diff  = norm_resid_df_original$nrmse_mila - norm_resid_df_original$nrmse_handcrafted
)

df_diff_pca <- subset(norm_resid_df_pca, Dimension %in% c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3"))
df_diff_pca <- data.frame(
  Space = "PC1-3",
  Diff  = df_diff_pca$nrmse_mila - df_diff_pca$nrmse_handcrafted
)

error_diff_long <- rbind(df_diff_original, df_diff_pca)


error_diff_summary <- ddply(
  error_diff_long,
  .(Space),
  summarise,
  average = mean_ci_95(Diff)$mean,
  error   = mean_ci_95(Diff)$error,
  n       = sum(is.finite(Diff))
)


COND_ORDER <- c("Original", "PC1-3")  # x-axis order
p_error_space <- ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data = error_diff_summary,
    aes(x = Space, y = average),
    shape = 21,          # filled circle with outline
    size = 1,          # slight bump to visually match triangles
    stroke = 0.6,
    fill = "black",
    color = "black",
    alpha = 1
  ) +
  geom_linerange(
    data = error_diff_summary,
    aes(x = Space, ymin = average - error, ymax = average + error),
    linewidth = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.25
  ) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = expression(Delta~e:~MAILA~-~handcrafted),
    title = "C",
    subtitle = "MAILA outperforms hand-crafted features"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, vjust = 1),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
    panel.spacing.y  = unit(1, "lines")
  )

```

```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(purrr)
library(stringr)
library(ggplot2)


df_for_plot <- norm_resid_df_pca %>%
  dplyr::filter(Dimension %in% c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3"))

regression_results_by_pca <- df_for_plot %>%
  dplyr::group_by(Dimension, type) %>%
  dplyr::group_split() %>%
  purrr::map_dfr(function(df_pc) {

    pc <- unique(df_pc$Dimension)
    tp <- unique(df_pc$type)

    feats_here <- intersect(present_features, names(df_pc))

    purrr::map_dfr(feats_here, function(f) {
      x <- as.numeric(df_pc[[f]])
      y <- as.numeric(df_pc$Predicted)

      if (all(is.na(x)) || sd(x, na.rm = TRUE) == 0) {
        return(tibble::tibble(Dimension = pc, type = tp, Feature = f,
                              Beta = NA_real_, SE = NA_real_, t = NA_real_, p = NA_real_))
      }

      df_fit <- tibble::tibble(Predicted = y, x = x) %>% tidyr::drop_na()
      if (nrow(df_fit) < 3) {
        return(tibble::tibble(Dimension = pc, type = tp, Feature = f,
                              Beta = NA_real_, SE = NA_real_, t = NA_real_, p = NA_real_))
      }

      fit  <- lm(Predicted ~ x, data = df_fit)
      smry <- summary(fit)

      tibble::tibble(
        Dimension = pc, type = tp, Feature = f,
        Beta = coef(smry)[2, 1],
        SE   = coef(smry)[2, 2],
        t    = coef(smry)[2, 3],
        p    = min(1, coef(smry)[2, 4] * length(feats_here)) 
      )
    })
  }) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    CI_low  = Beta - 1.96 * SE,
    CI_high = Beta + 1.96 * SE
  )


sort_order <- regression_results_by_pca %>%
  dplyr::filter(Dimension == "PC1", type == "cursor", !is.na(Beta)) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(Beta) %>%
  dplyr::pull(Feature)

if (length(sort_order) == 0) sort_order <- present_features
regression_results_by_pca$Feature <- factor(regression_results_by_pca$Feature, levels = sort_order)

# Pretty axis labels
pretty_feature_labeller <- function(x) {
  out <- feature_labels[x]
  out[is.na(out)] <- x[is.na(out)]
  out
}

# Order PCs
regression_results_by_pca$Dimension <- factor(regression_results_by_pca$Dimension, levels = c("PC1","PC2","PC3"))


pd <- position_dodge(width = 0.6)

loadings_of_features_pca <- ggplot(
  subset(regression_results_by_pca),
  aes(x = Feature, y = Beta, color = Dimension, shape = type)
) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = CI_low, ymax = CI_high),
    width = 0, position = pd, linewidth = 0.3
  ) +
  geom_point(position = pd, size = 1.2, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_line(position = pd) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  facet_wrap(~Dimension, ncol = 3, scale = 'free') +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = pretty_feature_labeller) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(cursor = 1, touch = 0)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = pc_palette, guide = "none") +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c(cursor = "dashed", touch = "dotted"), guide = "none") +
  labs(
    y = "Standardized Beta (±95% CI)",
    x = NULL,
    shape = NULL,
    title = "A", subtitle = "Loading of hand-crafted fearues on MAILA's predictions across dimensions"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  ) +
  coord_flip()
```


To probe which movement features underlie MAILA’s predictions, we regressed its predicted scores onto a battery of handcrafted cursor and touchscreen features (Table S4). In participants from the general population, higher predicted wellbeing was characterized by higher path efficiency, whereas higher predicted distress was associated with more tortuous trajectories and greater variability in speed (Figure S20A). Relative to the general population, participants who self-identified with mental illness showed more of the reverse-engineered features associated with distress (p = `r STAT.mdd_ocd_pos_Distress$coefficients[2,4]`) and less of the features linked to wellbeing (p = `r STAT.mdd_ocd_neg_Wellbeing$coefficients[2,4]`; Figure S20B). MAILA thus provides a principled way to test whether candidate handcrafted features capture meaningful inter-individual differences in mental health. 

Between cursor- and touch-based interactions, we observed substantial variability in how handcrafted features related to mental states, including reversals in the direction of association for `r (1 - prop_same_sign) * 100`% of all features (Figure S20C). MAILA outperformed these intuitive behavioral descriptors, which struggled to distill modality-general signatures of mental states, across all benchmarks, including lower prediction errors for inter-individual mental health differences in the general population (p = `r STAT.error_mila_handcrafted_original$p.value`) as well as higher accuracy in classifying individuals who self-identified as living with depression (AUC = `r subset(handcrafted_mdd_classification, context == 'combined')$auc` vs. `r subset(handcrafted_mdd_classification, context == 'handcrafted')$auc`) and obsessive–compulsive disorder (AUC = `r subset(handcrafted_ocd_classification, context == 'combined')$auc` vs. `r subset(handcrafted_ocd_classification, context == 'handcrafted')$auc`). 

MAILA's accuracy decreased when we reduced the number of group-level clusters, excluded participants from the training set, limited the amount of available test data, or distorted human–computer interactions with increasing levels of random noise (Figure S21). These patterns show that behavioral diversity, realistic sample sizes, and longer recordings enhance MAILA's performance, while modest user-side scrambling can substantially reduce unwanted digital profiling.

# Discussion

Closing the gap between mental-health need and access requires measurements that are scalable, accessible, affordable, and accurate enough to guide decision-making [@jain_digital_2015; @insel_digital_2017; @topol_high-performance_2019]. Gold-standard tools such as clinical interviews and questionnaires provide high-quality information, but rely on limited availability, shared language, and cultural context. Established biomarkers, including polygenic risk scores [@lewis_polygenic_2020; @murray_could_2021; @sanchez-roige_emerging_2020], neuroimaging [@schmaal_subcortical_2016; @kambeitz_detecting_2017; @marek_reproducible_2022], wearable sensors [@lyall_association_2018; @abd-alrazaq_systematic_2023; @liu_digital_2025; @xie_jets_2025; @kim_codas_2026], sleep monitoring [@thapa_multimodal_2026], cognitive tasks [@open_science_collaboration_estimating_2015], and digital behaviors [@eichstaedt_facebook_2018; @kelley_using_2022; @mirea_cognitive_2025] often encode persistent traits rather than dynamic mental states, depend on active participation, time-consuming protocols, or expensive equipment, generate data that are difficult to store and anonymize, or capture behavior in contexts far removed from everyday life. Current methods therefore occupy a narrow Pareto frontier on which no single tool achieves sufficient accuracy, cost-efficiency, and ecological validity at once [@opel_transforming_2026].

MAILA presents a fundamental advance across multiple roadblocks that have long constrained the assessment of mental health: while decoding psychological distress and wellbeing at a level of accuracy comparable to established psychiatric biomarkers, MAILA scales to billions of devices at zero marginal cost. Its predictions are continuous and dynamic, generalize across populations, contexts, and time, and improve when multiple observations are combined. Once integrated into existing pathways, MAILA may help to increase the temporal resolution of monitoring and enable earlier detection of clinically relevant states [@jain_digital_2015; @insel_digital_2017]. Aggregated across users, it yields high-fidelity population signals that can inform early-warning systems, resource allocation, and preventive public-health programs.

Unlike many areas of medicine where diagnostic categories are anchored to well-defined causal markers, mental-health classifications remain largely symptom-based. Existing diagnostic categories are therefore heavily shaped by language, history, and culture, grouping together individuals with diverse symptoms and treatment responses [@insel_research_2010; @kotov_paradigm_2018; @sanchez-roige_emerging_2020]. As a data-driven behavioral assessment, MAILA may be sensitive to mental states that traditional language-based assessments obscure [@bennett_two_2019; @torous_growing_2021; @mirea_cognitive_2025]. This can broaden access to care for individuals who struggle to recognize, articulate, or report their experiences, including non-verbal individuals and those facing language barriers [@clement_what_2015; @miteva_impact_2022]. 

MAILA may also support more personalized and context-aware conversational AI by giving language models access to user-state information that is not fully expressed in language. Because human–computer interactions are less susceptible than self-report to deliberate distortion, impression management, and social desirability, discrepancies between MAILA and language-based tools can serve as a consistency check wherever self-report reliability is critical. Data-driven predictions from human–computer interactions also provide convergent validation for computational phenotyping [@bennett_two_2019; @kelley_using_2022; @mirea_cognitive_2025]. Because of its scalability, MAILA can help to move the science of mental health into the data-rich regime in which deep learning most effectively advances large-scale predictive models of human cognition, emotion, and behavior [@lecun_deep_2015].

# Limitations

The MAILA dataset is large and demographically varied, but all participants were recruited online, limiting claims about non-English-speaking, low-resource, digitally underserved, neurodivergent, and clinical populations. MAILA was calibrated to self-reported psychological distress and wellbeing, not to structured diagnoses, clinician-rated outcomes, treatment response, or demonstrated clinical utility in care pathways. At the same time, MAILA’s predictive signal generalized across devices, tasks, time, demographic groups, and open-ended digital activities, providing a robust proof of principle that human–computer interactions carry transferable mental-health information. A central next step is prospective validation in clinical cohorts, ideally alongside ongoing treatment and in randomized trials testing whether MAILA improves decision-making and outcomes in mental health.

MAILA’s central strength is also its central risk: it provides sensitive information about an individual’s mental state from signals that can be obtained on any digital device [@char_implementing_2018; @huckvale_toward_2019; @opel_transforming_2026]. Passive mental-health screening, even when well-intentioned, can produce unintended consequences. For example, individuals flagged by automated tools may experience anxiety, stigma, or confusion if results are presented outside of an ecosystem that bridges the last mile toward mental-health support [@jain_digital_2015; @insel_digital_2017; @lebowitz_testing_2017]. There is also a danger that predictive mental-health technologies will be implemented in contexts that prioritize institutional or economic interests over individual wellbeing [@opel_transforming_2026]. Without strong safeguards, MAILA could be misused in hiring decisions, insurance risk assessments, or unwanted profiling in sectors such as education, immigration, or law enforcement [@char_implementing_2018]. Our own results show that human–computer interactions can in principle be used to detect inconsistent self-reports and to partially re-identify users across sessions (Results S1). When used in any of these contexts, mental-health predictions may exacerbate discrimination and reinforce existing inequalities. 

Preventing such harms requires transparent disclosure, opt-in participation, user control, privacy-preserving design, anti-misuse safeguards, continuous fairness validation, and strong normative and regulatory frameworks that limit use to beneficial contexts. To guide responsible development and deployment, predictive models should be evaluated against established standards for trustworthy AI. Table S5 summarizes MAILA’s alignment with the FUTURE-AI framework, a system for assessing the fairness, universality, traceability, usability, robustness, and explainability of AI in healthcare [@lekadir_future-ai_2025]. By adopting these recommendations, we aim to advance MAILA in a way that is transparent, inclusive, and ultimately beneficial to those in need of mental health support.

We thank Shi Chen, Maria Crespo-Ribadeneyra, Raymond Dolan, Sophocles Goulis, Janis Karan
Hesse, Jochen Michely, Matthew Nour, Philipp Sterzer, Dominik Thalmeier, and Pierre
Vassiliadis for their valuable feedback and support. V.W. was supported by the Leopoldina,
German National Academy of Sciences, and the Brain and Behavior Research Foundation (BBRF;
Young Investigator Award).

# Author Contributions

V.W. conceived the study and designed the experiments. V.W. developed the computational
framework and performed the analyses. D.W. provided conceptual guidance and supervision.
All authors contributed to interpretation of the results and to writing the manuscript.

# Competing Interests Statement

This work is related to a pending patent application filed by the University of California,
Berkeley. The authors declare no other competing interests.

# Data Availability Statement

The group-level data supporting the main figures of this study are publicly available in
the MAILA repository (`https://github.com/veithweilnhammer/maila_sdk`), in the `data/`
subfolder. The release contains de-identified, aggregated results for the
main results, a manifest describing each file, and a
reproduction tool (`reproduce.py`) that recomputes the reported results directly
from the data.

# Code Availability Statement

Code supporting this study is publicly available in the MAILA repository
(`https://github.com/veithweilnhammer/maila_sdk`). This includes the MAILA Sandbox — a
browser-side software development kit (`maila-recorder.js`), an end-to-end demo interface
(`example.html`), and example recordings — for recording human–computer interactions and
requesting model predictions from the MAILA backend, as well as the data-reproduction
utilities (`reproduce.py`) used to regenerate the main-figure results from the released
data. The manuscript was created using the R Quarto framework, which integrates the data,
analysis code, and text within a single document.
