```{r}
    fraction = value / total,
    cumfrac  = cumsum(fraction),
    offset   = lag(cumfrac, default = 0),
    ymin     = mila_f_span$ymin + offset * (mila_f_span$ymax - mila_f_span$ymin),
    ymax     = ymin + fraction * (mila_f_span$ymax - mila_f_span$ymin),
    middle   = (ymin + ymax) / 2,
    r0       = 0.2,
    r1       = 0.45
  )


plot_data <- bind_rows(
  dplyr::select(outer_angles,  label, ymin, ymax, middle, r0, r1),
  dplyr::select(middle_angles, label, ymin, ymax, middle, r0, r1),
  dplyr::select(g1_g2,         label, ymin, ymax, middle, r0, r1)
) %>%
  mutate(
    label = factor(
      label,
      levels = c(
        "MAILA-C-General-Interface",
        "MAILA-T-General-Interface",
        "MAILA-T-Depression-Interface",
        "MAILA-T-OCD-Interface",
        "MAILA-F",
        "MAILA-T-General-Drawing",
        "MAILA-T-Depression-Drawing",
        "MAILA-T-OCD-Drawing",
        "MAILA-G1",
        "MAILA-G2"
      )
    )
  )


custom_colors_purple <- c(
  "MAILA-C-General-Interface"    = "#3f007d",  # dark purple
  "MAILA-T-General-Interface"    = "#756bb1",  # medium purple
  "MAILA-T-Depression-Interface" = "#9e9ac8",  # lighter purple
  "MAILA-T-OCD-Interface"        = "#dadaeb",  # very light
  "MAILA-F"                      = "#bcbddc",  # inner ring (cursor F)
  "MAILA-T-General-Drawing"      = "#cbc9e2",
  "MAILA-T-Depression-Drawing"   = "#efedf5",
  "MAILA-T-OCD-Drawing"          = "#e0dcee",
  "MAILA-G1"                     = "#a6cee3",
  "MAILA-G2"                     = "#b2df8a"
)

label_descriptions <- c(
  "MAILA-C-General-Interface"    = "MAILA-C General (cursor interface, N = 4,000)",
  "MAILA-T-General-Interface"    = "MAILA-T General (touch interface, N = 3,500)",
  "MAILA-T-Depression-Interface" = "MAILA-T Depression (touch interface, N = 1,000)",
  "MAILA-T-OCD-Interface"        = "MAILA-T OCD (touch interface, N = 500)",
  "MAILA-F"                      = "MAILA-F (follow-up cohort within MAILA-C, N = 2,000)",
  "MAILA-T-General-Drawing"      = "MAILA-T General (drawing task, N = 3,500)",
  "MAILA-T-Depression-Drawing"   = "MAILA-T Depression (drawing task, N = 1,000)",
  "MAILA-T-OCD-Drawing"          = "MAILA-T OCD (drawing task, N = 500)",
  "MAILA-G1"                     = "MAILA-G1 (subsample 1 within MAILA-F, N = 600)",
  "MAILA-G2"                     = "MAILA-G2 (subsample 2 within MAILA-F, N = 600)"
)

p_mila_subsets <- ggplot(plot_data) +
  geom_arc_bar(
    aes(
      x0    = 0, y0 = 0,
      r0    = r0, r  = r1,
      start = 2 * pi * ymin,
      end   = 2 * pi * ymax,
      fill  = label
    ),
    color = "white",
    size  = 0.3
  ) +
  coord_fixed() +
  theme_void(base_size = base_font_size) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = custom_colors_purple,
    breaks = names(label_descriptions),
    labels = label_descriptions,
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "left",
    legend.text     = element_text(size = base_font_size)
  ) +
  labs(
    subtitle = NULL,
    caption  = "Nested segments indicate hierarchical subsets of the MAILA dataset."
  )

#p_mila_subsets
```

```{r}
#| label:  SF1_MAILA_dataset_interface
#| include: true
#| echo: false
#| out.width: "100%"

knitr::include_graphics("cursor_mind_files/Supplemental_Figure_S1.png")
```

**Figure S1. The MAILA experiments.** 

**A. The MAILA dataset.** We evaluated MAILA across structured and open-ended digital behavior, repeated assessments, and participants who self-identified as living with depression or obsessive–compulsive disorder.  This figure summarizes the structure of the dataset. Sections with filled markers indicate subsets used for model training and testing (5-fold cross-validation over participants), whereas hollow markers denote subsets on which we assessed the generalizability of trained models.

**B. Mental-health questionnaire.** Participants reported on their current mental health using a randomized response interface that dissociated cursor trajectories from the semantic content of their answers. Each trial began with a question screen; participants advanced by clicking a circle that appeared in one of the four screen corners at random. On the subsequent response screen, the same item reappeared and participants indicated their answer by clicking a randomly positioned and randomly oriented response line. Cursor trajectories were logged continuously from start to finish. Please note that items were presented in an order randomized per participant. 

**C. Non-mental-health survey.** Participants answered general survey questions unrelated to mental health using the same interface and randomized-response mapping as in **A**, isolating the effect of semantic content while holding the motor context constant.

**D. Gamified decision-making task (beads task).** Participants played an interactive decision-making game using the same randomized-response mapping as in **A**, isolating the effect of semantic content beyond surveys while holding the motor context constant.

**E. Touchscreen drawing.** Participants produced prompted, freehand drawings on their touchscreen device. Each prompt disappeared at first touch, and participants advanced by pressing a “Continue” button at the bottom center of the screen.

**F. Touchscreen interface.**  After the drawing task, participants completed a touchscreen version of the randomized-response interface from **A**, adapted for vertical screens. Instead of clicking, participants dragged a response dot, initially placed at random in one of the four corners of the screen, onto a randomly positioned response line.

\newpage
## Figure S2


```{r}
#| label:  SF2_Workflow
#| include: true
#| echo: false
#| out.width: "100%"

knitr::include_graphics("cursor_mind_files/Supplemental_Figure_S2.png")
```

**Figure S2. MAILA transforms cursor/touchscreen activity into participant-level features.** This schematic summarizes the representation-learning pipeline that converts raw human–computer interactions into a participant-by-motif feature matrix $\mathrm{X^{N \times C}}$.

**A. Human–computer interactions.** Continuous cursor or touchscreen trajectories are recorded and transformed into screen-normalized coordinates.

**B. Segmentation**. Each recording (on average `r mean_ci_95(across_datapoints)$mean` ± `r mean_ci_95(across_datapoints)$error` samples) is divided into partially overlapping windows of `r length(unique(cluster_data$Time_Step))` consecutive samples, yielding short trajectory segments that capture local movement dynamics.

**C. LSTM autoencoder.** A long short-term memory autoencoder, pretrained on naturalistic human–computer interactions [@kilic_bogazici_2021], maps each segment to a 50-dimensional movement embedding (encoder bottleneck) and reconstructs the input trajectory (decoder), producing a compact representation of segment-level movement structure.

**D. Pooling across participants.** Segment embeddings from all participants are pooled into a shared embedding space to define a common vocabulary of recurring interaction patterns.

**E. K-means clustering into motifs.** Pooled embeddings are grouped into C = `r length(unique(cluster_data$Cluster))` clusters, each representing a distinct, recurring movement motif (a “motif library”) that captures stereotyped patterns of human–computer interaction.

**F. Participant fingerprint**. For each participant, MAILA computes a fingerprint by estimating the proportion of segments assigned to each motif (distribution over C patterns). 

**G. Space of human–computer interaction.** This yields the feature matrix $\mathrm{X^{N \times C}}$, where each row places a participant at a location in the space of digital behavior and can be used for downstream prediction of mental-health outcomes.

\newpage
## Figure S3

```{r}
#| label: SF3_movement_matrix
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| cache: true
#| out.width: "100%"
##
## Movement
##

# **Global Styling Variables**
legend_position <- "none"
tile_aspect_ratio <- 1
viridis_palette_option <- "D"
alpha_range <- c(0, 1)
dot_size <- 4
line_alpha <- 0.2
line_width_range <- c(0.2, 2)

# Rename columns from X1, X2, ... to Feature 1, Feature 2, ...
colnames(movement_matrix) <- paste0("Feature ", seq_along(colnames(movement_matrix)))

# Convert to long format
movement_long <- movement_matrix %>%
  dplyr::mutate(ID = row_number()) %>%  # Add ID for reshaping
  pivot_longer(cols = -ID, names_to = "Feature", values_to = "Value")

# Extract numeric feature number
movement_long$Feature <- as.numeric(gsub("Feature ", "", movement_long$Feature))

movement_long$FacetGroup <- cut(movement_long$Feature, breaks = seq(0, 500, by = 50), include.lowest = TRUE, labels = paste(seq(1, 451, by = 50), "-", seq(100, 501, by = 50)))
p_movement_dist <- ggplot(movement_long, aes(x = Value, y = as.factor(Feature), fill = Feature)) + 
  geom_density_ridges(color = "white", linewidth = 0.1, rel_min_height = 0.01, bandwidth = 0.001, scale = 2.5, alpha = 0.85) +
  facet_wrap(~FacetGroup, scales = "free_y", ncol = 10) +  # adjust ncol as needed
  theme_ridges(font_size = 10, grid = FALSE, center_axis_labels = TRUE) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) + 
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 0.005), breaks = c(0.0025)) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "black", high = "grey60") +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    strip.background = element_blank()
  ) +
 labs(
  x = "Proportion of segments assigned to cluster",
  y = "Movement feature (cluster index)",
  title = "C",
  subtitle = "Fraction of cursor movement segments spent in each cluster (distribution across participants)"
)


# **Load correlation matrix & t-SNE coordinates**
cor_matrix <- read.csv(file.path(root, "movement_matrix", "correlation_matrix.csv"), row.names = 1, check.names = FALSE)
tsne_coords <- read.csv(file.path(root, "movement_matrix", "t-SNE_coordinates.csv"), row.names = 1, check.names = FALSE)

# **Standardize Dimension Names**
tsne_coords <- tsne_coords %>%
  mutate(Feature = rownames(.))

# **Convert Correlation Matrix to Long Format**
cor_long <- cor_matrix %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  mutate(Feature1 = rownames(.)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-Feature1, names_to = "Feature2", values_to = "Correlation") %>%
  filter(Feature1 != Feature2)

# **Filter for Strong Correlations**
cor_threshold <- 0
cor_long_filtered <- cor_long %>% filter(Correlation > cor_threshold)

# **Merge t-SNE Coordinates**
cor_long_filtered <- cor_long_filtered %>%
  left_join(tsne_coords %>% dplyr::rename(X1 = X, Y1 = Y) %>% dplyr::mutate(Feature1 = rownames(.)), by = "Feature1") %>%
  left_join(tsne_coords %>% dplyr::rename(X2 = X, Y2 = Y) %>% dplyr::mutate(Feature2 = rownames(.)), by = "Feature2")


random_clusters <- c(176, 437, 320, 370,  32)
#random_clusters <- sample(1:500, 5)
subset_data <- cluster_data[as.numeric(cluster_data$Cluster) %in% random_clusters,]

p_movement <- ggplot(subset_data, aes(x = X, y = Y, group = interaction(Cluster, Movement_ID), color = Time_Step)) +
  geom_path(alpha = 0.8, size = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ Cluster, scales = "free", ncol = 1) + 
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "D", direction = 1) + 
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5)
  ) +
  labs(color = "Time Step", x = "x", y = "y", title = "A", subtitle = "Example cursor trajectories \nper cluster")

# **t-SNE Plot**
p_movement_tsne <- ggplot() +
  #geom_segment(data = cor_long_filtered, aes(x = X1, y = Y1, xend = X2, yend = Y2, linewidth = abs(Correlation)/1000), 
  #             color = "gray60", alpha = line_alpha/10) +
  geom_point(data = tsne_coords, aes(x = X, y = Y, fill = as.numeric(Feature)), 
             size = dot_size/7, shape = 21, color = "white", stroke = 0.5) +
  geom_text_repel(data = tsne_coords, aes(x = X, y = Y, label = Feature), 
                  size = rel(2), box.padding = 0.3, point.padding = 0.3, 
                  force = 5, segment.color = "gray50", segment.size = 0.3) +
  scale_alpha_continuous(range = alpha_range, guide = "none") +  
  scale_linewidth_continuous(range = line_width_range, guide = "none") +  
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "black", high = "grey60") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
#  scale_y_continuous(position = "right") + 
  theme(legend.position = legend_position, panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2),) +
  labs(x = "t-SNE dimension 1", y = "t-SNE dimension 2", title = "B", subtitle = "t-SNE embedding of clusters in the space of human–computer interactions (cursor movement)")

# **Arrange & Display First Row of Plots**
lay1 <- rbind(c(1,3), c(1,2))
grid.arrange(p_movement, p_movement_dist, p_movement_tsne,  layout_matrix = lay1, heights = c(1,1), widths = c(0.25,1))
```

**Figure S3. The space of human–computer interaction.**

**A. Clustered cursor movements.** MAILA transforms segments of cursor and touchscreen movement into low-dimensional embeddings. By clustering the embeddings across participants, MAILA discovers recurring patterns of human–computer interaction at the group-level. Here, we show five exemplary clusters derived from cursor movement (time progresses from darker to lighter colors, number indicates cluster ID). Figure S5 shows examples from all 500 cursor clusters. While qualitatively similar patterns emerge for touchscreen data (not shown here), the specific clusters differ depending on the behavioral context of the interaction.

**B. Structure of cursor movement features.** Each dot represents a cursor movement cluster, positioned in t-SNE space based on its similarity to other clusters in the space of human–computer interactions $\mathrm{X^{N \times C}}$.

**C. The cursor movement feature matrix.** MAILA computes the per-participant (N) fraction of segments assigned each of the C = 500 clusters, resulting in a $\mathrm{X^{N \times C}}$ movement feature matrix. Each row encodes a participant’s location in a space of cursor movement patterns, derived from raw trajectories that are segmented, autoencoded, and assigned to discrete clusters defined at the group-level. Plots show the distribution of features across participants for the 500 cursor movement clusters. The shape of the cluster distributions are qualitatively identical for touchscreen interactions (not shown here).

\newpage
## Figure S4

```{r}
#| label: SF4_reconstruction_done
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| fig.align: "center"
#| fig.width: 7
#| fig.height: 6
#| cache: true

movement_data <- read.csv(file.path(root, "movement_matrix", "movement_reconstruction_data_example.csv"))
reconstruction_loss <- read.csv(file.path(root, "movement_matrix", "reconstruction_loss.csv"))

# Convert Sequence to numeric and order it
movement_data$Sequence <- as.numeric(movement_data$Sequence) + 1
movement_data$Sequence <- factor(movement_data$Sequence, levels = sort(unique(movement_data$Sequence)))

# Normalize Point_Index within each sequence (ensuring continuous coloring across chunks)
movement_data <- movement_data %>%
  group_by(Sequence) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(Point_Index = row_number()) %>%
  ungroup()

# Plot original vs reconstructed movement per sequence with Viridis color scale for points
p_movement <- ggplot(movement_data, aes(x = X_Original, y = Y_Original, group = interaction(Sequence, Chunk_Index))) +
  
  # Original movement points colored by continuous Point_Index (across sequence)
  geom_point(aes(color = Point_Index), size = 1, alpha = 0.5, color = my_blue) +
  
  # Reconstructed movement in solid red
  geom_path(aes(x = X_Reconstructed, y = Y_Reconstructed), color = my_red, linetype = "solid", size = 0.75, alpha = 0.5) +
  
  facet_wrap(~ Sequence, scales = "free") +
  labs(x = "x (cursor position)", y = "y (cursor position)", color = "Samples", subtitle = "LSTM encoding of human–computer interactions") +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "D", direction = 1) +  # Use Viridis (dark to light)
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) + xlim(0,1) + ylim(0,1) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")  # Keep legend to show progression

# Print plot
p_movement
```

**Figure S4. Autoencoded cursor movements.** Each subplot represents one of `r length(unique(movement_data$Sequence))` example cursor movements from our experiment, with original cursor positions shown in blue and reconstructed trajectories in red. MAILA's LSTM autoencoder was trained on human–computer interaction from a public dataset of naturalistic computer use [@kilic_bogazici_2021], frozen, and applied to the MAILA dataset, where it achieved an average reconstruction loss of `r reconstruction_loss$avg_loss` (relative to the participant’s screen resolution). This confirms that MAILA captured and reconstructed human–computer interaction with high precision.

\newpage

## Figure S5

```{r}
#| label: SF5_cluster_movement_done
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| fig.align: "center"
#| fig.width: 13
#| fig.height: 14
#| cache: true

# Load required libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(viridis)


# Plot movement trajectories for each cluster
p_movement <- ggplot(cluster_data, aes(x = X, y = Y, group = interaction(Cluster, Movement_ID), color = Time_Step)) +
  geom_path(alpha = 0.8, size = 0.1) +
  facet_wrap(~ Cluster, scales = "free") + xlim(0,1) + ylim(0,1) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "D", direction = 1) + 
  theme_void() +  
  theme(legend.position = "right") +  # Hide facet labels if needed
  labs(color = "Time Step", subtitle = "Example cursor movements across all clusters")

# Print plot
print(p_movement)
```

**Figure S5. Clusters of cursor movement.** Each subplot represents `r length(unique(cluster_data$Movement_ID))` example trajectories from one of the $C$ = `r length(unique(cluster_data$Cluster))` cursor movement clusters. Lighter colors indicate later time steps within each trajectory. Each cluster represents one distinct cursor movement motif observed in our experiment. For each of the N participants, we computed the fraction of embeddings assigned to each cluster C. This results in a participant-by-cluster $\mathrm{X^{N \times C}}$ movement feature matrix. The clusters are assigned in a data-driven way, that is, without any hypotheses about what movement features are meaningful for the downstream task of predicting mental states from human–computer interaction. Different movement clusters emerge when MAILA is calibrated to structured cursor movement (shown here), structured touchscreen activity (not shown here), or free-form touchscreen activity (not shown here). When assessing MAILA's ability to generalize, we performed clustering only on the training data, and transferred the frozen k-means centroids to the new datasets.

\newpage

## Figure S6

```{r}
#| label: SF6_mental_health_matrix
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| out.width: "100%"

plot_mental_health_dist <- predictions_df_all %>%
  filter(Source %in% c("baseline", "follow-up", "touch-d")) %>%
  filter(!Dimension %in% c("Overall Distress", "Overall Wellbeing")) %>%
  mutate(
    Questionnaire = factor(
      ifelse(grepl("Wellbeing", Dimension), "Wellbeing", "Distress"),
      levels = c("Distress", "Wellbeing")
    ),
    Dimension = factor(Dimension, levels = ordered_standard_dimensions)
  ) %>%
  arrange(Dimension)


# **Plot the responses to the different dimensions**    
p_mental_dist <- ggplot(plot_mental_health_dist, aes(x = True, y = Dimension, fill = Dimension)) + 
  geom_density_ridges(color = "white", rel_min_height = 0.01, bandwidth = 0.025, scale = 2.5, alpha = 0.85) +
  theme_ridges(font_size = 10, grid = FALSE, center_axis_labels = TRUE) +
  xlim(0, 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = custom_colors) +
  scale_y_discrete(position = "left") +  # Move y-axis ticks to the right
  facet_grid(rows = vars(Questionnaire), scales = "free_y", space = "free") +  # Facets on the left
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2),
    strip.placement = "outside",  # Required for switch
    strip.text.y.left = element_text(angle = 90),
  ) +
  labs(x = "Response", y = NULL, title = "A", subtitle = "Distribution of self-reports across dimensions")

cor_threshold <- 0
cor_long_true_filtered <- cor_long_true %>% 
  filter(Correlation > cor_threshold) %>%
  # (optional) drop edges touching Overall nodes
  filter(!Var1 %in% drop_dims, !Var2 %in% drop_dims)

cor_long_true_filtered <- cor_long_true_filtered %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    tsne_coords_true_nodes %>% dplyr::rename(X1 = X, Y1 = Y),
    by = c("Var1" = "Dimension")
  ) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    tsne_coords_true_nodes %>% dplyr::rename(X2 = X, Y2 = Y),
    by = c("Var2" = "Dimension")
  )

p_mental_tsne_true <- ggplot() +
  geom_segment(
    data = cor_long_true_filtered,
    aes(x = X1, y = Y1, xend = X2, yend = Y2, linewidth = abs(Correlation)/2),
    color = "gray60", alpha = line_alpha
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = tsne_coords_true_nodes,
    aes(x = X, y = Y, fill = Dimension),
    size = dot_size, shape = 21, color = "white", stroke = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = tsne_coords_true_nodes,
    aes(x = X, y = Y, label = Dimension),
    size = 1.5, box.padding = 0.3, point.padding = 0.3,
    force = 5, segment.color = "gray50", segment.size = 0.3
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = custom_colors) +
  scale_color_manual(values = custom_colors) +
  scale_alpha_continuous(range = alpha_range, guide = "none") +
  scale_linewidth_continuous(range = line_width_range/2, guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(position = "right") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = legend_position,
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2)
  ) +
  labs(x = "t-SNE dimension 1", y = "t-SNE dimension 2", title = "B", subtitle = "t-SNE embedding of dimensions in the space of mental health")

# Load required libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(reshape2)
library(dplyr)
library(viridis)
library(stringr)
library(gridExtra)
library(ggridges)
library(tidyr)

# Global style variables
legend_position <- "none"  # Change to "bottom" if legend needed
viridis_palette_option <- "D"  # Viridis color scheme
alpha_range <- c(0.25, 1)  # Transparency range
dot_size <- 2              # Scatter plot dot size

cor_long_true_raw <- cor_matrix_true %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("Var1") %>%
  pivot_longer(-Var1, names_to = "Var2", values_to = "Correlation") %>%
  dplyr::filter(!Var1 %in% c("Overall Wellbeing", "Overall Distress"),
         !Var2 %in% c("Overall Wellbeing", "Overall Distress")) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Var1 = str_replace_all(Var1, "[\\.-]", " "),
    Var2 = str_replace_all(Var2, "[\\.-]", " "),
    Var1 = factor(Var1, levels = selected_columns_clean),
    Var2 = factor(Var2, levels = selected_columns_clean)
  )
  
p_cor <- ggplot(cor_long_true_raw, aes(x = Var1, y = Var2, fill = Correlation)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "R", limits = c(-1, 1),
                       option = viridis_palette_option) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    axis.text.y = element_text(),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.key.height = unit(2, "mm"),
    legend.key.width  = unit(8, "mm"),
    legend.margin = margin(t = 0, r = 0, b = 0, l = 0),
    legend.box.margin = margin(t = -2, r = 0, b = 0, l = 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev(selected_columns_clean)) +
  labs(title = "C", subtitle = "Correlation among mental health dimensions")

tile_aspect_ratio <- 1     # Adjust for better alignment
viridis_palette_option <- "D"  # Viridis color scheme
alpha_range <- c(0.25, 1)  # Transparency range
dot_size <- 2              # Scatter plot dot size

# Load PCA loadings
pca_loadings_path <- file.path(root, 'regression', 'pca_loadings.csv')
pca_loadings <- read.csv(pca_loadings_path, row.names = 1)
pca_loadings$Dimension <- gsub("[-\\.]", " ", rownames(pca_loadings))

# Convert to long format
loadings_long <- melt(pca_loadings, id.vars = "Dimension", variable.name = "PC", value.name = "Loading")
loadings_long$Dimension <- gsub("[-\\.]", " ", loadings_long$Dimension)
loadings_long$Dimension <- factor(loadings_long$Dimension, levels = rev(selected_columns_clean))
loadings_long <- loadings_long[!is.na(loadings_long$Dimension), ]

# PCA Loadings Heatmap
p_loading <- ggplot(
  subset(loadings_long, PC %in% c("PC1","PC2","PC3","PC4","PC5","PC6","PC7","PC8","PC9","PC10","PC11","PC12","PC13")),
  aes(x = PC, y = Dimension, fill = Loading)
) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_viridis(name = "Loading", option = viridis_palette_option) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    axis.text.y = element_text(),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.key.height = unit(2, "mm"),
    legend.key.width  = unit(8, "mm"),
    legend.margin = margin(t = 0, r = 0, b = 0, l = 0),
    legend.box.margin = margin(t = -2, r = 0, b = 0, l = 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = selected_columns_clean) +
  labs(title = "D", subtitle = "Loading of dimensions onto PCs", x = "", y = "", fill = NULL) +
  coord_flip()

variance_explained$Components = as.factor(variance_explained$Components)
p_variance_explained = ggplot(variance_explained, aes(x = fct_rev(Components), y = Variance_Explained * 100)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#4B0082", fill = "#4B0082", width = 0.25) +  # from transparent (PC1) to opaque (last PC)
  labs(
    x = "Principal Component", y = "Variance (%)",
  title = "E", subtitle = "Variance by PC"
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(
    breaks = rev(variance_explained$Components[as.numeric(variance_explained$Components) %% 2 == 1])  # show 1, 3, 5, ...
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2)) + coord_flip()

lay1 <- rbind(c(1,2,2,2), c(3,3,4,5))
grid.arrange(p_mental_dist, p_mental_tsne_true, 
             p_cor , p_loading, p_variance_explained,
             layout_matrix = lay1, heights = c(1,1), widths = c(2.5,0.5,2.5,1))
```


**Figure S6. The space of mental health.**

**A. The mental health matrix.** The mental health matrix $\mathrm{Y^{N \times Q}}$ comprises self-reports for 67 questionnaire items, each belonging to an overarching dimension of distress and wellbeing. Distress distributions are shown in blue, wellbeing distributions in green, pooled across all participants from the general population in the MAILA dataset. 

**B. Structure and correlation of self-reported mental health.** Each point represents a mental health dimension, positioned in t-SNE space based on its similarity to other dimensions. Line thickness corresponds to the strength of positive correlations (negative correlations not shown).

**C. Correlation matrix.** Correlations between self-reports in the distress and wellbeing domain, pooled across all participants from the general population in the MAILA dataset. Colors indicate the correlation strength and direction. Responses were negatively correlated between the domains of distress and wellbeing (R = `r subset(domain_true_summary, Type == "Between Domains")$mean_r` ± `r subset(domain_true_summary, Type == "Between Domains")$error_r`) and positively correlated between the dimensions of each domain (e.g., anxiety to depression, or emotional to psychological wellbeing). Average correlations reached R = `r subset(domain_true_summary, Type == "Within Distress")$mean_r` ± `r subset(domain_true_summary, Type == "Within Distress")$error_r` between distress dimensions and R = `r subset(domain_true_summary, Type == "Within Wellbeing")$mean_r` ± `r subset(domain_true_summary, Type == "Within Wellbeing")$error_r` within wellbeing dimensions.

**D. PCA loadings.** Loading of each mental health dimension onto the first 13 principal components (PCs) of mental health self-reports (across participants). Colors indicate the strength and direction of the loading on the respective PC. For example, a positive loading for depression on PC1 means that, when a participant experiences increasing depressive symptoms, their score on PC1 will increase. 

**E. Variance explained.** The proportion of mental health variance explained by each principal component. Bars indicate the variance explained per component. Together, the first 3 components accounted for `r 100 * variance_explained$Cumulative_Variance[3]`% of the variance.

\newpage
## Figure S7

```{r}
#| label: SF7_within_clinical
#| include: true
#| out.width: "100%"

clinical_metrics_r
```

**Figure S7. MAILA predicts inter-individual differences among participants who self-identify with mental illness.** Models were trained on all participants and evaluated with 5-fold cross-validation. Markers denote within-group correlations of MAILA's predictions within the general sample, the self-identifying samples (depression and OCD), and the pooled (global) sample; violin plots show null distributions. MAILA captured inter-individual variation across dimensions within the self-identifying sample (R = `r mean_ci_95(subset(clinical_real_touch_all, group == "self-identifying")$rho, na.rm = TRUE)$mean` ± `r mean_ci_95(subset(clinical_real_touch_all, group == "self-identifying")$rho, na.rm = TRUE)$error`), despite broader and shifted symptom distributions relative to the general sample. This pattern supports a dimensional view in which self-identified depression and OCD reflect quantitative differences along shared mental-health dimensions rather than qualitatively distinct categories [@westerhof_mental_2010; @kotov_paradigm_2018].

\newpage
## Figure S8

```{r}
#| label: SF8_autocorr
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| cache: true
#| fig.align: "center"
#| out.width: "100%"


##
## AUTOCORRELATIONS
##

save_dir <- file.path(root, "generalization", "naturalistic")
summary_obs  <- read.csv(file.path(save_dir, "summary_curves_autocorr.csv"))
summary_perm <- read.csv(file.path(save_dir, "summary_perm_curves_autocorr.csv"))

# Tidy (so we can map one shared legend)
obs_df <- summary_obs %>%
  transmute(
    lag,
    mean = mean_r,
    low  = ci95_low,
    hi   = ci95_hi,
    group = "Observed"
  )

perm_df <- summary_perm %>%
  transmute(
    lag,
    mean = null_mean_r,
    low  = null_ci95_low,
    hi   = null_ci95_hi,
    group = "Permuted"
  )


autocorr_plot = ggplot() +
  geom_ribbon(
    data = obs_df,
    aes(x = lag, ymin = low, ymax = hi, fill = "Observed 95% CI"),
    alpha = 0.25
  ) +
  geom_ribbon(
    data = perm_df,
    aes(x = lag, ymin = low, ymax = hi, fill = "Permuted 95% band"),
    alpha = 0.18
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = obs_df,
    aes(x = lag, y = mean, color = "Observed mean R"),
    linewidth = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = perm_df,
    aes(x = lag, y = mean, color = "Permuted mean R"),
    linewidth = 0.5, linetype = "dotted"
  ) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_x_log10(
    name = "Lag (steps)",
    breaks = c(1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100),
    labels = c("1", "2", "5", "10", "20", "50", "100")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    trans = pseudo_log_trans(base = 10, sigma = 1e-3),
    breaks = c(-1, -0.3, -0.1, -0.03, -0.01, -0.003, 0, 0.003, 0.01, 0.03, 0.1, 0.3, 1),
    labels = scales::label_number(accuracy = 0.001),
    name   = "Autocorrelation (R)"
  ) +
  labs(title = "A", subtitle = "MAILA prediction evolve smoothly across consecutive sessions \nof naturalistic human–computer interactions") +
  scale_color_manual(
    name = NULL,
    values = c("Observed mean R" = "black", "Permuted mean R" = "grey30")
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = NULL,
    values = c("Observed 95% CI" = "grey20", "Permuted 95% band" = "grey60")
  ) +
  guides(
    color = guide_legend(nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE),
    fill  = guide_legend(nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = c(0.98, 0.98),      # inside panel: (x, y)
    legend.justification = c(1, 1),       # anchor legend's top-right corner
    legend.direction = "vertical",
    legend.box = "vertical",
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    legend.key = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    legend.margin = margin(4, 4, 4, 4),
    legend.spacing.y = unit(0.05, "lines")
  ) +
  guides(
    color = guide_legend(nrow = 1),
    fill  = guide_legend(nrow = 1)
  )

cor_true <- read.csv(file.path(save_dir, "C_true_long.csv")) %>%
  mutate(scope = "True")

cor_pred <- read.csv(file.path(save_dir, "C_pred_global_long.csv")) %>%
  mutate(scope = "Predicted")

cor_both <- bind_rows(cor_true, cor_pred)

item_levels <- unique(cor_true$Variable1)
cor_both <- cor_both %>%
  mutate(
    Variable1 = factor(Variable1, levels = item_levels),
    Variable2 = factor(Variable2, levels = item_levels)
  )

corr_plot = ggplot(cor_both, aes(x = Variable1, y = Variable2, fill = Correlation)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    name = "r",
    limits = c(-1, 1),
    option = "D"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~scope, nrow = 2) +  # vertical facets: True | Predicted
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x  = element_blank(),       # hide labels
    axis.text.y  = element_blank(),       # hide labels
    axis.ticks   = element_line(color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.3),  # keep subtle ticks
    legend.position = "right",
    panel.grid   = element_blank()
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = rev(item_levels)) +
  labs(
    title = "B", subtitle = "MAILA recovers the correlation structure of mental health from naturalistic human–computer interactions",
    x = "Scores (items, global, and dimensions)",
    y = "Scores (items, global, and dimensions)",
    fill = NULL
  )

correlation_per_window <- read.csv(file.path(save_dir, "correlation_nat_mila.csv"))

lay1 <- rbind(c(1,2))
grid.arrange(autocorr_plot, corr_plot, layout_matrix = lay1, heights = c(1), widths = c(1,1.5))
```

**Figure S8. Validating MAILA on naturalistic cursor movement.** MAILA was trained on cursor movements from the baseline and follow-up datasets and applied, without retraining, to naturalistic cursor activity from 19 individuals, each contributing multiple sessions recorded across an extended period of time and at multiple times of the day [@kilic_bogazici_2021]. While these analyses rely on unlabeled data, the temporal continuity (**A**) and structural consistency (**B**) of MAILA's predictions provide an indirect validation for the embedding of meaningful, generalizable dimensions of mental states in everyday human–computer interaction. In addition, **Figure 2F** shows that MAILA recovers known circadian fluctuations in mental state from this external dataset (higher positive affect in the morning and a rise in negative affect toward nightfall). These diurnal patterns have been independently reported before [@golder_diurnal_2011; @costa-gomes_public_2026] and are also present in the MAILA dataset (ground truth and prediction, **Figure 2D-E**), providing strong external validation of MAILA’s ability to predict mental health.

**A. Autocorrelation of predicted mental health.** Predicted mental-health scores (pooled across participants and items) exhibited a significant positive autocorrelation that decayed monotonically with increasing lag on a log-scaled x-axis. The observed mean (solid line) remained above the participant-wise time-shuffled null (dashed line), with non-overlapping 95% confidence intervals at short lags and convergence toward zero at longer lags. This pattern supports the interpretation that naturalistic cursor movements reflect temporally coherent, slowly evolving latent mental states. The autocorrelation of MAILA’s predictions extracted from independent naturalistic cursor movements closely mirrored that of self-reported scores in the MAILA dataset, where test-retest correlations declined from R = `r subset(change_by_week_dim, week == 0 & Dimension == "BSI")$cor_r` for distress and R = `r subset(change_by_week_dim, week == 0 & Dimension == "MHC-SF")$cor_r`  for wellbeing after one week to R = `r subset(change_by_week_dim, week == 8 & Dimension == "BSI")$cor_r` after eight weeks.

**B. Correlation structure of true and predicted mental health.** To compare the internal structure of mental health across datasets, we z-scored each item separately for true and predicted scores and computed the pairwise correlations between items. The resulting correlation matrices were highly similar (R = `r subset(correlation_per_window, scope == "global")$r_obs`), indicating that predictions derived from naturalistic cursor movements preserved the inter-item structure of mental health observed in the MAILA dataset. 

\newpage
## Figure S9

```{r}
#| label: angles_training_experimental
#| echo: false
#| include: False

angles_file <- file.path(root, "cross_dataset_comparison", "vectors", "angles_histogram_bars.csv")
ang <- read.csv(angles_file)

# Match dataset labels
ang <- ang %>%
  mutate(Dataset = case_when(
    dataset %in% c("Train","train") ~ "Pretraining",
    dataset %in% c("Test","test")   ~ "Experimental",
    TRUE ~ str_to_title(dataset)
  ))

# If no binwidth column, infer it from bin centers
if (!"binwidth" %in% names(ang)) {
  bw <- ang %>%
    distinct(angle_deg) %>%
    arrange(angle_deg) %>%
    mutate(d = angle_deg - dplyr::lag(angle_deg)) %>%
    summarise(bw = median(d, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% dplyr::pull(bw)
  ang$binwidth <- bw
} else {
  bw <- unique(ang$binwidth)
}

p_mila_natural_angle <- ggplot(ang, aes(x = angle_deg, y = count, color = Dataset, fill = Dataset)) +
  geom_col(position = "identity", alpha = 0.4, width = bw) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-180, 180, by = 45)) +
  labs(x = "Angle (deg)", y = "Count (log)", title = "A", subtitle = "Movement angles in MAILA dataset and during naturalistic human–computer interaction") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(legend.position = "top") +
  scale_y_log10()
```

```{r}
#| label: speed_training_experimental
#| echo: false
#| include: False
speed_file <- file.path(root, "cross_dataset_comparison", "vectors", "speed_histogram_bars.csv")
spd <- read.csv(speed_file)

spd <- spd %>%
  mutate(Dataset = case_when(
    dataset %in% c("Train","train") ~ "Pretraining",
    dataset %in% c("Test","test")   ~ "Experimental",
    TRUE ~ str_to_title(dataset)
  ))

# If no binwidth column, infer it from centers
if (!"binwidth" %in% names(spd)) {
  bw <- spd %>%
    distinct(speed) %>%
    arrange(speed) %>%
    mutate(d = speed - lag(speed)) %>%
    summarise(bw = median(d, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% pull(bw)
  spd$binwidth <- bw
} else {
  bw <- unique(spd$binwidth)
}

p_mila_natural_speed <- ggplot(spd, aes(x = speed, y = count, color = Dataset, fill = Dataset)) +
  geom_col(position = "identity", alpha = 0.4, width = bw) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(x = "Speed", y = "Count (log)", title = "B", subtitle = "Movement speed in MAILA dataset and during naturalistic human–computer interaction") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(legend.position = "top") +
  scale_y_log10()
```

```{r}
#| label: speed_vs_angle
#| echo: false
#| include: False
as2d_file <- file.path(root, "cross_dataset_comparison", "vectors", "angle_speed_2d_counts.csv")
as2d <- read.csv(as2d_file)

# Harmonize dataset labels
as2d <- as2d %>%
  mutate(dataset = case_when(
    dataset %in% c("Train","train") ~ "Pretraining",
    dataset %in% c("Test","test")   ~ "Experimental",
    TRUE ~ str_to_title(dataset)
  ))

# Ensure consistent angle/speed limits across facets (same scaling)
angle_limits <- c(-180, 180)
speed_limits <- range(as2d$speed, na.rm = TRUE)
count_limits <- range(as2d$count[as2d$count > 0], na.rm = TRUE)

as2d <- as2d %>% mutate(count = ifelse(count <= 0, NA, count))
count_limits <- range(as2d$count, na.rm = TRUE)

p_angle_speed_heat <- ggplot(as2d, aes(x = angle_deg, y = speed, fill = count)) +
  geom_tile(alpha = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ dataset, ncol = 2) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = angle_limits, breaks = seq(-180, 180, 45)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = speed_limits, expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_fill_viridis(option = "D", trans = "log10",
                     limits = count_limits,
                     breaks = scales::log_breaks()(count_limits),
                     na.value = "white") +
  labs(x = "Angle (deg)", y = "Speed", fill = "Count (log)",  title = "C", subtitle = "Joint distribution of angles and speed in MAILA dataset and during naturalistic human–computer interaction") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(legend.position = "right", strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"))
```

```{r}
#| label: SF9_natural_browsing_pos_angle
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| out.width: "100%"

lay1 <- rbind(c(1,2), c(3,3))
grid.arrange(p_mila_natural_angle, p_mila_natural_speed, p_angle_speed_heat, layout_matrix = lay1, heights = c(1,1), widths = c(1,1))
```

**Figure S9. Distribution of cursor movement angles and speeds in the MAILA dataset and everyday cursor movements.**

**A. Angles.** Histograms show the distribution of cursor movement directions (in degrees from -180° to 180°). The MAILA dataset (red) is compared to everyday cursor movements [@kilic_bogazici_2021] (blue). The overlapping distributions indicate that MAILA captures the natural range of movement directions typically observed during everyday computer use.

**B. Speeds.** Histograms show the distribution of cursor speed (log-scaled y-axis). The MAILA dataset (red) and everyday cursor movements (blue) exhibit highly similar profiles, suggesting comparable dynamics of cursor motion speed across experimental and naturalistic settings.

**C. Joint distribution of angle and speed.** Heatmaps show the logarithmic density of cursor movements as a function of direction and speed for both datasets. Color intensity reflects the frequency of a specific combination of direction and speed (log-scaled). The similarity in structure across datasets indicates that MAILA's response interface reproduces core features of natural cursor trajectories.

\newpage
## Figure S10

```{r}
#| label: SF10_natural_browsing_done
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| fig.align: "center"
#| fig.width: 12
#| fig.height: 12
#| cache: true
#| 
base_font_size = 10
library(ggplot2)
library(readr)

train_file <- file.path(root,'cross_dataset_comparison', "train_data.csv")
test_file <- file.path(root,'cross_dataset_comparison', "test_data.csv")

# Load data
train_data <- read.csv(train_file) %>% mutate(Dataset = "Train") 
test_data <- read.csv(test_file) %>% mutate(Dataset = "Test")

# Combine datasets
full_data <- bind_rows(train_data, test_data)

# Convert to long format for ggplot facets
long_data <- full_data %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -Dataset, names_to = "Feature", values_to = "Value") %>%
  mutate(Feature = gsub("Feature_", "Embedding ", Feature)) 

long_data$Feature = factor(long_data$Feature, levels = unique(long_data$Feature))
long_data$Dataset <- gsub("Test", "Experimental", gsub("Train", "Pretraining", long_data$Dataset))

# Compute mean and standard deviation for each feature and dataset using ddply
feature_stats <- ddply(long_data, .(Dataset, Feature), summarise,
                       Mean = mean(Value, na.rm = TRUE),
                       SD = sd(Value, na.rm = TRUE))

# Merge computed stats back into the original dataset
long_data <- left_join(long_data, feature_stats, by = c("Dataset", "Feature"))

# Plot with means (vertical lines) and variance (shaded region for mean ± SD)
p_dist_test_train <- ggplot(long_data, aes(x = Value, color = Dataset, fill = Dataset)) +
  geom_histogram(alpha = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~Feature, scales = "free", ncol = 5) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  
  # Add vertical lines for means
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = Mean, linetype = Dataset), linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  
  labs(x = "Feature Value", y = "Density", subtitle = "Encoded momvent features in MAILA dataset and during naturalistic human–computer interaction") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) + 
  theme(legend.position = "top")

p_dist_test_train


feature_comparison_file <- file.path(root, 'cross_dataset_comparison', 'feature_comparison.csv')
feature_comparison <- read.csv(feature_comparison_file)

svc_results_file <- file.path(root, 'cross_dataset_comparison', "svc_dataset_predictions.csv")
svc_results <- read.csv(svc_results_file)
```

**Figure S10. Feature space similarity between the MAILA dataset and everyday cursor movements.** We compared LSTM embeddings from the MAILA dataset (experimentally induced cursor movements, red) to those from a public dataset of everyday computer use [@kilic_bogazici_2021] (blue). Across all features, the distributions showed substantial overlap. On average, MAILA embeddings differed by only `r mean(feature_comparison$rel_mean_diff, na.rm = TRUE) * 100` ± `r sd(feature_comparison$rel_mean_diff, na.rm = TRUE) * 100`% of the respective feature range, and `r mean(feature_comparison$within_fraction, na.rm = TRUE) * 100` ± `r sd(feature_comparison$within_fraction, na.rm = TRUE) * 100`% of MAILA embeddings fell within the bounds of the pretraining distribution. Variance was slightly lower in the MAILA dataset compared to the naturalistic dataset ($\Delta_{var}$ = `r mean(feature_comparison$var_diff, na.rm = TRUE)` ± `r sd(feature_comparison$var_diff, na.rm = TRUE)`). Together, these results suggest that, despite our dataset being experimental, it remained broadly representative of everyday computer use.

<!-- A support vector classifier differentiated embeddings form MAILA and the pretraining dataset at an accuracy of `r mean(svc_results$Accuracy, na.rm = TRUE)` ± `r sd(svc_results$Accuracy, na.rm = TRUE)` (5-fold cross-validation).  -->

\newpage
## Figure S11

```{r}
#| label: SF11_paradigm
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| out.width: "100%"
#| out.height: "100%"
#| 
# Load required libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(gridExtra)

base_font_size = 6

screen_min <- -0.5
screen_max <- 0.5
center_min <- -0.2
center_max <- 0.2
radius_min <- 0.1
radius_max <- 0.2

generate_trial <- function(trial_name = NULL) {
  repeat {
    central_x <- runif(1, center_min, center_max)
    central_y <- runif(1, center_min, center_max)
    radius <- runif(1, radius_min, radius_max)
    angle <- runif(1, 0, 2 * pi)
    
    low_x <- central_x + radius * cos(angle)
    low_y <- central_y + radius * sin(angle)
    high_x <- central_x - radius * cos(angle)
    high_y <- central_y - radius * sin(angle)
    
    # Check bounds
    if (all(c(low_x, low_y, high_x, high_y) >= screen_min) &&
        all(c(low_x, low_y, high_x, high_y) <= screen_max)) {
      return(list(
        low_x = low_x, low_y = low_y,
        high_x = high_x, high_y = high_y,
        mid_x = (low_x + high_x)/2,
        mid_y = (low_y + high_y)/2,
        central_x = central_x,
        central_y = central_y,
        radius = radius,
        angle = angle,
        trial = trial_name
      ))
    }
  }
}

set.seed(123)
n_trials <- 10000
trials <- replicate(n_trials, generate_trial(), simplify = FALSE)
positions <- bind_rows(trials)

set.seed(456)
example_list <- lapply(1:3, function(i) generate_trial(paste0("Trial ", i)))
example_df <- bind_rows(example_list)

# Create points (low/high) per trial
example_points <- example_df %>%
  select(trial, low_x, low_y, high_x, high_y, central_x, central_y, radius) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(low_x, high_x, low_y, high_y), names_to = c("label", ".value"),
               names_pattern = "(low|high)_(x|y)") %>%
  mutate(label = ifelse(label == "low", "Not at all", "Very much"))

# Create segments
example_segments <- example_df %>%
  select(trial, low_x, low_y, high_x, high_y)

# Create circles
circle_data <- example_df %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(circle_x = list(central_x + radius * cos(seq(0, 2 * pi, length.out = 100))),
         circle_y = list(central_y + radius * sin(seq(0, 2 * pi, length.out = 100)))) %>%
  unnest(cols = c(circle_x, circle_y))

 
trial_levels <- unique(example_segments$trial)
question_boxes <- data.frame(
  trial = trial_levels,
  xmin = -0.5,
  xmax = 0.5,
  ymin = -0.5,
  ymax = 0.5
)

question_labels <- data.frame(
  trial = trial_levels,
  x = 0.0,
  y = 0.3,
  label = "Item Question"
)

# Plot
example_trials = ggplot() +
  geom_rect(data = question_boxes, aes(xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = ymin, ymax = ymax), 
            fill = "white", color = "black") +
  geom_text(data = question_labels, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label), size = 2, fontface = "bold") +
  geom_segment(data = example_segments, aes(x = low_x, y = low_y, xend = high_x, yend = high_y), color = "black") +
  geom_point(data = example_points, aes(x = x, y = y, color = label), size = 1) +
  
  # <<< UPDATED COLORS >>>
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
  "Not at all" = "#5C01A6",   # plasma purple
  "Very much"  = "#FCA50A"  # plasma orange
  )) +
  
  facet_wrap(~ trial, nrow = 1) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = 'none',
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(x = "x", y = "y", color = NULL, title = "A", subtitle = "Example screens in the MAILA interface")

# Scatter plot
# Create a long-format dataframe for plotting with legend
positions_long <- positions %>%
  select(low_x, low_y, high_x, high_y, mid_x, mid_y) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = everything(),
    names_to = c("label", ".value"),
    names_pattern = "(low|high|mid)_(x|y)"
  ) %>%
  mutate(label = case_when(
    label == "low" ~ "Not at all",
    label == "high" ~ "Very much",
    label == "mid" ~ "Middle"
  ))

# Plot with legend
scatter_plot_endpoints <- ggplot(positions_long, aes(x = x, y = y, color = label)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 0.25) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
  "Not at all" = "#5C01A6",   # plasma purple
  "Very much"  = "#FCA50A",   # plasma orange
    "Middle"    = "black"     # still red
  )) +
  xlim(screen_min, screen_max) + ylim(screen_min, screen_max) +
  labs(x = "x", y = "y", color = NULL, title = "B", subtitle = "Randomized response endpoints across items") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "top",
    legend.spacing.x = unit(0.2, "lines"),
    legend.key.width = unit(0.8, "lines"),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2)
  )

positions_long_combined <- positions_long %>%
  select(label, x, y) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(x, y), names_to = "axis", values_to = "value")

hist_xy <- ggplot(positions_long_combined, aes(x = value, fill = label)) +
  geom_histogram(color = 'white', alpha = 0.75) +
  facet_grid(label ~ axis, scales = "free") +
  labs(x = "screen location", y = "count", subtitle = " ") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Not at all" = my_green, "Very much" = my_blue, "Middle" = my_red)) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')

```

```{r}
#| label: SF11_screen_positions
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| out.width: "100%"


# compute Spearman correlation for eccentricity vs response position
STAT.response_eccentricity <- cor.test(interface_data$avg_eccentricity, 
               interface_data$avg_pos_response, 
               method = "spearman", 
               use = "complete.obs")

scatter_quest_val <-  ggplot(
  interface_data,
  aes(x = avg_final_x, y = avg_final_y, z = avg_pos_response)
) +
  stat_summary_hex(
    fun = mean,
    bins = 70             # try 20-60; fewer = larger hexes
    # OR: binwidth = c(0.03, 0.03)  # explicit size in x/y units
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option = "plasma",
    guide = guide_colorbar(barheight = 0.2, barwidth = 4, title.position = "top")
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2)
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Avg. response location (x)",
    y = "Avg. response location (y)",
    fill = "Self-report",
    title = "D", subtitle = "Cursor eccentricity is not related \nto extreme responses"
  ) + xlim(-0.05, 0.05) + ylim(-0.05, 0.05)


scatter_quest_ecc = ggplot(
  interface_data,
  aes(x = avg_eccentricity, y = avg_pos_response, z = avg_pos_response)
) +
  # hex-binned density
  stat_summary_2d(fun = mean, bins = 150) +
   scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "grey70",     # light
    high = "black",    # dark
    guide = guide_colorbar(
      barheight = 0.2,
      barwidth = 4,
      title.position = "top"
    )
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2)
  ) + xlim(0,0.1) +
  labs(x = "Eccentricity", y = "Self-report", subtitle = "B")

plot_scatter_ecc_pos = ggplot(ecc_pos_data, aes(x = Type, y = observed_rho)) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = mean_ci_95(observed_rho)$mean - mean_ci_95(observed_rho)$error, ymax = mean_ci_95(observed_rho)$mean + mean_ci_95(observed_rho)$error),
    width = 0.1, linewidth = 1, color = 'black'
  ) +
  geom_jitter(size = 1, alpha = 0.25, width = 0.01) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black", linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  labs(
    x = "Predictor",
    y = "Spearman r",
    subtitle = "C"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(1, "lines")
  ) + coord_flip()

# enforce facet/legend order: Position first, then Eccentricity
ecc_pos_data <- ecc_pos_data %>%
  dplyr::mutate(Type = factor(Type, levels = c("Position", "Eccentricity")))

# if question_summary is derived separately, rebuild it from the ordered data
question_summary <- ecc_pos_data %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    null_lo = null_mean - 1.96 * null_sd,
    null_hi = null_mean + 1.96 * null_sd
  )

# Plot: observed rho vs. null band per question
plot_question_rho <- ggplot(question_summary,
                            aes(x = factor(question_ID), y = observed_rho)) +

  # null band (grey vertical interval)
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = null_lo, ymax = null_hi),
                width = 0.3, colour = "grey90", alpha = 0.8) +

  # observed rho (blue point)
  geom_point(colour = "black", size = 1) + facet_wrap(~Type, ncol = 2) +

  # zero line
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +

  labs(x = "Question ID",
       y = "Spearman r",
       subtitle = "D") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1)
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(
    breaks = function(x) {
      # keep only every 5th tick
      idx <- as.numeric(x)
      x[idx %% 5 == 0]
    }
  )

##
##
##
  
# Real metrics
real_cursor_location <- read_with_source(file.path(root,"validation", "regression_from_location", "summary_regression.csv"), "Positions") |>
  select(Dimension, NRMSE, R, AUC_ROC, Source) |> distinct()
real_cursor_mila <- read_with_source(file.path(root,"regression", "summary_regression.csv"), "MAILA") |>
select(Dimension, NRMSE, R, AUC_ROC_50, Source) |> distinct()
real_cursor_mila$AUC_ROC = real_cursor_mila$AUC_ROC_50

real_joint_df_location <- bind_rows(real_cursor_location, real_cursor_mila)


# Permuted
perm_rho_cursor_location  <- read_with_source(file.path(root,"validation", "regression_from_location","summary_permuted_rhos.csv"),  "positions")
permuted_rhos_df_location <- bind_rows(perm_rho_cursor_location)
  


permuted_rhos_df_location$Dimension <- factor(permuted_rhos_df_location$Dimension, levels = ordered_standard_dimensions)
real_joint_df_location$Dimension      <- factor(real_joint_df_location$Dimension, levels = ordered_standard_dimensions)


## Long format for metrics
perm_rho_long_location <- permuted_rhos_df_location |>
  dplyr::rename(Value = Permuted_R) |>
  mutate(Metric = "r") |>
  select(Dimension, Value, Metric, Source)

permuted_combined_location <- bind_rows(perm_rho_long_location) |>
  mutate(
    Metric = factor(Metric, levels = c("r"), ordered = TRUE),
    Questionnaire = factor(ifelse(grepl("Wellbeing", Dimension), "Wellbeing", "Distress"),
                           levels = c("Distress","Wellbeing"))
  )

# Real (KEEP Source → different shapes)
joint_long_location <- real_joint_df_location |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c("R", "AUC_ROC", "NRMSE"), names_to = "Metric", values_to = "Value") |>
  mutate(Metric = recode(Metric, "R" = "r", "AUC_ROC_50" = "AUC", "AUC_ROC" = "AUC", "NRMSE" = "e"),
         Metric = factor(Metric, levels = c("r","AUC", "e"), ordered = TRUE),
         Questionnaire = factor(ifelse(grepl("Wellbeing", Dimension), "Wellbeing", "Distress"),
                                levels = c("Distress","Wellbeing")))

pd <- position_dodge2(width = 0.2)
p_metrics_permuted_location <- ggplot() +
  # pooled null violins (transparent outline)
  geom_violin(
    data = permuted_combined_location %>% dplyr::filter(Metric == "r"),
    aes(x = Dimension, y = Value, fill = Dimension),
    alpha = 0.35, width = 1, bw = 0.01, color = NA
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = joint_long_location %>% dplyr::filter(Metric == "r"),
    aes(x = Dimension, y = Value, color = Dimension, fill = Dimension, shape = Source),
    #position = pd,
    size = 1, stroke = 0.6
  ) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black", linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  # Shape mapping (fillable shapes)
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("MAILA" = 21, "Positions" = 22)) +
  # Dimension colors (outline + baseline fill)
  scale_color_manual(values = custom_colors, guide = "none") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = custom_colors, guide = "none") +

  facet_grid("Regression" ~ Questionnaire, scales = "free", space = "free_x") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "R", title = "C", subtitle = "Screen location are not predictive of survey responses", shape = NULL) +
  theme(
  legend.position = "bottom",
  axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
  panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
  panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
  panel.spacing.y = unit(1, "lines")
  ) + ylim(-0.05,0.3) +
guides(shape = guide_legend(ncol = 2))


pd_jit <- position_jitterdodge(jitter.width = 0.06, dodge.width = 0.5)
p_auc_jitter_location <- ggplot() +
  # 1) BASELINE — solid markers: fill = Dimension color (matches outline)
  geom_point(
    data = joint_long_location %>% dplyr::filter(Metric == "AUC"),
    aes(x = Source, y = Value, color = Dimension, fill = Dimension, shape = Source),
    position = pd_jit,
    size = 1, stroke = 0.6
  ) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c("MAILA" = 21, "Positions" = 22)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = custom_colors, guide = "none") +  # no Dimension color legend
  scale_fill_manual(values = custom_colors, guide = "none") +   # hide fill legend too
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "AUC", subtitle = "C") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, color = "black", linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",                      # only the Source (shape) legend will show
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(1, "lines")
  ) + ylim(0.48, 0.65)
  

```


```{r}
#| label: SF11_screen_positions_plot
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| out.width: "100%"
lay1 <- rbind(c(1,2), c(3,2), c(3,4), c(3,4))
grid.arrange(example_trials, scatter_plot_endpoints, p_metrics_permuted_location, scatter_quest_val, layout_matrix = lay1, heights = c(1.2,0.8,1,1), widths = c(2,1))
```

**Figure S11. Questionnaire paradigm.**

**A. Task interface and randomized response mapping.** Example response screens from the web-based questionnaire paradigm. Each panel shows one trial in which the response line appears at a random location and orientation. The line is flanked by two color-coded anchors: “Not at all” (illustrated here in purple) and “Very much” (illustrated here in orange). The item prompt (e.g., “How much are you distressed by feeling blue?”) appears at the top of the screen.
The spatial position, orientation, and length of the response line were independently randomized on every trial and for every participant, ensuring that motor behavior could not trivially encode the intended response. Note: In the actual experiment, the anchors were shown in green (“Not at all”) and blue (“Very much”).

**B. Spatial distribution of response endpoints.** Screen coordinates of response-line midpoints and endpoints across 10,000 simulated trials. Endpoints labeled “Not at all” (purple) and “Very much” (orange) are symmetrically arranged around randomized center positions; midpoints (never displayed) are shown in black. This randomized spatial encoding prevents raw pointer coordinates from carrying systematic information about the meaning of participants’ responses.

**C. Regression analysis.** To further confirm that screen positions did not permit trivial decoding of mental health, even when considering human–computer interactions recorded during survey completion, we trained support vector regression models to predict self-reported mental health from x and y screen coordinates. Their cross-validated performance did not exceed permuted baselines and remained well below MAILA’s movement-based features (R = `r mean_ci_95(subset(joint_long_location, Metric == 'r' & Source == 'Positions')$Value)$mean` ± `r mean_ci_95(subset(joint_long_location, Metric == 'r' & Source == 'Positions')$Value)$error`; round versus square markers). These controls complement analyses where we applied frozen MAILA models to non-mental-health settings (non-psychological survey and gamified decision-making experiment) and to free-form digital activity without any link to self-reports (Figure 2), confirming that successful decoding of mental health relied on movement features rather than trivial spatial cues.

**D. Self-reports versus screen positions.** The plot shows average x and y response cursor positions per participant, colored by average of the associated self-reports. The uniform color distribution indicates that eccentricity was not correlated with the self-reports (R = `r STAT.response_eccentricity$estimate`, p = `r STAT.response_eccentricity$p.value`). Please note that, as additional safeguards against trivial decoding, MAILA received the entirety of the cursor or touch trajectory as its input, without any labeling of the screen position of the response, or at what point in the recording a specific mental health item was presented (random order of intermixed distress and wellbeing items).

\newpage
## Figure S12

```{r}
#| label: SF12_generalization
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| cache: true
#| out.width: "100%"

base_font_size <- 6
predictions_df_all$e = abs(predictions_df_all$True - predictions_df_all$Predicted)

# Styled histogram plot
p_error_distribution_retest <- ggplot(subset(predictions_df_all, Source %in% c("baseline", "follow-up")), aes(x = e, fill = Source)) +
  geom_histogram(position = "identity", bins = 100, alpha = 1, color = "white") +
  scale_x_log10() + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(1e-3, NA)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("follow-up" = "black", "baseline" = "grey70")) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  labs(
    x = "e",
    y = "count",
    title = "A", subtitle = "MAILA generalizes over time"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = 'top',        # upper-left inside plot
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(1.5, "lines")
  )

# Styled histogram plot
p_error_distribution_survey <- ggplot(subset(predictions_df_all, Source %in% c("baseline", "survey")), aes(x = e, fill = Source)) +
  geom_histogram(position = "identity", bins = 100, alpha = 1, color = "white") +
  scale_x_log10() + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(1e-3, NA)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("survey" = "black", "baseline" = "grey70")) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  labs(
    x = "e",
    y = "count",
    title = "B", subtitle = "MAILA generalizes to non-mental-health surveys"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = 'top',        # upper-left inside plot
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(1.5, "lines")
  )

# Styled histogram plot
p_error_distribution_game <- ggplot(subset(predictions_df_all, Source %in% c("baseline", "game")), aes(x = e, fill = Source)) +
  geom_histogram(position = "identity", bins = 100, alpha = 1, color = "white") +
  scale_x_log10() + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(1e-3, NA)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("game" = "black", "baseline" = "grey70")) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  labs(
    x = "e",
    y = "count",
    title = "C", subtitle = "MAILA generalizes to gameplay"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = 'top',        # upper-left inside plot
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(1.5, "lines")
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: SF12_non_mental_health_corr
#| include: true
#| out.width: "100%"


perm_df_beads <- read.csv(
  file.path(root, "generalization/cv_item_to_item_perm_r_beads.csv")
)
perm_df_beads$Type <- "game"

cv_corr_matrix_beads <- read.csv(
  file.path(root, "generalization/cv_item_to_item_r_beads.csv")
)

perm_df_quest <- read.csv(
  file.path(root, "generalization/cv_item_to_item_perm_r_quest.csv")
)
perm_df_quest$Type <- "survey"

cv_corr_matrix_quest <- read.csv(
  file.path(root, "generalization/cv_item_to_item_r_quest.csv")
)


overall_beads_mean <- mean_ci_95(cv_corr_matrix_beads[, -1])$mean
overall_quest_mean <- mean_ci_95(cv_corr_matrix_quest[, -1])$mean
overall_beads_ci   <- mean_ci_95(cv_corr_matrix_beads[, -1])$error
overall_quest_ci   <- mean_ci_95(cv_corr_matrix_quest[, -1])$error


perm_overall_beads <- perm_df_beads %>%
  group_by(perm_idx) %>%
  summarise(mean_perm = mean(r_perm, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

overall_beads_p <- sum(perm_df_beads$r_perm >= overall_beads_mean) /
                   length(perm_df_beads$r_perm)

perm_overall_quest <- perm_df_quest %>%
  group_by(perm_idx) %>%
  summarise(mean_perm = mean(r_perm, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

overall_quest_p <- sum(perm_df_quest$r_perm >= overall_quest_mean) /
                   length(perm_df_quest$r_perm)

obs_df <- data.frame(
  Type         = c("game", "survey"),
  overall_mean = c(overall_beads_mean, overall_quest_mean),
  overall_ci   = c(overall_beads_ci,   overall_quest_ci),
  overall_p    = c(overall_beads_p,    overall_quest_p)
)


obs_single_beads <- data.frame(
  Type  = "game",
  Value = rowMeans(cv_corr_matrix_beads[, -1], na.rm = TRUE)
)

obs_single_quest <- data.frame(
  Type  = "survey",
  Value = rowMeans(cv_corr_matrix_quest[, -1], na.rm = TRUE)
)

obs_single_all <- bind_rows(obs_single_beads, obs_single_quest)


perm_df_all <- bind_rows(perm_df_beads, perm_df_quest)


perm_df_all$Type <- factor(
  perm_df_all$Type,
  levels = c("survey", "game")
)

obs_df$Type <- factor(
  obs_df$Type,
  levels = c("survey", "game")
)

non_mental_health_to_mental_helath = ggplot(perm_df_all, aes(x = r_perm)) +
  geom_density(fill = "grey80", alpha = 0.7, color = NA) +
  geom_vline(
    data = obs_df,
    aes(xintercept = overall_mean),
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0,
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "white",
    linewidth = 0.25
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ Type, nrow = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position   = "bottom",
    legend.key.width  = unit(0.6, "lines"),
    legend.spacing.x  = unit(0.3, "lines"),
    legend.text       = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.8),
    panel.grid.major  = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor  = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
    panel.spacing.y   = unit(1, "lines")
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "R: Predicting mental health self-reports from behavior unrelated to mental health",
    y = "Density", title = "D", subtitle = "Behavior on the non-mental-health and during gameplay does not predict self-reported distress and wellbeing"
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: SF12_plot
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| out.width: "100%"

lay1 <- rbind(c(1,2,3), c(1,4,4))
grid.arrange(p_error_distribution_retest, p_error_distribution_survey, p_error_distribution_game, non_mental_health_to_mental_helath,
             layout_matrix = lay1, heights = c(2,1), widths = c(1,1,1))
```

**Figure S12. Cursor-based mental health predictions generalize across contexts that are not related to mental health.** 

**A.-C. Error distributions across contexts.** Distributions of normalized root mean squared errors (e) are shown on a logarithmic scale for the baseline (grey) and three generalization datasets (black). Models trained on baseline cursor-movement data (N = 4,000) were applied without retraining to follow-up sessions (**A**, subset of N = 2000), independent online surveys (**B**, subset of N = 600 within the follow-up dataset), and an interactive game (**C**, independent subset of N = 600 within the follow-up dataset). Compared to baseline cross-validation, prediction errors decreased by `r -mean_ci_95(100*summary_nrmse_participant_og_time$difference)$mean` ± `r mean_ci_95(100*summary_nrmse_participant_og_time$difference)$error`% when frozen MAILA models were applied to the follow-up data (p = `r STAT.error_test_time$coefficients[1,5]`). Prediction errors increased by `r mean_ci_95(100*summary_nrmse_participant_og_survey$difference)$mean` ± `r mean_ci_95(100*summary_nrmse_participant_og_survey$difference)$error`% when mental health was inferred from cursor movements during survey completion (p = `r STAT.error_test_survey$coefficients[1,5]`), and decreased by `r -mean_ci_95(100*summary_nrmse_participant_og_game$difference)$mean` ± `r mean_ci_95(100*summary_nrmse_participant_og_game$difference)$error`% for predictions based on gameplay (p = `r STAT.error_test_game$coefficients[1,5]`). The overlapping error distributions indicate that cursor-based predictions of mental health generalize robustly across time, task, and behavioral context.

**D. Predicting mental-health self-reports from responses in the non-mental health survey and gameplay**. In this control analysis, we confirmed that items from the generalization experiments (non-mental health survey and game) did not carry any above-chance information about the participants’ mental-health self-reports at follow-up. We trained linear models to predict each of the 67 mental-health items from each non-mental-health item using 5-fold cross-validation, and quantified prediction accuracy using the Spearman correlation between predicted and observed responses. The grey density curves show the permuted null distributions; solid vertical lines indicate the empirical cross-validated correlations, averaged across all predictor-target pairs (survey: R = `r overall_quest_mean`, p = `r overall_quest_p`; game: `r overall_beads_mean`, p = `r overall_beads_p`). For both the non-mental health survey (left) and the game (right), empirical correlations did not exceed the permuted null, indicating that responses to non-mental-health items did not provide above-chance information about the participants’ mental-health self-reports. 

**Figure S11** and **Table S2** highlight two additional safeguards ensuring that MAILA’s generalization performance relied on context-invariant movement patterns rather than any trivial association with task content. First, the randomized response mapping eliminated any direct relationship between pointer coordinates and participants’ answers (Figure S11). Second, MAILA did not learn to predict the non–mental-health items themselves (R = `r mean_ci_95(subset(gen_survey_within_regression_data, Questionnaire == "Control")$Real_Rho)$mean` ± `r mean_ci_95(subset(gen_survey_within_regression_data, Questionnaire == "Control")$Real_Rho)$error`, p = `r STAT.prediction_within_survey$p.value`) or gameplay behavior (R = `r mean_ci_95(gen_game_within_regression_data$Real_Rho)$mean` ± `r mean_ci_95(gen_game_within_regression_data$Real_Rho)$error`, p = `r STAT.prediction_within_game$p.value`, Table S2).

\newpage
## Figure S13

```{r}
#| label: SF13_example_drawings
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| fig.align: "center"
#| fig.width: 7
#| fig.height: 7
#| cache: true

base_dir <- file.path(root, "example_drawings")

files <- tibble(
  file    = c("example_1.csv", "example_2.csv", "example_3.csv", "example_4.csv", "example_5.csv"),
  example = c("Example 1",     "Example 2",     "Example 3",     "Example 4",     "Example 5")
)

df <- map2_dfr(files$file, files$example, ~
  readr::read_csv(file.path(base_dir, .x), show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
    mutate(example = .y)
)

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    participant = factor(example, levels = files$example),  # legend order = file list
    label       = factor(label)
  )

bb <- df %>%
  group_by(label, participant, seg_idx) %>%
  summarise(
    xmin = first(box_x0), xmax = first(box_x1),
    ymin = first(box_y0), ymax = first(box_y1),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(xmin), !is.na(xmax), !is.na(ymin), !is.na(ymax))

if (!exists("base_font_size")) base_font_size <- 9

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    participant = factor(participant, levels = unique(participant)),
    label       = factor(label,       levels = unique(label))
  )

lab_lvls <- levels(df$label)
cut_idx  <- ceiling(length(lab_lvls) / 2)
labs_left  <- lab_lvls[1:cut_idx]
labs_right <- lab_lvls[(cut_idx + 1):length(lab_lvls)]

df_left  <- df %>% filter(label %in% labs_left)
df_right <- df %>% filter(label %in% labs_right)

make_panel <- function(d){
  labs_this <- unique(d$label)
  d <- d %>%
    mutate(
      label       = factor(label, levels = labs_this),          # only these labels exist
      participant = factor(participant, levels = levels(df$participant))  # keep 5 cols
    )

  ggplot(d, aes(x = x, y = y, colour = participant)) +
    geom_path(aes(group = interaction(participant, seg_idx)),
              linewidth = 0.5, alpha = 0.7, na.rm = TRUE) +
    scale_y_reverse() +
    coord_fixed() +
    facet_grid(
      rows   = vars(label),
      cols   = vars(participant),
      switch = "y",
      drop   = FALSE                                           # keep empty participant cols
    ) +
    labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, colour = NULL, subtitle = NULL) +
    theme_void(base_size = base_font_size) +
    guides(colour = "none") +
    theme(
      legend.position   = "none",
      panel.spacing     = unit(6, "pt"),
      plot.margin       = margin(6, 6, 6, 6),
      strip.placement   = "outside",
      strip.background  = element_blank(),
      strip.text.x      = element_blank(),
      strip.text.y.left = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 1, vjust = 0.5)
    )
}

p_left  <- make_panel(df_left)
p_right <- make_panel(df_right)

g <- gridExtra::grid.arrange(p_left, p_right, ncol = 2, widths = c(1, 1))

```

**Figure S13. Example drawings.** Free-form touchscreen drawings from five randomly selected participants, with prompts displayed to the left. See Table S3 for a list of prompts.

\newpage
## Figure S14

```{r}
#| label: SF14_reliability
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| out.width: "100%"
p_reliability_metrics_permuted

ggsave("cursor_mind_files/rev_Figure_S14.svg", p_reliability_metrics_permuted, width = 9, height = 5, units = "in", device = "svg")
```

**Figure S14. Split-half reliability of MAILA across datasets and dimensions.**  To assess the internal reliability of MAILA, we divided each participant’s cursor or touchscreen trajectories into randomized 50/50 subsets and trained support vector regression models on one half to predict self-reported mental health in the other. We repeated this procedure in the reverse direction, yielding two independent prediction vectors per participant. Violin plots depict permutation-based null distributions obtained by shuffling one split. Filled markers denote correlation coefficients from unseen participants in 5-fold cross-validation (baseline, touch, and touch-d); unfilled markers denote correlation coefficients when frozen MAILA models were applied to independent generalization datasets (follow-up, survey, and game). MAILA’s predictions were highly consistent across randomized halves (R = `r mean_ci_95(av_reliability_joint_df$R)$mean` ± `r mean_ci_95(av_reliability_joint_df$R)$error`, across all dimensions and datasets), demonstrating a level of reliability that exceeded most behavioral and neuroimaging markers of mental health.

\newpage
## Figure S15

```{r}
#| label: SF15_algorithmic_bias
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| fig.align: "center"
#| fig.width: 12
#| fig.height: 14
#| out.width: "100%"
#| cache: true

base_font_size = 8
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(forcats)  # For dynamic reordering in ggplot
library(lubridate)

# Bin Age into decades and add to categories
error_dem$Age_Bin <- cut(as.numeric(error_dem$Age), breaks = seq(10, 100, by = 10), right = FALSE, include.lowest = TRUE)
error_dem$Age_Bin <- as.character(error_dem$Age_Bin)
error_dem$Age_Bin[is.na(error_dem$Age_Bin)] <- "Other"  # Handle NA

# Add Age_Bin to categories for reshaping
error_dem$Age <- NULL  # optional: remove raw Age if not needed
names(error_dem)[names(error_dem) == "Age_Bin"] <- "Age"  # rename for consistency
error_dem$Age <- gsub("\\)", "]", error_dem$Age)  # Replace closing parenthesis with square bracket
error_dem$Age <- factor(error_dem$Age, levels = c(sort(unique(error_dem$Age[error_dem$Age != "Other"])), "Other"))

names(error_dem)[names(error_dem) == "Sex"] <- "Gender"  # rename for consistency
# Parse datetime and extract hour
error_dem$Hour <- hour(ymd_hms(error_dem$Started.at))

# List of categorical variables
categories <- c("Gender", "Ethnicity.simplified", 
                "Country.of.birth", "Country.of.residence", 
                "Nationality", "Language", 
                "Student.status", "Employment.status", "Age")

# Convert to long format
error_dem_long <- error_dem %>%
  gather(key = "Variable", value = "Category", all_of(categories), factor_key = TRUE)
```
