```{r}
error_dem_long = subset(error_dem_long, !Category %in% c("DATA_EXPIRED", "CONSENT_REVOKED"))

error_dem_long <- error_dem_long %>%
  mutate(Variable = gsub(" simplified", "", gsub("\\.", " ", Variable)))

error_dem_long$Category <- gsub("^$|^Other$|^Prefer not to say$|^Unknown$", "Other", error_dem_long$Category)

error_dem_long_avg <- ddply(
  error_dem_long, .(Participant_ID, Variable, Category), summarize,
  mean_error = mean(NRMSE, na.rm = TRUE)
)

total_pids <- length(unique(error_dem_long_avg$Participant_ID))

group_error_dem_long_avg <- ddply(
  error_dem_long_avg, .(Variable, Category), summarize,
  av_error = mean_ci_95(mean_error, na.rm = TRUE)$mean,
  ci_error = mean_ci_95(mean_error, na.rm = TRUE)$error,
  fraction = length(mean_error)/total_pids  # Use fraction instead of count
)

# Sort categories by frequency for better visualization
group_error_dem_long_avg <- group_error_dem_long_avg %>%
  arrange(Variable, fraction)

# Ensure categories are ordered correctly
group_error_dem_long_avg$Category <- factor(
  group_error_dem_long_avg$Category, 
  levels = unique(group_error_dem_long_avg$Category)
)

# Compute facet-specific max values for scaling using ddply
facet_max_values <- ddply(
  group_error_dem_long_avg, .(Variable), summarize,
  max_av_mse = max(av_error, na.rm = TRUE),
  max_fraction = max(fraction, na.rm = TRUE)
)

# Merge back with the main dataset using ddply
group_error_dem_long_avg <- merge(group_error_dem_long_avg, facet_max_values, by = "Variable")

# Define variable groups
variable_groups <- list(
  c("Nationality", "Country of birth", "Country of residence"),
  c("Gender", "Ethnicity"),
  c("Student status", "Employment status"),
  c("Age")
)

for (i in seq_along(variable_groups)) {
  selected_vars <- variable_groups[[i]]

p <- ggplot(
    group_error_dem_long_avg %>%
      filter(Variable %in% selected_vars),  
    aes(x = fct_reorder(Category, fraction, .desc = TRUE), y = av_error)
  ) +

    geom_segment(
      aes(xend = fct_reorder(Category, fraction, .desc = TRUE),
          y = av_error - ci_error, 
          yend = av_error + ci_error),
      color = my_red,
      size = line_size_segment,
      alpha = alpha_segment
    ) +
    geom_point(
      size = dot_size_main/4,
      color = my_red
    ) +
    
    # Fraction (red points + connecting lines)
    geom_line(
      aes(y = fraction), 
      group = 1, color = "black", linetype = "dashed", size = line_size_count, alpha = alpha_count/2
    ) +
    geom_point(
      aes(y = fraction),
      size = dot_size_count/4, color = "black", alpha = alpha_count/2
    ) +

    # Clean theme and labels
    theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
    labs(
      y = "log-scaled e (black) and group fractions (red)",
      x = NULL,
      subtitle = "MAILA generalizes fairly across demographic groups"
    ) +
    
    # Log scale transformation
    scale_y_log10() +

    # Flip coordinates and facet by variable
    coord_flip() +
    facet_wrap(~Variable, scales = "free_y", nrow = 1) + 
    theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(1, "lines"))

# Store plot dynamically
assign(paste("p_dem_", i, sep = ""), p)
}

lay <- rbind(c(1), c(3,2,4))
grid.arrange(
p_dem_1, p_dem_2, p_dem_3, p_dem_4, 
  layout_matrix = lay,
  heights = c(10,2),
  widths = c(1,1,0.5)
)
```

**Figure S15. Algorithmic bias.** MAILA’s prediction errors pooled over the first three principal components of mental health (log-scaled normalized root mean squared error, e), shown as mean ± 95% confidence intervals across participants. Errors are shown in red, and the log-scaled fraction of individuals within each demographic category is overlaid in grey. To assess the influence of each demographic factor, we fitted linear mixed-effects models to the errors, with fixed effects corresponding to the categorical levels of the respective factor and a random intercept to capture individual-level differences in predictive performance. We evaluated the significance of each demographic factor using type III analysis of variance (ANOVA) on the fixed effects. There was no significant effect of gender (F = `r anova_demographics$Fvalue[2]`, p = `r anova_demographics$pvalue[2]`), ethnicity (F = `r anova_demographics$Fvalue[3]`, p = `r anova_demographics$pvalue[3]`), country of birth (F = `r anova_demographics$Fvalue[4]`, p = `r anova_demographics$pvalue[4]`), country of residence (F = `r anova_demographics$Fvalue[5]`, p = `r anova_demographics$pvalue[5]`), nationality (F = `r anova_demographics$Fvalue[6]`, p = `r anova_demographics$pvalue[6]`), student status (F = `r anova_demographics$Fvalue[8]`, p = `r anova_demographics$pvalue[8]`), or age (F = `r anova_demographics$Fvalue[10]`, p = `r anova_demographics$pvalue[10]`). MAILA's prediction errors varied significantly with employment status (F = `r anova_demographics$Fvalue[9]`, p = `r anova_demographics$pvalue[9]`), and there was a borderline significant effect of language (F = `r anova_demographics$Fvalue[7]`, p = `r anova_demographics$pvalue[7]`).

\newpage

## Figure S16

```{r}
#| label: SF16_demographics_composition
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| out.width: "100%"

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(forcats)
library(scales)

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(forcats)
library(scales)

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(forcats)
library(scales)
base_font_size = 6
df_sub <- error_dem %>%
  dplyr::filter(Status == "APPROVED") %>%
  dplyr::filter(
    Age != "DATA_EXPIRED",
    Gender != "DATA_EXPIRED",
    `Employment.status` != "DATA_EXPIRED",
    `Ethnicity.simplified` != "DATA_EXPIRED",
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Age                 = forcats::fct_explicit_na(Age, na_level = "Other"),
    Gender              = forcats::fct_explicit_na(Gender, na_level = "Other"),
    `Employment.status` = forcats::fct_explicit_na(`Employment.status`, na_level = "Other"),
    `Ethnicity.simplified` = forcats::fct_explicit_na(`Ethnicity.simplified`, na_level = "Other")
  )

age_counts <- dplyr::count(df_sub, Source, Age, name = "n")

p_age <- ggplot(age_counts, aes(x = Source, y = n, fill = Age)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(y = "Proportion", x = "Dataset", fill = "Age bin", subtitle = "A") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  coord_flip() +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE)) +
  theme(
    legend.position    = "right",
    legend.text        = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.85),
    legend.title       = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.9),
    legend.key.size    = unit(3, "mm"),
    legend.spacing.y   = unit(1, "mm"),
    legend.box.margin  = margin(t = 2, r = 2, b = 2, l = 2, unit = "mm"),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1)
  )

gender_counts <- dplyr::count(df_sub, Source, Gender, name = "n")

p_gender <- ggplot(gender_counts, aes(x = Source, y = n, fill = Gender)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(y = "Proportion", x = "Dataset", fill = "Gender", subtitle = "B") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  coord_flip() +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(ncol = 1, byrow = TRUE)) +
  theme(
    legend.position    = "right",
    legend.text        = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.85),
    legend.title       = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.9),
    legend.key.size    = unit(3, "mm"),
    legend.spacing.y   = unit(1, "mm"),
    legend.box.margin  = margin(t = 2, r = 2, b = 2, l = 2, unit = "mm"),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1)
  )

emp_counts <- dplyr::count(df_sub, Source, `Employment.status`, name = "n")

p_emp <- ggplot(emp_counts, aes(x = Source, y = n, fill = `Employment.status`)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(y = "Proportion", x = "Dataset", fill = "Employment status", subtitle = "C") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  coord_flip() +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(ncol = 1, byrow = TRUE)) +
  theme(
    legend.position    = "right",
    legend.text        = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.85),
    legend.title       = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.9),
    legend.key.size    = unit(3, "mm"),
    legend.spacing.y   = unit(1, "mm"),
    legend.box.margin  = margin(t = 2, r = 2, b = 2, l = 2, unit = "mm"),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1)
  )

ethn_counts <- dplyr::count(df_sub, Source, `Ethnicity.simplified`, name = "n")

p_ethn<- ggplot(ethn_counts, aes(x = Source, y = n, fill = `Ethnicity.simplified`)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(y = "Proportion", x = "Dataset", fill = "Ethnicity", subtitle = "D") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  coord_flip() +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(ncol = 1, byrow = TRUE)) +
  theme(
    legend.position    = "right",
    legend.text        = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.85),
    legend.title       = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.9),
    legend.key.size    = unit(3, "mm"),
    legend.spacing.y   = unit(1, "mm"),
    legend.box.margin  = margin(t = 2, r = 2, b = 2, l = 2, unit = "mm"),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1)
  )

# layout
lay1 <- rbind(c(1), c(2), c(3), c(4))
grid.arrange(p_age, p_gender, p_emp,  p_ethn, layout_matrix = lay1, heights = c(1,1,1,1), widths = c(1))
```

**Figure S16. Age, gender, employment, and ethnicity composition between MAILA's datasets.** Stacked bar plots show the proportional distribution of participants across (**A**) age bins, (**B**) gender, (**C**) employment status, and ethnicity (**D**), separately for MAILA's dataset.

\newpage
## Figure S17

```{r}
#| label: Figure_S17
#| include: true
#| out.width: "100%"

p_residualized_pred

ggsave("cursor_mind_files/rev_Figure_S17.svg", p_residualized_pred, width = 9, height = 5, units = "in", device = "svg")
```

**Figure S17. Demographic confound control for MAILA’s mental-health predictions.** Points show correlations (R) between MAILA predictions and ground-truth self-reports across mental-health dimensions, shown separately for each dataset. Filled markers show raw correlations; hollow markers show correlations after residualizing both predicted and true scores with respect to all available demographic covariates (partial correlation with age, gender, ethnicity, country of birth/residence, nationality, language, student and employment status as covariates). Across datasets and dimensions, adjusted correlations remained greater than zero (mean R = `r ci_rho_adj$mean` ± `r ci_rho_adj$error`, T(`r STAT.adj$parameter`) = `r STAT.adj$statistic`, p = `r STAT.adj$p.value`), indicating that MAILA’s predictive signal cannot be reduced to demographic proxies.

\newpage
## Figure S18

```{r}
#| label: SF18_simulation_done
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| cache: true
#| fig.align: "center"
#| out.width: "100%"
#| 

base_font_size = 6
# Define the file path to the saved simulation results
results_path <- file.path(root, "simulation", "simulation_results_n_10000.csv")

# Load the data
heatmap_sim_df <- read.csv(file.path(root, "simulation", "simulation_results_n_10000.csv"))

# Convert to long format for heatmaps
heatmap_sim_df_long <- melt(heatmap_sim_df, id.vars = c("True.Mean", "True.Variance"), 
                     measure.vars = c("Mean.Error", "Variance.Error"))

# Create separate heatmaps with adjusted legends
heatmap_mean <- ggplot(subset(heatmap_sim_df_long, variable == "Mean.Error"), aes(x = True.Mean, y = True.Variance, fill = value)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_viridis(option = "D", name = "error") +
  guides(fill = guide_colorbar(barheight = unit(0.25, "cm"))) +
  labs(title = "A", x = "True population mean", y = "True population variance", subtitle = "Simulated recovery of average mental health in groups of increasing size") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

heatmap_variance <- ggplot(subset(heatmap_sim_df_long, variable == "Variance.Error"), aes(x = True.Mean, y = True.Variance, fill = value)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_viridis(option = "D", name = NULL) +
  guides(fill = guide_colorbar(barheight = unit(0.25, "cm"))) +
  labs(subtitle = " ", x = "True population mean", y = "True population variance") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

simulation_results <- read.csv(file.path(root, "simulation/simulation_results_n_variable.csv"))
simulation_results$NRMSE = round(sqrt(simulation_results$MSE), digits = 2)
# Convert Num.People to numeric (for log scale)
simulation_results$Num.People <- as.numeric(simulation_results$Num.People)

# Reshape data for faceting
simulation_long <- simulation_results %>%
  select(Num.People, NRMSE, Mean.Error, Variance.Error, Mean.Correlation, Variance.Correlation) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Mean.Error, Variance.Error, Mean.Correlation, Variance.Correlation), 
               names_to = "Metric", values_to = "Value")

# Define facet labels
facet_labels <- c(
  "Mean.Error" = "Mean recovery error",
  "Variance.Error" = "Variance recovery error",
  "Mean.Correlation" = "True vs recovered correlation: mean",
  "Variance.Correlation" = "True vs recovered correlation: variance"
)

simulation_long$NRMSE = factor(simulation_long$NRMSE)
custom_greys <- rev(colorRampPalette(c("black", "grey70"))(length(unique(simulation_long$NRMSE))))
names(custom_greys) <- levels(simulation_long$NRMSE)

mean_error_group <- ggplot(subset(simulation_long, Metric %in% c("Mean.Correlation", "Mean.Error")), aes(x = Num.People, y = Value, color = as.factor(NRMSE))) +
  geom_line(size = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = 1) +
  scale_x_log10(breaks = scales::log_breaks(base = 10)) +
  facet_wrap(~Metric, scales = "free_y", labeller = labeller(Metric = facet_labels), nrow = 1) +
  labs(x = "Number of People (log scale)", 
       y = "Value", 
       color = 'nrmse (participant-level)', 
       title = "A", subtitle = "Simulated recovery of average mental health in groups of increasing size") +
  scale_color_manual(values = custom_greys) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(legend.position = 'right')

variance_error_group <- ggplot(subset(simulation_long, Metric %in% c("Variance.Correlation", "Variance.Error")), aes(x = Num.People, y = Value, color = as.factor(NRMSE))) +
  geom_line(size = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = 1) +
  scale_x_log10(breaks = scales::log_breaks(base = 10)) +
  facet_wrap(~Metric, scales = "free_y", labeller = labeller(Metric = facet_labels), nrow = 1) +
  labs(x = "Number of People (log scale)", 
       y = "Value", 
       color = 'nrmse (participant-level)', 
       title = "B", subtitle = "Simulated recovery of mental health variance in groups of increasing size") +
  scale_color_manual(values = custom_greys) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(legend.position = 'none')

df_daily <- read.csv(file.path(root, "public_mental_health", "daily_by_question.csv"))
df_daily$Day <- as.Date(df_daily$Day)

df_long <- df_daily %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(True_Mean, Pred_Mean), names_to = "Type", values_to = "Mean") %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(True_SEM, Pred_SEM), names_to = "SEM_Type", values_to = "SEM") %>%
  filter((Type == "True_Mean" & SEM_Type == "True_SEM") | (Type == "Pred_Mean" & SEM_Type == "Pred_SEM")) %>%
  mutate(Type = recode(Type, "True_Mean" = "True", "Pred_Mean" = "Predicted"))

##
## Plot selected timecourses
##
selected_dimensions <- c("Overall Distress", "Overall Wellbeing")  # <-- adjust names to match your data exactly

df_long_selected <- df_long %>%
  filter(Dimension %in% selected_dimensions)

p_daily_dots <- ggplot(df_long_selected, aes(x = as.factor(Day), y = Mean, color = Type)) +
  geom_point(position = position_dodge(width = 0.5), size = 1) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = Mean - SEM, ymax = Mean + SEM),
                width = 0.3, position = position_dodge(width = 0.5)) +
  facet_wrap(~ Dimension, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("True" = 'black', "Predicted" = 'grey70')) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  labs(x = "Day", y = "Score", color = NULL,
       subtitle = "B") +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)  # Optional: tilt x labels
  )

##
## Correlation
##
# Ensure factor levels match ordered_standard_dimensions
df_daily$Dimension = str_replace_all(df_daily$Dimension, "\\-", " ")
df_daily$Dimension <- factor(df_daily$Dimension, levels = ordered_standard_dimensions)

# Plot
p_scatter_daily_global <- ggplot(df_daily, aes(x = True_Mean, y = Pred_Mean, color = Dimension)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 1) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, linetype = "dotted", size = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~ Dimension, scales = "free", nrow = 3) +  # <<< changed this line to ncol = 1
  scale_color_manual(values = custom_colors) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  labs(x = "True weekly mean", 
       y = "Predicted weekly mean", 
       subtitle = "C") +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.7),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = base_font_size * 0.7),
    strip.text = element_text(size = base_font_size),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

lay1 <- rbind(c(1), c(2))
grid.arrange(mean_error_group, variance_error_group, layout_matrix = lay1, heights = c(1,1))
```

**Figure S18. Simulated recovery of population-level mental health.** To assess whether human–computer interactions encode information about group-level mental health, we simulated populations of group size N, each defined by a specific mean and variance of a ground truth mental-health feature. We then simulated MAILA's predictions, with noise levels defined by normalized mean squared errors (e) ranging from 17% to 35% (matching MAILA's prediction errors). The resulting mental health predictions were clipped to the unit interval to ensure they remained within the bounds of mental health scores. We then recovered the population means (**A**) and variances (**B**) from the noisy participant-level predictions and compared them to the ground truth of the simulated populations. The law of large numbers predicts that accuracy improves as a function of population size and the inverse of the prediction error. 

**A. Recovery of the group-level mean.** The left panel shows the correlation between the true and recovered population means. The right panel presents the corresponding absolute recovery error. The x-axis represents population size on a logarithmic scale, illustrating how larger sample sizes improve group-level accuracy. Shades of grey represent different error levels, which were informed by the range of prediction errors observed for items, dimensions, and global scores of distress and wellbeing in the MAILA dataset.

**B. Recovery of the group-level variance.** The left panel shows the correlation between the true and recovered population variances. The right panel presents the corresponding absolute error when recovering the variance. 


\newpage
## Figure S19

```{r}
#| label: SF19_correlation_mila_ground_truth
#| include: true
#| out.width: "100%"

lay_combined = rbind(c(1,2))
grid.arrange(p_corr_structure, scatter_corr_structure,
             #p_corr_structure_change, scatter_corr_structure_change, 
  layout_matrix = lay_combined,
  heights = c(1),
  widths = c(1.5,1)
)
```

**Figure S19. Overlapping correlation structure between MAILA and the ground truth.**

**A. MAILA recovers the correlation structure of ground-truth mental health.** Heatmaps show pairwise correlations among mental-health dimensions in the ground truth (left) and in MAILA's predictions (right). Although MAILA was trained with separate support-vector regressions for each dimension, its predictions closely reproduced the correlation structure of the true self-reports (R = `r STAT.cor_result_structure$estimate`, p = `r STAT.cor_result_structure$p.value`). Correlation coefficients deviated from the ground-truth structure by only `r 100*STAT.rmse_corr_structure_error`% of the possible range (p = `r STAT.rmse_corr_structure_pvalue`). This indicates that human–computer interactions encode shared latent dimensions of mental health.

**B. Agreement between ground-truth and MAILA correlations.** Scatter plot comparing the pairwise correlation coefficients between mental-health dimensions derived from ground-truth data (x-axis) and MAILA's predictions (y-axis). Each point represents one unique pair of dimensions (e.g., anxiety ~ depression), colored by the corresponding ground-truth correlation strength. The diagonal marks perfect agreement. Points for negative ground-truth correlations lie mostly below the diagonal and positive ones mostly above, indicating that MAILA tends to overestimate the magnitude of inter-dimensional correlations overall (paired t-test on |r|: p = `r STAT.abs_corr_ttest$p.value`).

<!-- **C. MAILA reproduces the correlation structure of changes in mental health.** Heatmaps show pairwise correlations among changes in mental-health dimensions (follow-up relative to baseline) for the ground truth (left) and MAILA predictions (right). MAILA’s estimates of within-person change captured the inter-dimensional dependency structure of true longitudinal changes (R = `r STAT.cor_result_structure_change$estimate`, p = `r STAT.cor_result_structure_change$p.value`, deviation from the ground truth structure: e = `r 100*STAT.nrmse_corr_structure_error_change`%, p = `r STAT.nrmse_corr_structure_pvalue_change`), suggesting that shared latent trajectories of mental health are encoded in human–computer interactions. -->

<!-- **D. Agreement between ground-truth and MAILA correlations for changes in mental health.** Scatter plot comparing the pairwise correlation coefficients between changes in mental-health dimensions computed from the ground truth (x-axis) and from MAILA's predictions (y-axis). Each point represents one pair of dimensions, colored by the ground-truth correlation strength. The diagonal marks perfect agreement. Negative ground-truth correlations tend to lie below and positive ones above the diagonal, again indicating that MAILA slightly overestimates inter-dimensional dependencies in changes (paired t-test on |r|: p = `r STAT.abs_corr_ttest_change$p.value`). -->

\newpage
## Figure S20

```{r}
#| label: SF20_interpreting_MAILA
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| fig.align: "center"
#| out.width: "100%"

lay1 <- rbind(c(1,2), c(1,3))
grid.arrange(loadings_of_features, features_within_groups, p_error_space, layout_matrix = lay1, heights = c(3,1.5), widths = c(2.65,1))
```

**Figure S20. Interpreting MAILA.** Like many data-driven models, MAILA learns predictive features that do not have immediate verbal interpretations. To explain its performance in terms of intuitive descriptors, we computed `r length(movement_features)` established metrics of cursor and touchscreen activity and regressed them onto MAILA’s predictions. 

**A. Associations between handcrafted movement features and MAILA’s predictions in the general population.** We correlated participant-level handcrafted movement features with MAILA’s predictions, shown here for overall distress in blue and overall wellbeing in green. Markers show standardized regression coefficients estimated separately for cursor- and touch-based datasets (circles vs. squares), with horizontal bars indicating 95% confidence intervals across datasets. Across modalities, higher distress and lower wellbeing were associated with more tortuous trajectories and greater variability in speed, whereas higher path efficiency predicted greater wellbeing and lower distress. Despite these broad consistencies, several handcrafted metrics showed substantial cross-modal heterogeneity: only `r prop_same_sign * 100`% of features loaded onto predicted mental health in the same direction across cursor and touchscreen data.

**B. Expression of features in self-identifying versus general participants.** For each population (participants who did or did not self-identify with depression and OCD), handcrafted features were grouped according to whether they loaded positively or negatively on predicted wellbeing or distress. Mean z-scored feature values (±95% CI) are plotted for positively loading (upward arrow) and negatively loading (downward arrow) feature groups. In the general population, features associated with lower distress and higher wellbeing were more strongly expressed. In participants who self-identified with depression or OCD, this pattern reversed for both distress (p = `r STAT.mdd_ocd_pos_Distress$coefficients[2,4]`) and wellbeing (p = `r STAT.mdd_ocd_neg_Wellbeing$coefficients[2,4]`), indicating that interpretable aspects of human–computer interaction systematically tracked higher distress and lower wellbeing in people who endorsed living with mental illness.

**C. Predictive advantage of MAILA over handcrafted feature models.** MAILA outperformed models built from handcrafted features across all benchmarks. In the original symptom space, MAILA achieved lower prediction errors for inter-individual differences in mental health in the general population (p = `r STAT.error_mila_handcrafted_original$p.value`). Along PC1-3, which capture the level and specific causes of distress and wellbeing (**Figure 4**), MAILA also outperformed handcrafted models (p = `r STAT.error_mila_handcrafted_pca$p.value`). Together, these results demonstrate that MAILA provides more accurate and specific predictions of mental health than models based solely on handcrafted metrics.


\newpage
## Figure S21

```{r}
#| label: plots_information_loss
#| echo: false
#| include: true


library(tidyverse)
library(scales)

# --- Load data ---
cursor_within_dropout <- read.csv(file.path(root, "corrupt", "cursor_within_dropout_original.csv"))
cursor_corrupt_embedding <- read.csv(file.path(root, "corrupt", "cursor_corrupted_embedding_original.csv"))
cursor_limited_training <- read.csv(file.path(root, "corrupt", "cursor_limited_training_original.csv"))
cursor_limited_clusters <- read.csv(file.path(root, "corrupt", "cursor_limited_clusters_original.csv"))

# --- Add method labels ---
cursor_within_dropout$Method <- "Limited test data"
cursor_corrupt_embedding$Method <- "Noise injection"
cursor_limited_training$Method <- "Limited training"
cursor_limited_clusters$Method <- "Limited clusters"

# --- Combine ---
combined_cursor_data <- bind_rows(cursor_within_dropout, cursor_corrupt_embedding, cursor_limited_training, cursor_limited_clusters)
combined_cursor_data$input = "cursor" 

# --- Load data ---
touch_draw_within_dropout <- read.csv(file.path(root, "corrupt", "touch_draw_within_dropout_original.csv"))
touch_draw_corrupt_embedding <- read.csv(file.path(root, "corrupt", "touch_draw_corrupted_embedding_original.csv"))
touch_draw_limited_training <- read.csv(file.path(root, "corrupt", "touch_draw_limited_training_original.csv"))
touch_draw_limited_clusters <- read.csv(file.path(root, "corrupt", "touch_draw_limited_clusters_original.csv"))

# --- Add method labels ---
touch_draw_within_dropout$Method <- "Limited test data"
touch_draw_corrupt_embedding$Method <- "Noise injection"
touch_draw_limited_training$Method <- "Limited training"
touch_draw_limited_clusters$Method <- "Limited clusters"

# --- Combine ---
combined_touch_draw_data <- bind_rows(touch_draw_within_dropout, touch_draw_corrupt_embedding, touch_draw_limited_training, touch_draw_limited_clusters)
combined_touch_draw_data$input = "touch_draw"

# --- Load data ---
touch_quest_within_dropout <- read.csv(file.path(root, "corrupt", "touch_quest_within_dropout_original.csv"))
touch_quest_corrupt_embedding <- read.csv(file.path(root, "corrupt", "touch_quest_corrupted_embedding_original.csv"))
touch_quest_limited_training <- read.csv(file.path(root, "corrupt", "touch_quest_limited_training_original.csv"))
touch_quest_limited_clusters <- read.csv(file.path(root, "corrupt", "touch_quest_limited_clusters_original.csv"))

# --- Add method labels ---
touch_quest_within_dropout$Method <- "Limited test data"
touch_quest_corrupt_embedding$Method <- "Noise injection"
touch_quest_limited_training$Method <- "Limited training"
touch_quest_limited_clusters$Method <- "Limited clusters"

# --- Combine ---
combined_touch_quest_data <- bind_rows(touch_quest_within_dropout, touch_quest_corrupt_embedding, touch_quest_limited_training, touch_quest_limited_clusters)
combined_touch_quest_data$input = "touch_quest"


combined_data = bind_rows(combined_cursor_data, combined_touch_draw_data, combined_touch_quest_data)
combined_data$Dimension <- str_replace_all(combined_data$Dimension, "\\-", " ")
combined_data$Dimension      <- factor(combined_data$Dimension, levels = ordered_standard_dimensions)
# --- Convert to long format ---
simulation_long <- combined_data %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(Spearman_Rho, MSE),
    names_to = "Metric",
    values_to = "Value"
  )


simulation_long_avg = ddply(subset(simulation_long, Metric == "Spearman_Rho"), 
                            .(Dimension, Method, Corruption_Level), summarise,
                     average = mean_ci_95(Value)$mean,
                     error = mean_ci_95(Value)$error)

subset_data <- subset(pca_perm_rho_long, Dimension %in% c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3"))
upper_pct <- quantile(subset_data$Value, probs = 0.999, na.rm = TRUE)
upper_pct_5 <- quantile(subset_data$Value, probs = 0.950, na.rm = TRUE)

simulation_long_avg <- simulation_long_avg %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Signal = (1 - Corruption_Level) * 100   # 0-100% signal retained
  )

p_information_loss <- ggplot(
  simulation_long_avg, 
  aes(x = Signal, y = average, color = Dimension)
) +
  # per-dimension lines + points
  geom_line(size = 0.25, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = 1, alpha = 0.5, shape = 1) +

  # LOESS smooth across all dimensions within each Method facet
  geom_smooth(
    aes(x = Signal, y = average),
    method = "loess",
    se = TRUE,
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 0.4,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +

  facet_wrap(~ Method, nrow = 1) +

  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0, 
    linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Signal retained (%)",
    y = "R",
    color = NULL,
    subtitle = "MAILA is robust across preprocessing, training and test parameters"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    trans  = "sqrt",                    # try "sqrt" first; stronger: custom 1/3, 1/4, ...
    limits = c(0, 100),
    breaks = c(0, 25, 50, 75, 100),
    expand = c(0, 0)
  )  +
  scale_color_manual(values = custom_colors) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.spacing.y = unit(0.2, "lines"),
    legend.key.height = unit(0.2, "lines"),
    legend.key.width  = unit(0.4, "lines"),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", linewidth = 0.2),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(1, "lines")
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: SF21_corrupting_MAILA
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| fig.align: "center"
#| out.width: "100%"

p_information_loss
```

**Figure S21. Robustness of MAILA to information loss.** We systematically degraded MAILA in four ways (from left to right panel): (i) we limited the number of K-means clusters used to construct the movement feature matrix $\mathrm{X^{N \times C}}$, simulating reduced behavioral diversity; (ii) we reduced the amount of human–computer interaction available per participant in the test folds by removing contiguous segments from each trajectory, simulating inferences from shorter cursor or touch recordings; (iii) we subsampled the number of participants in the training folds, simulating the effect of smaller calibration datasets; (iv) we corrupted cursor/touch trajectories by linearly mixing true samples with random values drawn from a uniform distribution. In all cases, correlations declined smoothly with increasing degradation, indicating predictable performance loss under impoverished behavior, limited test data, limited training data, or injected noise.

\newpage
## Table S1
```{r}
#| label: ST1_print_questionnaire_summary_2
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
#| out.width: "100%"

library(dplyr)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(purrr)
library(readr)

# --- Load data ---
questions_df <- read.csv("/Users/veithweilnhammer/Github/cursor_mind/mental_health_matrix/mental_health_questions_table.csv")
results_quest <- read.csv("/Users/veithweilnhammer/Github/cursor_mind/mental_health_matrix/mental_health_responses_table.csv")

enhanced_results      <- read.csv("/Users/veithweilnhammer/Github/cursor_mind/regression/enhanced_regression_results.csv")
generalization_followup <- read.csv("/Users/veithweilnhammer/Github/cursor_mind/generalization/retest/original/enhanced_regression_results.csv")
generalization_survey   <- read.csv("/Users/veithweilnhammer/Github/cursor_mind/generalization/questionnaire/original/enhanced_regression_results.csv")
generalization_game     <- read.csv("/Users/veithweilnhammer/Github/cursor_mind/generalization/beads/original/enhanced_regression_results.csv")

touch_quest <- read.csv("/Users/veithweilnhammer/Github/cursor_mind/touch_regression/general/quest/original/regression_results.csv")
touch_draw  <- read.csv("/Users/veithweilnhammer/Github/cursor_mind/touch_regression/general/draw/original/regression_results.csv")

# --- Select item responses (1-67 only) ---
results_items   <- results_quest[, 2:68]
questions_items <- questions_df[1:ncol(results_items), ]

# --- Compute summary stats per item ---
summary_stats_items <- purrr::map_dfr(seq_len(ncol(results_items)), function(i) {
  x  <- results_items[[i]]
  ci <- mean_ci_95(x)
  tibble(Mean = ci$mean, CI = ci$error, IQR = IQR(x, na.rm = TRUE))
})

# --- Combine metadata + stats ---
questions_items_summary <- questions_items %>%
  bind_cols(summary_stats_items) %>%
  mutate(
    Index   = question_ID,
    Summary = sprintf("%.2f ± %.2f (%.2f)", Mean, CI, IQR)
  ) %>%
  arrange(Questionnaire, Dimension, Index)

# --- Table core (Index, Question, Summary) ---
questions_print_items <- questions_items_summary %>%
  select(Index, Question, `Mean ± 95% CI (IQR)` = Summary)

# --- Grouping index for pack_rows ---
group_index <- dplyr::count(
  questions_items_summary,
  Questionnaire, Dimension,
  name = "n"
) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    group = dplyr::if_else(
      Questionnaire == "BSI",
      paste("Distress -", Dimension),
      paste("Wellbeing -", Dimension)
    )
  )
pack_index <- stats::setNames(group_index$n, group_index$group)


# --- Helper to robustly extract R (handles R or Pearson_r) ---
pick_R <- function(df, newname) {
  # choose the first present correlation column
  rcol <- intersect(c("R", "Pearson_r"), names(df))
  if (length(rcol) == 0) stop("Neither 'R' nor 'Pearson_r' found in the data frame.")
  rcol <- rcol[1]

  out <- df %>%
    dplyr::transmute(Index = question_ID, value = .data[[rcol]])
  names(out)[2] <- newname
  out
}

# --- Build R columns for all datasets (note: use your actual objects) ---
R_baseline <- pick_R(enhanced_results,        "baseline")
R_followup <- pick_R(generalization_followup, "follow_up")
R_survey   <- pick_R(generalization_survey,   "survey")
R_game     <- pick_R(generalization_game,     "game")
R_touch_q  <- pick_R(touch_quest,             "touch_quest")
R_touch_d  <- pick_R(touch_draw,              "touch_draw")

# --- Merge into table ---
table_items <- questions_print_items %>%
  dplyr::left_join(R_baseline, by = "Index") %>%
  dplyr::left_join(R_followup, by = "Index") %>%
  dplyr::left_join(R_survey,   by = "Index") %>%
  dplyr::left_join(R_game,     by = "Index") %>%
  dplyr::left_join(R_touch_q,  by = "Index") %>%
  dplyr::left_join(R_touch_d,  by = "Index")

# Optional: clean questionnaire tags in item text
table_items$Question <- stringr::str_replace_all(
  table_items$Question,
  c("BSI" = "Distress", "MHC-SF" = "Wellbeing")
)

# --- Print LaTeX table: correlations only (no Index), compact mean col, wide question ---
# Columns: Question | Mean ± 95% CI (IQR) | baseline | follow-up | survey | game | touch-quest | touch-draw
kable(
  table_items %>% select(-Index),
  booktabs = TRUE,
  longtable = TRUE,
  align = "l",
  col.names = c("Question", "Mean ± 95% CI (IQR)", "baseline", "follow-up",
                "survey", "game", "touch-q", "touch-d")
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    latex_options = c("striped", "hold_position", "repeat_header"),
    font_size = 6
  ) %>%
  # widen Question, shrink Mean±CI(IQR), keep R columns compact
  column_spec(1, width = "5cm") %>%
  column_spec(2, width = "2.4cm") %>%
  column_spec(3, width = "0.9cm") %>%
  column_spec(4, width = "0.9cm") %>%
  column_spec(5, width = "0.9cm") %>%
  column_spec(6, width = "0.9cm") %>%
  column_spec(7, width = "0.9cm") %>%  # touch labels slightly longer
  column_spec(8, width = "0.9cm") %>%
  add_header_above(c(" " = 2, "MAILA's correlation to ground truth (R)" = 6), bold = TRUE) %>%
  pack_rows(index = pack_index)
```

**Table S1. Predicting mental health from human–computer interactions.** For each questionnaire item, the average score is reported as mean ± 95% confidence interval (inter-quartile range). To the right, Spearman correlations (R) indicate the correspondence between predicted and true scores in the calibration cursor dataset (baseline, 5-fold cross-validation), follow-up data, an independent non-mental-health survey, and a gamified decision-making task. All generalization models were trained on the baseline dataset and applied without retraining to the respective target data. The final two columns show correlations from two touch-based tasks, interface interaction (touch-q) and free-form drawing (touch-d), each evaluated using 5-fold cross-validation. 

\newpage
## Table S2

```{r}
#| label: ST2_print_non_mh_summary
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
library(dplyr)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(stringr)

# Load within-dataset results
generalization_survey_within <- read.csv(
  "/Users/veithweilnhammer/Github/cursor_mind/generalization/questionnaire/within/enhanced_regression_results.csv"
)

generalization_survey_within$Question <- 
  gsub("^How much", "To what extent", generalization_survey_within$Question)

# Build table rows (drop any composite rows if present)
survey_tbl <- generalization_survey_within %>%
  dplyr::filter(Questionnaire != "Composite") %>%
  dplyr::transmute(
    Dimension = as.character(Dimension),
    Question  = Question,
    r         = Real_Rho
  )

survey_tbl$Dimension <- recode(
  survey_tbl$Dimension,
  "body_awareness"  = "Body Awareness",
  "decision_making" = "Decision Making",
  "head_impact"     = "Head Impact",
  "motor_control"   = "Motor Control",
  "Civic"           = "Civic",
  "Cognition"       = "Cognition",
  "Economy"         = "Economy",
  "Politics"        = "Politics",
  "Society"         = "Society",
  "Technology"      = "Technology",
  "Values"          = "Values"
)

# Ensure the rows are in the order you want to print:
survey_tbl <- survey_tbl %>%
  # Example ordering: by Dimension then by Question text
  arrange(Dimension, Question)

# Build pack_rows index in the *exact printed order*
rl <- rle(survey_tbl$Dimension)
pack_index <- stats::setNames(rl$lengths, rl$values)


# Print table: Question | r, grouped by Dimension
kable(
  survey_tbl %>% select(-Dimension),
  booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, align = "l",
  col.names = c("Question", "Ground truth correlation (R)")
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    latex_options = c("striped", "hold_position", "repeat_header"),
    font_size = 6
  ) %>%
  column_spec(1, width = "12.5cm") %>%  # wide question text
  column_spec(2, width = "2cm") %>%   # compact r column
  pack_rows(index = pack_index)
```

**Table S2. Predicting responses to non-mental health items from human–computer interactions.** For each question from the non-psychological survey and gamified task, the table reports the Spearman correlation coefficient (R) between predicted and true item scores obtained within each dataset using 5-fold cross-validation. Dimensions correspond to thematic categories of items. Each group header indicates the corresponding dimension, and individual rows list the specific items within that domain. MAILA failed to predict the responses to the non-mental health survey (R = `r mean_ci_95(subset(gen_survey_within_regression_data, Questionnaire == "Control")$Real_Rho)$mean` ± `r mean_ci_95(subset(gen_survey_within_regression_data, Questionnaire == "Control")$Real_Rho)$error`, p = `r STAT.prediction_within_survey$p.value`), suggesting that cursor movements capture dynamic mental states associated with psychological distress and wellbeing, but not more stable self-assessments of abilities, attitudes, or beliefs, or response artifacts induced by our interface. 

\newpage
## Table S3

```{r}
#| label: ST3_print_non_mh_summary
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
library(dplyr)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(stringr)

# Load within-dataset results
free_form_prompts <- read_excel(
  "/Users/veithweilnhammer/Github/cursor_mind/experiments/prompts_for_touch_draw/drawing_conditions_3b.xlsx"
)

# Tidy + order
tbl_draw <- free_form_prompts %>%
  transmute(
    Dimension = str_to_title(Dimension),   # "objects" -> "Objects"
    Prompt    = Prompt,
    order     = drawing_ID                 # keep order internal, not printed
  ) %>%
  arrange(Dimension, order) %>%
  select(-order)

# Build pack_rows index in exact print order
rl <- rle(tbl_draw$Dimension)
pack_index <- stats::setNames(rl$lengths, rl$values)

# Print table: Prompt only, grouped by Dimension
kable(
  tbl_draw %>% select(Prompt),
  booktabs  = TRUE,
  longtable = TRUE,
  align     = "l",
  col.names = c("Prompt: Draw …")
) %>%
  kable_styling(
    latex_options = c("striped", "hold_position", "repeat_header"),
    font_size     = 6
  ) %>%
  column_spec(1, width = "14.7cm") %>%   # single wide column
  pack_rows(index = pack_index)
```

**Table S3. Predicting mental health from free-form digital behavior.** Participants were instructed to draw each prompt using a touchscreen interface. For readability, prompts are grouped by their underlying semantic category (e.g., objects, digits). Each prompt was shown once in random order across participants.

\newpage

## Table S4

| Feature | Operational definition | Implementation notes | Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Average speed | Mean cursor/touch speed across samples. | Speed computed from successive differences in $(x_t,y_t)$ with $\Delta t=1$; $s_t=\sqrt{v_x^2+v_y^2}$. | Per segment; then averaged within participant |
| Speed kurtosis | Kurtosis of the segment speed distribution. | Computed on $\{s_t\}$. | Per segment; then averaged within participant |
| Jerk (speed change magnitude) | Mean absolute change in speed over time. | Discrete acceleration $a_t=\Delta s_t/\Delta t$; jerk is $\mathrm{mean}(|a_t|)$ (with $\Delta t=1$). | Per segment; then averaged within participant |
| Movement area | Bounding-box area covered by the trajectory. | $(\max x-\min x)\times(\max y-\min y)$. | Per segment; then averaged within participant |
| Relative idle time | Fraction of samples with speed below a stationary threshold. | $\mathrm{mean}(s_t<\tau)$ with $\tau=0.001$ (screen-normalized units/sample). | Per segment; then averaged within participant |
| Path efficiency | Straight-line displacement divided by total path length. | Straight-line distance between first and last point divided by cumulative path length (0 if path length = 0). | Per segment; then averaged within participant |
| Average turn angle | Mean absolute change in heading direction. | Heading $\theta_t=\mathrm{atan2}(v_y,v_x)$; angle changes $\Delta\theta_t$ wrapped to $(-\pi,\pi]$; report $\mathrm{mean}(|\Delta\theta_t|)$. | Per segment; then averaged within participant |
| Tortuosity | Total path length divided by straight-line displacement. | Path length / straight-line distance (defined only if straight-line distance $>0$; else 0). | Per segment; then averaged within participant |
| Turn rate per distance | Mean absolute turning per unit distance traveled. | Average turn angle divided by total path length (0 if path length = 0). | Per segment; then averaged within participant |
| Horizontal–vertical bias | Difference between horizontal and vertical motion magnitude. | $\mathbb{E}(|v_x|)-\mathbb{E}(|v_y|)$; positive values indicate more horizontal motion. | Per segment; then averaged within participant |
| Speed entropy (spectral entropy) | Entropy of the normalized power spectrum of the speed trace. | FFT-based: compute power spectrum of $\{s_t\}$, normalize to probabilities, then compute entropy. | Per segment; then averaged within participant |
| Speed fluctuation rate | Rate of sign changes in speed around its mean. | Count zero-crossings of $s_t-\overline{s}$ (sign changes relative to mean speed). | Per segment; then averaged within participant |

**Table S4. Definitions of human-interpretable cursor/touch features.** The table defines the 12 handcrafted movement features used to interpret MAILA predictions. Features were computed for each trajectory segment and then averaged across segments within participant to obtain one participant-level feature vector; features were z-scored across participants prior to regression analyses.

\newpage
## Table S5
```{r}
#| label: ST4_print_future_ai
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
# --- Load Excel ---
path <- "./References/FUTURE_AI.xlsx"   # adjust if needed
df <- read_excel(path)

# Make names easier to match
names(df) <- names(df) |>
  str_replace_all("\\.", "_") |>
  str_replace_all("\\s+", "_")

# Robustly pick columns (handles minor naming changes)
col_category <- grep("^category$", names(df), ignore.case = TRUE, value = TRUE)[1]
col_reco     <- grep("^recomm",   names(df), ignore.case = TRUE, value = TRUE)[1]
col_current  <- grep("^current",  names(df), ignore.case = TRUE, value = TRUE)[1]
col_future   <- grep("^future",   names(df), ignore.case = TRUE, value = TRUE)[1]

stopifnot(!is.na(col_category), !is.na(col_reco), !is.na(col_current), !is.na(col_future))

# Build printable table (Category only for grouping)
tbl <- df |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Category          = str_to_title(as.character(.data[[col_category]])),
    Recommendation    = as.character(.data[[col_reco]]),
    `Current efforts` = as.character(.data[[col_current]]),
    `Future priorities` = as.character(.data[[col_future]])
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(Category, Recommendation)

# pack_rows index in printed order
rl <- rle(tbl$Category)
pack_index <- stats::setNames(rl$lengths, rl$values)

# Print: Recommendation | Current | Future, grouped by Category
kable(
  tbl |> dplyr::select(-Category),
  booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, align = "l",
  col.names = c("Recommendation", "Current efforts", "Future priorities")
) |>
  kable_styling(
    latex_options = c("striped", "hold_position", "repeat_header"),
    font_size = 6
  ) |>
  # Tweak widths to match your style (wide left column)
  column_spec(1, width = "5cm") |>
  column_spec(2, width = "5cm") |>
  column_spec(3, width = "5cm") |>
  add_header_above(c(" " = 1, "Status" = 2), bold = TRUE) |>
  pack_rows(index = pack_index)
```

**Table S5. Recommendations for responsible and transparent use of AI in mental health research, aligned with the FUTURE-AI framework.** Each row summarizes a key recommendation grouped by overarching category. The table outlines how the FUTURE-AI principles are currently addressed and highlights proposed next steps for advancing best practices.

<!-- With people spending an average of 6-7 hours online each day [@noauthor_datareportal_nodate], mental health could, in principle, be monitored for nearly half of their waking life. -->
<!-- # Cover letter -->

<!-- A current challenge in mental health research is to develop methods that can generalize across contexts while remaining sensitive to individual differences. In the present work we report what we believe is one promising avenue toward this goal. Our framework for predicting mental health from cursor movements involves a combination of three ingredients: (1) the use of an autoencoder to compress raw trajectories into a low-dimensional representation, (2) training on datasets that span a series of related tasks and populations, and (3) mapping these latent features onto mental health outcomes using machine learning models. The key result, which emerges naturally from this setup rather than being specially engineered, is that the representations capture stable, person-specific signatures of digital behavior that generalize across tasks and contexts, enabling robust prediction of mental health states. -->

<!-- # Material -->

<!--  It enables people to infer the emotions, thoughts, and intentions of others in real-time, and expands their ability to interact beyond the limits of language. -->

<!-- . Today, digital devices have become second nature to most people, and many have used them their whole life [@noauthor_datareportal_nodate] -->

<!-- nature style abstract: Establishing a unified theory of cognition has been an important goal in psychology1,2. A first step towards such a theory is to create a computational model that can predict human behaviour in a wide range of settings. Here we introduce Centaur, a computational model that can predict and simulate human behaviour in any experiment expressible in natural language. We derived Centaur by fine-tuning a state-of-the-art language model on a large-scale dataset called Psych-101. Psych-101 has an unprecedented scale, covering trial-by-trial data from more than 60,000 participants performing in excess of 10,000,000 choices in 160 experiments. Centaur not only captures the behaviour of held-out participants better than existing cognitive models, but it also generalizes to previously unseen cover stories, structural task modifications and entirely new domains. Furthermore, the model’s internal representations become more aligned with human neural activity after fine-tuning. Taken together, our results demonstrate that it is possible to discover computational models that capture human behaviour across a wide range of domains. We believe that such models provide tremendous potential for guiding the development of cognitive theories, and we present a case study to demonstrate this -->

<!-- MAILA multiple dimensions of mental hleath, not only one [@gillan_smartphones_2021; @mirea_cognitive_2025] -->

<!-- test retest also lakcing in computational psychaitry, general importance of repeated measures ...  [@gillan_smartphones_2021] -->

<!-- rudledge, summerfield -->

<!-- Public mental helath figure: caveat that all these data are cross-sectional ... -->

<!-- A total of 25 trials in these seven adult field trial sites were successful in obtaining adequate samples that permitted reliable kappa estimates for 15 separate diagnoses. Three diagnoses were in the very good (kappa 0.60-0.79) range: PTSD, complex somatic symptom disorder, and major neurocognitive disorder. Seven were in the good (kappa 0.40-0.59) range: schizophrenia, schizoaffective disorder, bipolar I disorder, binge eating disorder, alcohol use disorder, mild neurocognitive disorder, and borderline personality disorder. Four diagnoses were in the questionable (kappa 0.20-0.39) range: major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, mild traumatic brain injury, and antisocial personality disorder. Finally, one newly proposed diagnosis, mixed anxiety-depressive disorder, was in the unacceptable (kappa \<0.20) range\[rieger_DMS-5_2013\]. -->
