<!-- some citation for digital behavior\ [@chandrasekaran_detecting_2024 -->

<!-- -   Predction with an accuracy beyond baseline expectation and even surpassing the performance of other gold-standard digital phenotypes -->

<!-- -   self-reported -->

<!-- A central goal in psychiatry is to develop scalable, real-time indicators of mental health. Traditional assessments — like questionnaires and clinical interviews — remain episodic, and difficult to administer at scale. -->

<!-- While the vocabulary of psychopathology offers a framework for organizing and communicating mental health, its cultural and historical roots constrain what can be seen, measured, and treated.  -->

<!-- Here, we show that digital behaviors encode robust signatures of mental health. We used representation learning and supervised regression to map cursor and touchscreen movements onto a multidimensional space of psychological distress and wellbeing. These behavioral embeddings enabled accurate predictions of global mental health scores, specific symptom dimensions (e.g., anxiety, depression), and responses to individual questionnaire items in 8k people recruited online. They also generalized across tasks and devices, inferred changes in mental health, and tracked user identity over time. -->

<!-- Our findings establish a dynamic and non-verbal behavioral phenotype of mental health that is ecologically valid, privacy-preserving, and scalable. These results have broad implications for building digital systems that adapt to human's mental states. Movement-based behavioral signals could inform the development of emotionally sensitive AI interfaces, fine-tune large language models to user affect, and enhance human verification, fraud detection, and risk prediction in fields ranging from healthcare to finance and cybersecurity. -->

<!-- This enables passive, continuous, and dynamic assessments that can power scalable mental health monitoring, adaptive interfaces, and digital phenotyping for precision medicine. -->

<!-- <!-- A central goal in psychiatry is to develop scalable, real-time indicators of mental health. Traditional assessments — like questionnaires and clinical interviews — remain episodic, subjective, and difficult to administer at scale. In contrast, passive digital behaviors offer a continuous and unobtrusive window into an individual’s internal state. Can such digital footprints be used to predict mental health? -->

<!-- <!-- We show that individuals occupy idiosyncratic, state-dependent locations in the space of cursor movements that map onto specific positions in the space of self-reported mental health. This mapping enables accurate, dynamic, and robust predictions across global distress and wellbeing, specific symptom dimensions, and individual items. These results demonstrate the feasibility of using passive behavioral signals for scalable mental health assessment and suggest novel digital phenotypes for precision psychiatry. -->

<!-- <!-- This approach is particularly promising in contexts where language-based assessments fall short—such as early in illness, during crisis, or when individuals are unable or unwilling to articulate their experiences. Our experimental design ensured that cursor movements were unrelated to response content and indistinguishable from typical computer use (Figure X; Figures SX1-SX2), making this method both ecologically valid and privacy-preserving. -->

<!-- A central goal in psychiatry is to develop scalable, real-time indicators of mental health. Traditional assessments — like questionnaires and clinical interviews — remain episodic, subjective, and difficult to administer at scale. Here, we show that digital behaviors encode robust signatures of mental health. We used representation learning and supervised regression to map cursor and touchscreen movements onto a multidimensional space of psychological distress and wellbeing. These behavioral embeddings enabled accurate predictions of global mental health scores, specific symptom dimensions (e.g., anxiety, depression), and responses to individual questionnaire items in 8k people recruited online. They also generalized across tasks and devices, inferred changes in mental health, and tracked user identity over time. -->

<!-- Our findings establish a dynamic and non-verbal behavioral phenotype of mental health that is ecologically valid, privacy-preserving, and scalable. These results have broad implications for building digital systems that adapt to human's mental states. Movement-based behavioral signals could inform the development of emotionally sensitive AI interfaces, fine-tune large language models to user affect, and enhance human verification, fraud detection, and risk prediction in fields ranging from healthcare to finance and cybersecurity. -->

<!-- This enables passive, continuous, and dynamic assessments that can power scalable mental health monitoring, adaptive interfaces, and digital phenotyping for precision medicine. -->

<!-- - Screening accuracy is typically assessed with the area under the curve ($\text{auc}$) of the receiver operating characteristic, which describes the trade-off between true and false positives. -->

<!-- - overcome the limitations of clinical assessments in terms of time, cost, accessibility, and sensitivity -->

<!-- - combination with other features can improve precision [@murray_could_2021] -->

<!-- - part of the evaluation in people who present with unspecific symptoms, e.g. at-risk mental state [@murray_could_2021]. -->

<!-- - MAILA’s ability to track the continuity of identity across sessions may support session-level authentication. This could help defend against device takeover attacks, in which one person uses another’s device, and Sybil attacks, where a single individual attempts to appear as multiple users. -->

<!-- The movement feature matrix defined each participant’s location in the space of movement as a summary of their behavior across a recording session (Figure 2). To assess the stability of these signatures, we compared mental health predictions from SVR model trained and tested on two non-overlapping cursor movement subsets, each comprising 50% of the available data per participant. Predictions from these subsets were highly correlated ($\overline{\rho}$ = `r mean(regression_stability$Correlation)` ± `r sd(regression_stability$Correlation)`, p = $\text{XYZ}$). Predictive cursor movements patterns thus remained consistent over an average tracking period of `r mean(datapoints_time$sum_positive_diffs, na.rm = TRUE)/60` ± `r sd(datapoints_time$sum_positive_diffs, na.rm = TRUE)/60` min. This suggest that cursor movements may reflect the continuity of identity — that is, signal whether a returning user is the same individual or someone else. -->

<!-- When tested on held-out data during 5-fold cross-validation, SVCs achieved an accuracy of 90% in determining whether two feature vectors, each built from 50% of a participant’s movement embeddings, originated from the same or different individuals. Even without explicit identifiers, cursor movements thus encode internal states that reflect and track the identity of users over time. While this capability could enhance safety through passive user authentication, it also raises important concerns about re-identification and user surveillance without consent. -->

<!-- derived from `r (max(ident_results$fold) - 1) / max(ident_results$fold) * 100`% of each participant’s embeddings and tested on the remaining `r 100 / max(ident_results$fold)`% of the same session. Each of the `r max(ident_results$fold)` cross-validation folds contained consecutive and non-overlapping subsets of each participant’s movement embeddings. Wihtin one session, we accurately identified `r 100*summary_ident_combined[summary_ident_combined$week == 0,]$av_acc`% of our participants (chance level = `r 100*(1/nrow(summary_ID_ident_combined[summary_ID_ident_combined$week == 0,]))`%; Figure S15C). To evaluate identification performance over time, we trained SVC classifiers on  tested how well models trained on all movement embeddings could identify the subset of `r summary_demographics[summary_demographics$sample == 2,]$n` participants who were tested a second time. Accuracy decreased over time, reaching `r 100*summary_ident_combined[summary_ident_combined$week == 8,]$av_acc`% for a test-retest interval of `r max(summary_ident_combined$weeks)` weeks (Figure SXA). The ability to predict mental health was independent of the ability to identify individuals from cursor movements (p = `r STAT.identification_to_regression$coefficients[2,5]`). -->

<!-- Together, these results indicate that the $N \times C$ movement feature matrix captured generalizable markers of mental state - features that are characteristic of mental health at a given movement - and do not reflect persistent traits or computer hardware. At a maximum identification performance of `r (max(ident_results$fold) - 1) / max(ident_results$fold) * 100`%, cursor movements reflect predictors of mental health that largely preserve user privacy in large groups. -->

<!-- ### Mental health screening and monitoring  -->

<!-- The vector-based approach captures continuous change in a multidimensional space of mental of mental health, where each location correspond to a specific constellation of psychological distress and wellbeing. Conventional clinical decision-making, by contrast, often relies on categorical classifications, where disorders do not evolve along a continuum, but emerge when symptoms surpass a threshold of distress from a defined set of symptoms. -->

<!-- In this context, at least three trajectories are of particular clinical relevance. The first are diagnostic transitions, where symptoms cross a diagnostic threshold, leading to the onset or resolution of a mental health condition such as depression. Cursor movements predicted the onset and resolution of high distress states, defined by transitions exceeding the `r unique(transition_results$Percentile)[1]`, at an average $\text{auc}$ of `r auc_results[auc_results$Dimension == "Overall Distress",]$AUC` (all p \< 0.001, see Figure 6A for predictions of diagnostic transitions on subdimensions of distress). Cursor-based predictions thus reliably captured whether participants crossed the boundary to and from high distress states. -->

<!-- The second clinically relevant trajectory is the development of comorbidity, such as the emergence of significant anxiety alongside preexisting depression. To assess whether movement-based predictions capture changes in comorbidity, we tested their ability to track increases and decreases in the number of symptom dimensions exceeding the distress threshold. Observed and predicted comorbidity changes were strongly correlated (R = `r comorbidity_corr$estimate`, p = `r mean(permuted_corrs$Permuted_Corr >= comorbidity_corr$estimate)`; Figure 6B). In addition to individual diagnostic transitions on isolated dimensions of mental health, cursor-based predictions thus captured comorbidity transitions across multiple dimensions. -->

<!-- The third important mental health trajectory is axis transitions, where symptoms redistribute across dimensions — such as a reduction in depressive symptoms accompanied by an increase in anxiety and a simultaneous decline in emotional wellbeing. These transitions can be quantified by entropy, a measure of symptom dispersion across dimensions. A shift from isolated symptoms to a broader distress pattern increases entropy, while the consolidation of symptoms into a singular dimension decreases entropy. Axis transitions are often observed during treatment and are critical for evaluating the functional impact of mental health interventions beyond the effect on a specific symptom. Entropy changes in self-reported mental health were significantly correlated to the entropy of the cursor-based predictions (R = `r entropy_corr$estimate`, p = `r perm_p_entropy`; Figure 6C). -->

<!-- A key strength of cursor tracking is its ability to provide continuous mental health predictions in real time without interrupting daily activities. This real-time capability is critical for early detection and intervention, as it enables immediate support when a person’s mental health state begins to deteriorate. Cursor-based predictions could therefore serve as a scalable, low-cost tool for real-time mental health monitoring in individuals who undergo intermittent clinical assessments, such as those enrolled in randomized controlled trials or early intervention programs. -->

<!-- Together, our results confirm that cursor movements encode approximate low-dimensional representations of the mental states that shape psychological distress and wellbeing. To understand the structure of these representations, we performed gradient ascent and descent in the $N \times C$ movement space to maximize or minimize predictions in the $N \times Q$ mental health space, and compared human-interpretable movement feature such as speed and idle time at the resulting endpoints. We found no significant differences across these features, suggesting that cursor movements predict mental health states through complex and multidimensional signals rather than through isolated human-centered descriptions of behavior. -->

<!-- Just as we do not need to have seen every possible movement to infer its meaning, we do not require exhaustive experience with all mental states to recognize their expression in others.  -->

<!-- This approach is particularly promising in contexts where language-based assessments fall short—such as early in illness, during crisis, or when individuals are unable or unwilling to articulate their experiences. Our experimental design ensured that cursor movements were unrelated to response content and indistinguishable from typical computer use (Figure X; Figures SX1-SX2), making this method both ecologically valid and privacy-preserving. -->

<!-- **Background.** Scalable and real-time assessments of mental health remain a major challenge in psychiatry. Traditional language-based assessments are episodic and difficult to deploy at scale.  -->

<!-- **Objective.** We investigated whether cursor movements — ubiquitous byproducts of every-day computer use — can serve as dynamic, dimensional, and generalizable indicators of mental health. -->

<!-- **Methods.** We recorded cursor movements in XYZ participants and transformed them into low-dimensional feature vectors using a pretrained LSTM autoencoder and K-means clustering. We trained machine learning models to predict responses to 67 self-reported mental health items, spanning global distress and wellbeing, symptom dimensions, and individual items. We further explored the geometric alignment between behavioral and psychological spaces and tracked mental health changes across time and dimensions. -->

<!-- **Results.** Cursor movements predicted global distress and wellbeing (ρ = .16 and .11), as well as 10/10 symptom dimensions and 45/69 individual items. Predictions generalized across participants and time, tracked meaningful changes in mental health (ρ = .62 for distress), and captured diagnostic transitions, comorbidity, and symptom redistribution. The structure of cursor behavior aligned with the latent structure of mental health, supporting its role as a digital endophenotype. Distinct cursor movement subspaces reflected behavioral subtypes with different symptom trajectories. -->

<!-- **Conclusions.** Cursor movements encode interpretable, continuous representations of mental health that can be used to identify at-risk individuals, monitor symptom change, and support behavioral subtyping. This work introduces a scalable, low-cost, and privacy-preserving tool for digital mental health and precision psychiatry. -->

<!-- ## Movement as an endophenotype of mental health -->

<!-- The human-defined constructs used in mental health, such as depression and anxiety, are inherently heterogeneous. They are binary labels inspired by other medical conditions, which are often classified based on the presence or absence of a pathogenic agent. For example, an infectious disease like tuberculosis is diagnosed by detecting mycobacterium tuberculosis - either the bacterium is present, confirming the disease, or it is absent, ruling it out. However, in psychiatry, no such causal markers exist. Instead, diagnoses are shaped by language, historical definitions, and cultural factors. As a result, individuals with the same diagnosis can present with vastly different symptom profiles, trajectories, and treatment responses. Efforts to refine these constructs often seek to uncover alternative maps of mental health — continuous representations that reveal latent states and diagnostic subtypes not immediately apparent through conventional assessment. -->


```{r}
#| label: SF7_human_movement_matrix
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| fig.align: "center"
#| fig.width: 12
#| fig.height: 13
#| cache: true

# Define file path
movement_matrix_path <- file.path(root, "movement_matrix", "human_movement_feature_matrix.csv")

# Load data
movement_matrix <- read.csv(movement_matrix_path)

# Convert Cluster to factor for faceting
movement_matrix$Cluster <- as.factor(movement_matrix$Cluster)

# Convert data to long format for plotting
movement_long <- movement_matrix %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -c(Cluster, Movement_Index), names_to = "Feature", values_to = "Value")

# Compute mean and standard deviation per feature within each cluster using ddply
movement_summary <- ddply(movement_long, .(Cluster, Feature), summarise,
                          mean = mean(Value, na.rm = TRUE),
                          sd = sd(Value, na.rm = TRUE))

movement_summary$Feature = gsub("_", " ", movement_summary$Feature)

# Plot movement feature distributions across clusters
p_movement_features <- ggplot(movement_summary, aes(x = Cluster, y = mean, group = Feature)) +
  
  # Points for mean values
  geom_point(size = 1.2, alpha = 0.8) +
  
  # Error bars for standard deviation
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean - sd, ymax = mean + sd), width = 0.2, alpha = 0.5) +
  
  # Facet by Feature
  facet_wrap(~ Feature, scales = "free", ncol = 1) +
  
  # Color by Feature
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "D") +
  
  # Theme settings
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x = element_blank()) +
  
  # Labels
  labs(x = "Cluster", y = "Feature Value (Z-Score)")

# Print plot
#p_movement_features
```

<!-- **Figure S8. Human-interpretable movement features across clusters.** Each panel represents the distribution of a human-interpretable movement features (depicted as mean ± sd), normalized across the $C$ = `r length(unique(movement_summary$Cluster))` movement clusters. The variation in movement features across clusters reflects the diversity of cursor movement patterns captured in our experiment. -->

```{r}
#| label: load_feature_peaks
#| echo: false
#| include: false

# 1. Load the CSV file with peak data
peak_data <- read.csv(file.path(root, "subtypes", "feature_peaks.csv"), stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
min_max_regression <- read.csv(file.path(root, "subtypes", "min_max_regression.csv"), stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
ttest_prognosis <- read.csv(file.path(root, "subtypes", "two_extrema_ttest_results.csv"), stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# 2. Count the number of peaks by (dimension_name, peak_type)
peak_summary <- ddply(
  peak_data,
  .(dimension_name, peak_type),
  summarise,
  n_peaks = length(peak_type)  # total # of peaks in that category
)

# 3. Label dimensions as "Wellbeing" or "Distress"
peak_summary$dimension_type <- ifelse(
  grepl("Wellbeing", peak_summary$dimension_name),
  "Wellbeing",
  "Distress"
)

# 4. Aggregate by dimension_type (Distress/Wellbeing) and peak_type (maxima/minima)
peak_stats <- ddply(
  peak_summary,
  .(dimension_type, peak_type),
  summarise,
  mean_peaks = round(mean(n_peaks), 2),
  sem_peaks  = round(sd(n_peaks) / sqrt(length(n_peaks)), 2)
)
```

<!-- The geometric correspondence between movement and mental health may help us to discover clinically meaningful subtypes that traditional diagnostic categories obscure. To identify these subtypes, we used the the SVR models trained to predict mental health from movement (see above), and performed a systematic grid search over the first two PCs of the cursor movement embeddings. This resulted in predictive landscapes (Figure X) that were unique for each dimension, corroborating the idea that cursor-based predictions reflect multiple aspects of mental health rather than a single global factor. Distress landscapes were characterized by an average of `r subset(peak_stats, dimension_type=="Distress" & peak_type=="maxima")$mean_peaks` ± `r subset(peak_stats, dimension_type=="Distress" & peak_type=="maxima")$sem_peaks` local maxima and a single global minimum. In contrast, wellbeing landscapes showed `r subset(peak_stats, dimension_type=="Wellbeing" & peak_type=="minima")$mean_peaks` ± `r subset(peak_stats, dimension_type=="Wellbeing" & peak_type=="minima")$sem_peaks` local minima a single global maximum. These multiple high- and low-scoring regions in movement space suggest that distinct behavioral pathways can lead to similar symptom levels on each dimension. -->

<!-- To understand the features encoded in these regions of movement space, we computed a battery of human-interpretable features (speed, speed kurtosis, stationary time, movement area, path efficiency, jerk, and angle changes) for the cursor data that corresponded to the maxima and minima of the dimension-specific predictive landscapes. Each dimension of mental health showed a unique movement profile at its maxima and minima (Figure X). Regions with high predicted scores for anxiety, for example, were marked by relatively slower speeds, higher stationary times, and smaller movement areas, whereas regions with high predicted scores for paranoid ideation exhibited faster speeds, lower stationary times, and broader screen coverage. Within the example of the anxiety landscape, we observed two local maxima, one with slower speed and high stationary time, and another with faster speeds and increased angle changes. This could reflect at two behavioral subtypes of anxiety: a “freeze” or avoidant subtype characterized by inhibited, hesitant cursor movements, and a “restless” or hypervigilant subtype marked by rapid, varied movements. -->

<!-- The identification of multiple, distinct predictive subregions highlights the potential to use cursor movements to discover clinical meaningful subtypes that are driven by distinct biological, psychological, or social pathways. To assess their prognostic value, we assigned participants to whichever of the two local maxima (or minima) they were closest to in each predictive landscape and compared their changes in the respective mental health at follow-up. Indeed, for the dimension of interpersonal sensitivity, this exploratory analysis suggested that people closer to one of two local maxima in session 1 showed improved mental health outcomes in session 2 (change scores: `r ttest_prognosis[ttest_prognosis$QuestionIndex == "Interpersonal Sensitivity",]$Mean_GroupA` ± `r ttest_prognosis[ttest_prognosis$QuestionIndex == "Interpersonal Sensitivity",]$SD_GroupA` versus `r ttest_prognosis[ttest_prognosis$QuestionIndex == "Interpersonal Sensitivity",]$Mean_GroupB` ± `r ttest_prognosis[ttest_prognosis$QuestionIndex == "Interpersonal Sensitivity",]$SD_GroupB`, p = `r ttest_prognosis[ttest_prognosis$QuestionIndex == "Interpersonal Sensitivity",]$Permutation_p`). -->

<!-- ## Forensics  -->

<!-- Cursor movements provide an passive and unobtrusive behavioral marker of mental states that is less susceptible to conscious manipulation than self-report and traditional clinical assessments. Language-based instruments, including structured interviews and questionnaires, rely on verbal or written responses, which can be intentionally distorted to present a desired impression. In contrast, habitual cursor movements reflect implicit cognitive and motor processes that are likely more resistant to deliberate control. Our simulations indicate that the geometric relationship between cursor trajectories and mental health reports collapses when the data are permuted, mimicking a scenario in which self-reported mental health states do not accurately reflect an individual’s mental state. We were able to discriminate true from permuted pairs of movement features and mental health with an accuracy of XYZ. This suggests that cursor-based metrics can serve as an implicit consistency check, flagging discrepancies between reported experiences and underlying cognitive-affective states. This capability could be valuable for credibility assessments in forensic evaluations, insurance claims, security screenings, and clinical settings where accurate self-reporting is critical. -->

<!-- In sum, these results suggest that cursor movement can be used to subtype individuals into distinct behavioral subtypes that may develop into new endophenotypes for precision medicine.  -->

<!-- 1/3 of health care costs in US tied to hospitalization[@topol_high-performance_2019]. -->

<!-- A digital twin is a dynamic virtual model that mirrors an individual’s real-time state using continuous data inputs. In mental health, digital twins could combine behavioral signals, environmental factors, and clinical data to track changes, predict risk, and inform personalized interventions. Cursor tracking — as a passive, continuous, and easily anonymized behavioral marker — could enrich these models by providing real-time insights into cognitive and emotional states, potentially enhancing early detection and intervention strategies through virtual assistants or human clinicians. -->

<!-- [personalized mental health care and digital phenotyping] -->

<!-- closed loop interventions -->

<!-- Digital TWIN: PEOPLE CLOSE TO YOU, SIMILAR PROFILE!  -->

<!-- The identification of distinct subspaces in movement features highlights CMs’ potential in stratifying mental health dimensions into meaningful subtypes. For example, subspaces for depression reflect different movement patterns, potentially linked to variations in clinical presentation or treatment response. This approach lays the groundwork for more personalized mental health interventions. -->

<!-- Supplementary control analyses shed light on the practical requirements for effective cursor-based predictions. Reducing the calibration sample size to fewer than XYZ participants led to a significant decline in prediction accuracy, emphasizing the need for a sufficiently large cohort to ensure robust calibration. Similarly, decreasing the duration of cursor tracking below XYZ minutes reduced predictive performance, although meaningful predictions could still be achieved with as little as XYZ minutes of data. While this study focused on straightforward online tasks, future research should explore whether more complex behaviors further enhance predictive power, potentially expanding the applicability of cursor-based mental health assessments. -->

<!-- In parallel to the greater precision in understanding neural and cognitive systems, there is a need for greater precision in symptom profiling. -->

<!-- Although dimensional models (for example, HiTOP, tri-level model) -->

<!-- have many advantages over diagnostic categories, they are limited to -->

<!-- traditional symptom questionnaire measures that represent explicit -->

<!-- retrospective judgements of oneself that, although relevant in and -->

<!-- of themselves, are biased (for example, depressive recall of negative -->

<!-- over positive self-referent information)116. Ecological momentary -->

<!-- assessments of subjective experience capture affective dynamics -->

<!-- in daily life that are less biased than traditional questionnaires that -->

<!-- estimate status over lengthy intervals, but remain limited to self -->

<!-- reporting117. A challenge for the field is achieving greater precision in -->

<!-- individualized symptom assessment that is not fully reliant on -->

<!-- language, self-awareness and retrospection[@craske_neural_2023], although in nascent -->

<!-- stages, digital phenotyping of behaviors and peripheral physiology -->

<!-- through smartphones and wearable sensors can record features -->

<!-- such as movement, sleep, social interaction, heart rate variability, -->

<!-- vocal and facial featureS128 that continuously snapshot mental -->

<!-- health-relevant features. Traditional questionnaire symptom ratings -->

<!-- combined with moment-to-moment dynamic trajectories in selfreport plus continuous physiological and behavioral expressions may -->

<!-- lead to more precise measurement needed for precision mapping -->

<!-- onto neurobiology. The digital divide will remain an obstacle, however, for global implementation of these high-frequency naturalistic -->

<!-- measurements. -->

<!-- Anbgel npj -->

<!-- The first step in generating meaningful clinical information from data derived from digital sensors is to generate features, which are the smallest constructed building blocks, designed to explain the behaviours of interest (see Mohr et al. 10 for a detailed analytical framework). These low-level features are often aggregated to define high-level behavioural markers, which can be understood as symptoms. For example, GPS data (sensor), can be translated into ‘location type’ (low-level feature), ‘increased time at home location’ (high-level behaviour) derived from location data may indicate social withdrawal or lack of energy (symptom), and may therefore be associated with depression severity. -->

```{r}
#| label: color_wheel
#| include: true

generate_circular_color_wheel <- function(resolution = 300, white_center_fraction = 0.4, brightness = 0.8) {
  # Generate a grid of points
  x <- seq(-1, 1, length.out = resolution)
  y <- seq(-1, 1, length.out = resolution)
  grid <- expand.grid(x = x, y = y)
  
  # Convert to polar coordinates
  grid$radius <- sqrt(grid$x^2 + grid$y^2)
  grid$theta <- atan2(grid$y, grid$x)
  
  # Calculate HSV values for circular hue mapping
  grid$hue <- (grid$theta + pi) / (2 * pi)  # Map angle to [0, 1] for hue
  grid$saturation <- pmin(1, grid$radius)   # Saturation increases with radius
  grid$value <- brightness                  # Reduced brightness for darker tones
  
  # Convert HSV to RGB
  hsv_to_rgb <- function(h, s, v) {
    rgb_matrix <- hsv(h, s, v)
    return(rgb_matrix)
  }
  
  grid$color <- hsv_to_rgb(grid$hue, grid$saturation, grid$value)
  
  # Mask out the white center
  grid <- grid %>% mutate(color = ifelse(radius <= white_center_fraction, "white", color))
  
  # Only keep points inside the circle (remove corners)
  grid <- grid %>% filter(radius <= 1)
  
  # Create a circular clipping mask using a polygon
  circle_clip <- data.frame(
    x = cos(seq(0, 2 * pi, length.out = 100)),
    y = sin(seq(0, 2 * pi, length.out = 100))
  )
  
  # Plot the perfect circular color wheel
  color_wheel <- ggplot() +
    geom_tile(data = grid, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = color), color = NA, alpha = 0.6) +
    geom_polygon(data = circle_clip, aes(x = x, y = y), fill = NA, color = "white", size = 1.5) +
    scale_fill_identity() +
    coord_fixed() +
    theme_void()
  
  return(color_wheel)
}

# Generate the Perfect Circular Color Wheel
color_wheel <- generate_circular_color_wheel(resolution = 300, white_center_fraction = 0.4, brightness = 0.5)
# color_wheel
```

```{r}
#| label: prediction_icon
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| cache: true
# Function to generate Perfect Circular Color Wheel

dot_size <- 0.5

# Set seed for reproducibility
set.seed(42)

# Generate synthetic data
n_samples <- 100
mean_vec <- c(0, 0, 0)
cov_matrix <- matrix(c(0.5, 0, 0, 
                       0, 0.5, 0, 
                       0, 0, 0.5), ncol = 3)

# Generate 3D data for Mental Health
mental_data <- MASS::mvrnorm(n_samples, mean_vec, cov_matrix)

# Rotation function
rotate_data <- function(data, angle = pi / 4) {
  rotation_matrix <- matrix(c(cos(angle), -sin(angle), sin(angle), cos(angle)), ncol = 2)
  rotated_data <- as.matrix(data[, 1:2]) %*% rotation_matrix
  data[, 1:2] <- rotated_data
  return(data)
}

# Rotate Movement Data before Normalization
movement_data <- mental_data + matrix(rnorm(n_samples * 3, 0, 0.2), ncol=3)
movement_data <- rotate_data(movement_data)

# Normalize function to unit interval [0, 1]
normalize_to_unit_interval <- function(data) {
  min_val <- apply(data, 2, min)
  max_val <- apply(data, 2, max)
  return((data - min_val) / (max_val - min_val))
}

# Normalize both datasets to [0, 1]
mental_data <- normalize_to_unit_interval(mental_data)
movement_data <- normalize_to_unit_interval(movement_data)

# Create data frames
df_movement <- as.data.frame(movement_data)
df_mental <- as.data.frame(mental_data)
colnames(df_movement) <- c("X", "Y", "Z")
colnames(df_mental) <- c("X", "Y", "Z")

# Generate RGB colors based on the 3 dimensions in Mental Health
get_rgb_color <- function(x, y, z) {
  # Normalize each dimension to [0, 1]
  norm_x <- (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
  norm_y <- (y - min(y)) / (max(y) - min(y))
  norm_z <- (z - min(z)) / (max(z) - min(z))
  
  # Clip to [0, 1] to avoid rgb() errors
  norm_x <- pmin(pmax(norm_x, 0), 1)
  norm_y <- pmin(pmax(norm_y, 0), 1)
  norm_z <- pmin(pmax(norm_z, 0), 1)
  
  # Combine to form RGB colors
  rgb_colors <- rgb(norm_x, norm_y, norm_z, maxColorValue = 1)
  return(rgb_colors)
}

# Apply the RGB color function to the 3 dimensions of Mental Health data
df_mental$color <- get_rgb_color(df_mental$X, df_mental$Y, df_mental$Z)
df_movement$color <- df_mental$color  # Colors are based on Mental Health

# Plot Cursor Movements
plot_movement <- ggplot(df_movement, aes(x = X, y = Y, color = color)) +
  geom_point(size = dot_size) +
  scale_color_identity() +
  labs(x = expression(x[1]), y = expression(x[2])) +
  #annotate("text", x = 0.02, y = 1, label = "Cursor Movement", hjust = 0, vjust = 1, size = 3) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  xlim(0,1) + ylim(0,1) + labs(subtitle = 'A') + facet_wrap(~"Movement") + theme(panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2))

# Plot Mental Health Space
plot_mental <- ggplot(df_mental, aes(x = X, y = Y, color = color)) +
  geom_point(size = dot_size) +
  scale_color_identity() +
  labs(x = expression(y[1]), y = expression(y[2])) +
  #annotate("text", x = 0.02, y = 1, label = "Mental Health", hjust = 0, vjust = 1, size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.75, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.75, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  xlim(0,1) + scale_y_continuous(position = "right", limits = c(0, 1)) + labs(subtitle = ' ') + facet_wrap(~"Mental Health") + theme(panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2))

# Arrange the three plots side by side
#grid.arrange(plot_movement, color_wheel, plot_mental, heights = c(1), widths = c(1, 0.3, 1))
```

```{r}
#| label: geometry_icon
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| cache: true
# Load required libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(gridExtra)

# Subset Data for a Few Participants
subset_indices <- sample(1:n_samples, 5)  # Select 10 random participants
df_mental_subset <- df_mental[subset_indices, ]
df_movement_subset <- df_movement[subset_indices, ]

# Function to Create Fully Connected Data Frame
create_fully_connected_df <- function(data) {
  connections <- expand.grid(start = 1:nrow(data), end = 1:nrow(data))
  connections <- connections[connections$start != connections$end, ]
  
  df_lines <- data.frame(
    x_start = data[connections$start, "X"],
    y_start = data[connections$start, "Y"],
    x_end = data[connections$end, "X"],
    y_end = data[connections$end, "Y"]
  )
  
  return(df_lines)
}

# Create Fully Connected Data Frames
df_lines_movement <- create_fully_connected_df(df_movement_subset)
df_lines_mental <- create_fully_connected_df(df_mental_subset)

# Plot Cursor Movements (Subset) with Fully Connected Lines
plot_movement_geom <- ggplot(df_movement_subset, aes(x = X, y = Y, color = color)) +
  geom_point(size = dot_size) +
  geom_segment(data = df_lines_movement, 
               aes(x = x_start, y = y_start, xend = x_end, yend = y_end), 
               color = "black", alpha = 0.1) +  # Black lines to connect all dots
  scale_color_identity() +
  labs(x = expression(x[1]), y = expression(x[2]), subtitle = 'A') +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) + xlim(0, 1) + ylim(0, 1) + theme(panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2)) + facet_wrap(~"Movement")

# Plot Mental Health Space (Subset) with Fully Connected Lines
plot_mental_geom <- ggplot(df_mental_subset, aes(x = X, y = Y, color = color)) +
  geom_point(size = dot_size) +
  geom_segment(data = df_lines_mental, 
               aes(x = x_start, y = y_start, xend = x_end, yend = y_end), 
               color = "black", alpha = 0.1) +  # Black lines to connect all dots
  scale_color_identity() +
  labs(x = expression(y[1]), y = expression(y[2]), subtitle = '')  +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.75, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.75, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) + xlim(0,1) + scale_y_continuous(position = "right", limits = c(0, 1)) + theme(panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2)) + facet_wrap(~"Mental Health")

# Arrange the two plots side by side
#grid.arrange(plot_movement_geom, color_wheel, plot_mental_geom, heights = c(1), widths = c(1, 0.3, 1))
```

```{r}
#| label: change_icon
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| cache: true
#| 
# Load required libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(gridExtra)
library(ggforce)  # For arrows in ggplot2

dot_size <- 0.5
n_change_samples = 20
# Subset Data for a Few Participants
subset_indices <- sample(1:n_samples, n_change_samples)  # Select 10 random participants
df_mental_subset <- df_mental[subset_indices, ]
df_movement_subset <- df_movement[subset_indices, ]

# Reuse the same starting positions for both spaces
df_movement_start <- df_movement_subset
df_mental_start <- df_mental_subset

# Generate correlated change vectors for Mental Health and Movement
change_vectors_mental <- MASS::mvrnorm(n_change_samples, c(0.05, 0.05, 0.05), cov_matrix * 0.05)
change_vectors_movement <- change_vectors_mental + 
  MASS::mvrnorm(n_change_samples, c(0.02, -0.02, 0.03), cov_matrix * 0.05)

# Compute new positions by adding change vectors
df_movement_end <- df_movement_start
df_mental_end <- df_mental_start

# Normalize function to unit interval [0, 1] and Clip to stay within [0, 1]
normalize_and_clip <- function(data) {
  min_val <- apply(data, 2, min)
  max_val <- apply(data, 2, max)
  normalized <- (data - min_val) / (max_val - min_val)
  # Clip to stay within [0, 1]
  normalized <- pmin(pmax(normalized, 0), 1)
  return(normalized)
}

df_movement_end[, 1:3] <- normalize_and_clip(as.matrix(df_movement_start[, 1:3]) + change_vectors_movement)
df_mental_end[, 1:3] <- normalize_and_clip(as.matrix(df_mental_start[, 1:3]) + change_vectors_mental)

# Generate RGB colors based on the final positions in Mental Health
get_rgb_color <- function(x, y, z) {
  # Normalize and Clip to [0, 1] to avoid rgb() errors
  norm_x <- pmin(pmax((x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)), 0), 1)
  norm_y <- pmin(pmax((y - min(y)) / (max(y) - min(y)), 0), 1)
  norm_z <- pmin(pmax((z - min(z)) / (max(z) - min(z)), 0), 1)
  
  # Combine to form RGB colors
  rgb_colors <- rgb(norm_x, norm_y, norm_z, maxColorValue = 1)
  return(rgb_colors)
}

# Apply the RGB color function to the 3 dimensions of final Mental Health data
df_mental_end$color <- get_rgb_color(df_mental_end$X, df_mental_end$Y, df_mental_end$Z)
df_movement_end$color <- df_mental_end$color  # Same color coding for both spaces

# Start points keep the same color as end points for consistency
df_movement_start$color <- df_movement_end$color
df_mental_start$color <- df_mental_end$color

# Create long format data for arrows (Change Vectors)
df_arrows_mental <- data.frame(
  x_start = df_mental_start$X,
  y_start = df_mental_start$Y,
  x_end = df_mental_end$X,
  y_end = df_mental_end$Y,
  color = df_mental_start$color
)

df_arrows_movement <- data.frame(
  x_start = df_movement_start$X,
  y_start = df_movement_start$Y,
  x_end = df_movement_end$X,
  y_end = df_movement_end$Y,
  color = df_movement_start$color
)

# Plot Cursor Movements with Change Vectors
plot_movement_change <- ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = df_movement_start, aes(x = X, y = Y, color = color), size = dot_size, alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_point(data = df_movement_end, aes(x = X, y = Y, color = color), size = dot_size, alpha = 1) +
  geom_segment(data = df_arrows_movement, 
               aes(x = x_start, y = y_start, xend = x_end, yend = y_end, color = color), 
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm")), alpha = 0.25) +
  scale_color_identity() +
  labs(x = expression(x[1]), y = expression(x[2]), subtitle = 'A') +
  theme_classic(base_font_size) + xlim(0, 1) + ylim(0, 1) + facet_wrap(~"Movement") + theme(panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2))

# Plot Mental Health Space with Change Vectors
plot_mental_change <- ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = df_mental_start, aes(x = X, y = Y, color = color), size = dot_size, alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_point(data = df_mental_end, aes(x = X, y = Y, color = color), size = dot_size, alpha = 1) +
  geom_segment(data = df_arrows_mental, 
               aes(x = x_start, y = y_start, xend = x_end, yend = y_end, color = color), 
               arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm")), alpha = 0.25) +
  scale_color_identity() +
  labs(x = expression(y[1]), y = expression(y[2]), subtitle = ' ') +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.75, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.75, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  theme_classic(base_font_size) + xlim(0,1) + scale_y_continuous(position = "right", limits = c(0, 1)) + facet_wrap(~"Mental health") + theme(panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2))

# Arrange the two plots side by side
#grid.arrange(plot_movement_change, color_wheel, plot_mental_change, heights = c(1), widths = c(1, 0.3, 1))
```

```{r}
#| label: Supplemental_Figure_S6X_discrete_clinical
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| cache: true
#| out.width: "100%"
#| out.height: "100%"


# Ensure cleaned column names for all factors
permuted_aucs$Dimension <- str_replace_all(permuted_aucs$Dimension, "\\-", " ") 

# Set factor levels: Overall dimensions first, then ordered standard dimensions
permuted_aucs$Dimension <- factor(
  permuted_aucs$Dimension, 
  levels = c(ordered_standard_dimensions)
)

# Add Questionnaire column
permuted_aucs$Questionnaire <- ifelse(grepl("Wellbeing", permuted_aucs$Dimension), "Wellbeing", "Distress")
permuted_aucs$Questionnaire <- factor(permuted_aucs$Questionnaire, levels = c("Distress", "Wellbeing"))


p_disease_transitions = ggplot(permuted_aucs[permuted_aucs$Type == "Permuted",], 
       aes(x = Dimension, y = AUC, color = Dimension, fill = Dimension)) +
    geom_violin(color = 'white', alpha = 0.5, width = 1, bw = 0.01) +
    geom_point(data = permuted_aucs[permuted_aucs$Type == "Real",], 
               aes(color = Dimension), 
               size = 1) + 
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = custom_colors) +
    scale_color_manual(values = custom_colors) +
    theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) + 
    facet_grid(~Questionnaire, scales = 'free', space = 'free_x') +
    labs(x = "Dimension",
         y = "AUC (transition across the 90% percentile",
         subtitle = "A") +
    theme(legend.position = "none",  
        legend.spacing.x = unit(0.2, "cm"),  
        legend.text = element_text(size = base_font_size),  
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
        panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
        panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2))

# Create a single plot with faceting
p_facet_comorbidity <- ggplot(comorbidity_long, aes(x = Test, y = Retest)) +
  geom_point(size =  0.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "D") +
  facet_wrap(~ Type, ncol = 1, labeller = as_labeller(c(
    "Actual" = "Actual Comorbidity",
    "Predicted" = "Predicted Comorbidity"
  ))) +
  labs(x = "Test", 
       y = "Retest", subtitle = "B") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) + theme(legend.position = 'none', panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2))

# Scatter Plot: Change in Actual vs. Predicted Comorbidity
p_corr_comorbidity <- ggplot(comorbidity_df, aes(x = Comorbidity_Change_Actual, y = Comorbidity_Change_Predicted)) +
  geom_point(size =  0.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  stat_density_2d(
    aes(fill = after_stat(level)),
    geom = "polygon",
    color = NA,
    alpha = 0.4,
    contour_var = "ndensity"
  ) +
   scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "D") +
  #geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = my_red, fill = my_red, alpha = 0.25, linetype = "solid", size = 0.25) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = 0), linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +  # Add vertical lines
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = 0), linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  labs(x = "Actual comorbidity change", y = "Predicted comorbidity change", subtitle = " ") + stat_cor(method = "spearman",  label.x.npc = "left", label.y.npc = "top", size = 1.5) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) + theme(legend.position = 'none', panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2))



p_facet_entropy <- ggplot(entropy_long, aes(x = Test, y = Retest)) +
  geom_point(size =  0.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  facet_wrap(~ Type, ncol = 1, labeller = as_labeller(c(
    "Actual" = "Actual Entropy",
    "Predicted" = "Predicted Entropy"
  ))) +
  labs(x = "Test", 
       y = "Retest", subtitle = "C")+ theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) + theme(legend.position = 'none', panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2)) 

p_corr_entropy <- ggplot(entropy_df, aes(x = Entropy_Change_Actual, y = Entropy_Change_Predicted)) +
  geom_point(size =  0.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  stat_density_2d(
    aes(fill = after_stat(level)),
    geom = "polygon",
    color = NA,
    alpha = 0.4,
    contour_var = "ndensity"
  ) +
   scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "D") +
  #geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = my_red, fill = my_red, alpha = 0.25, linetype = "solid", size = 0.25) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = 0), linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +  # Add vertical lines
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = 0), linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  labs(x = "Actual entropy change", y = "Predicted entropy change", subtitle = " ") +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) + theme(legend.position = 'none') + stat_cor(method = "spearman",  label.x.npc = "left", label.y.npc = "top", size = 1.5) + theme(panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90", size = 0.4),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey95", size = 0.2),)

library(gridExtra)
# lay1 <- rbind(c(1,2,3), c(1,4,5))

#              p_corr_comorbidity, p_facet_entropy, p_corr_entropy, 
#              layout_matrix = lay1, heights = c(1,1), widths = c(1.8,1,2))
```

```{r}
#| label: mental_health_3D_plot
#| include: true

# Load required libraries
library(plotly)
library(dplyr)
library(viridis)

# Define file path
pca_projections_path <- file.path(root, 'dimensions', 'pca_projections.csv')

# Load the PCA projections CSV
pca_projections <- read.csv(pca_projections_path)

# Ensure 'Dimension' is a factor with defined order
pca_projections$Dimension <- factor(pca_projections$Dimension, levels = unique(pca_projections$Dimension))

# Define color palette consistent with previous plots
color_palette <- viridis_pal()(length(unique(pca_projections$Dimension)))

# Create a Plotly 3D scatter plot
plot_3d <- plot_ly()

# Iterate through each dimension and plot separately
for (i in seq_along(unique(pca_projections$Dimension))) {
  
  # Select subset for the current dimension
  dim_name <- unique(pca_projections$Dimension)[i]
  dim_data <- pca_projections %>% filter(Dimension == dim_name)
  
  # Define color and alpha range
  color <- color_palette[i]
  alphas <- seq(0.3, 1, length.out = nrow(dim_data))  # Increasing transparency
  
  # Add trajectory points with increasing alpha
  plot_3d <- plot_3d %>%
    add_trace(
      type = "scatter3d",
      mode = "markers",
      x = dim_data$PC1, y = dim_data$PC2, z = dim_data$PC3,
      marker = list(size = dot_size_main, color = color, opacity = alphas),
      name = as.character(dim_name),
      showlegend = FALSE
    )
  
  # Add endpoint label
  plot_3d <- plot_3d %>%
    add_trace(
      type = "scatter3d",
      mode = "text",
      x = dim_data$PC1[nrow(dim_data)], 
      y = dim_data$PC2[nrow(dim_data)], 
      z = dim_data$PC3[nrow(dim_data)],
      text = as.character(dim_name),
      textposition = "top right",
      showlegend = FALSE,
      textfont = list(size = base_font_size, color = color)
    )
}

# Final layout adjustments with consistent font size
plot_3d <- plot_3d %>%
  layout(
    title = NULL,
    scene = list(
      xaxis = list(title = "Principal Component 1", titlefont = list(size = base_font_size), tickfont = list(size = base_font_size)),
      yaxis = list(title = "Principal Component 2", titlefont = list(size = base_font_size), tickfont = list(size = base_font_size)),
      zaxis = list(title = "Principal Component 3", titlefont = list(size = base_font_size), tickfont = list(size = base_font_size)),
      camera = list(eye = list(x = 1.5, y = 1.5, z = 1))  # Adjust elevation and azimuth
    ),
    font = list(size = base_font_size)  # Ensure all text follows base_font_size
  )

# Show the plot
#plot_3d
```

<!-- **Figure S3. Inferring mental health from digital behavior.** -->

<!-- **A. Communication as probabilistic mapping.** Humans infer each other’s mental states through both verbal and non-verbal communication. Both channels map onto a high-dimensional latent space of mental states (top row), illustrated here in 2D, with blue indicating neutral states and red indicating distress. Verbal signals are precise but sparse - they require active engagement and often interrupt ongoing behavior. Non-verbal signals are noisier but continuous - they can be observed passively during ongoing activity. Verbal and non-verbal communication enable probabilistic inferences about others’ mental states. While both are essential to social interaction, mental health is typically assessed through verbal reports, such as questionnaires or structured interviews. -->

<!-- **B. MAILA as digital non-verbal communication.** -->
<!-- MAILA enables non-verbal communication in the digital world. Each individual is represented as a point in the space of digital behavior $\mathrm{X^{N \times C}}$, where $C$ denotes cursor movement features (e.g., $x_1$-$x_3$), and in the space of self-reported mental health $Y^{N \times Q}$, where $Q$ denotes mental health features (e.g., $y_1$ = sadness, $y_2$ = anxiety, $y_3$ = anger: N = number of individuals). An encoding function $f(Y; \gamma): Y \rightarrow X$ governs how mental states shape behavior, with $\gamma$ reflecting the strength of this relationship. MAILA approximates the inverse decoding function $f(X; \delta): X \rightarrow Y$, where $\delta$ denotes the learned weights of support vector regressors trained to infer mental health profiles from cursor movement features. Because cursor movements are ubiquitous, passive byproducts of almost all digital activity, MAILA enables probabilistic mental health inferences in anyone using a computer. -->

<!-- **C. Hierarchical structure of the MAILA dataset.** -->
<!-- The MAILA dataset includes 10,000 recordings of cursor and touchscreen behavior annotated with mental health self-reports. It comprises two main branches: cursor-based data (MAILA-C, 6k recordings) and touchscreen-based data (MAILA-T, 4k). A subset of cursor data includes longitudinal follow-up recordings (MAILA-F, 2k), from which two generalization sets are drawn (MAILA-G1, MAILA-G2, 0.6k each). MAILA-G3 includes 4,000 touchscreen-based generalization recordings. Together, these subsets support model training, validation, and generalization across input devices and behavioral context. -->

```{r}
#| label: Supplemental_Figure_S10_causal_intervention
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| cache: true
#| 
library(ggplot2)
library(plyr)
library(dplyr)

# Plot parameters
dodge_width <- 0.5
alpha_dot <- 0.3
dot_size <- 1.2
line_size <- 0.3

# Prepare dataframe
df <- rt_valence_arousal %>%
  filter(Dimension %in% c("Overall Distress", "Overall Wellbeing")) %>%
  mutate(
    Valence = factor(Valence, levels = c("Low", "High"), labels = c("Negative", "Positive")),
    Arousal = factor(Arousal, levels = c("Low", "High"))
  )

# Compute group summary
group_summary <- ddply(df, .(Arousal, Valence, Dimension), summarise,
  mean = mean(Prediction, na.rm = TRUE),
  error = sd(Prediction, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(length(Prediction))
)

# Plot
p_valence_arousal <- ggplot(df, aes(x = Arousal, y = Prediction, color = Valence)) +
  geom_jitter(
    alpha = alpha_dot,
    size = dot_size/2,
    position = position_jitterdodge(dodge.width = dodge_width, jitter.width = 0.2, jitter.height = 0)
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    data = group_summary,
    aes(x = Arousal, ymin = mean - error, ymax = mean + error, color = Valence),
    width = dodge_width / 3,
    position = position_dodge(width = dodge_width * 2),
    size = line_size * 2,
    alpha = 1,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1", direction = 1) +
  labs(
    x = "Arousal",
    y = "Predicted score",
    subtitle = "B",
    color = "Valence"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ Dimension) +
  theme(
    panel.spacing = unit(1, "lines"),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

set.seed(42)
n <- 100
# Define lengths for the three phases that sum to n
phase1_n <- floor(n / 3)
phase2_n <- floor(n / 3)
phase3_n <- n - (phase1_n + phase2_n)  # remainder goes to phase3

# Simulate trajectory phases
valence <- c(seq(0.5, 0.2, length.out = phase1_n),
             seq(0.2, 0.2, length.out = phase2_n),
             seq(0.2, 0.5, length.out = phase3_n)) + rnorm(n, 0, 0.015)

arousal <- c(seq(0.5, 0.8, length.out = phase1_n),
             seq(0.8, 0.4, length.out = phase2_n),
             seq(0.4, 0.5, length.out = phase3_n)) + rnorm(n, 0, 0.015)

# Clamp values between 0 and 1
valence <- pmin(pmax(valence, 0), 1)
arousal <- pmin(pmax(arousal, 0), 1)

# Construct trajectory dataframe
trajectory <- data.frame(
  Valence = valence,
  Arousal = arousal,
  Time = 1:n
)

# Keep within circular bounds
trajectory$Valence <- pmin(pmax(trajectory$Valence, 0), 1)
trajectory$Arousal <- pmin(pmax(trajectory$Arousal, 0), 1)

# Outer circle
circle <- data.frame(angle = seq(0, 2*pi, length.out = 200)) %>%
  mutate(x = 0.5 + 0.5 * cos(angle), y = 0.5 + 0.5 * sin(angle))

# Grid circles
grid_radii <- seq(0.1, 0.5, by = 0.1)
grid_circles <- lapply(grid_radii, function(r) {
  data.frame(angle = seq(0, 2*pi, length.out = 200)) %>%
    mutate(x = 0.5 + r * cos(angle), y = 0.5 + r * sin(angle), r = r)
}) %>% bind_rows()

# Tick segments for Arousal and Valence axes
tick_segments <- data.frame(
  x = c(0, 1, 0.5, 0.5),
  xend = c(0, 1, 0.49, 0.51),
  y = c(0.5, 0.5, 0, 1),
  yend = c(0.51, 0.49, 0, 1)
)

# Axis labels
axis_labels <- data.frame(
  label = c("Valence", "Arousal"),
  x = c(1, 0.5),
  y = c(0.5, 0.75),
  hjust = c(1.1, 0.5),
  vjust = c(0.5, -0.5)
)

p_emotion_cursor <- ggplot() +
  geom_path(data = grid_circles, aes(x = x, y = y, group = r), color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = 1, y = 0.5, yend = 0.5), color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0.5, xend = 0.5, y = 0, yend = 1), color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_segment(data = tick_segments, aes(x = x, xend = xend, y = y, yend = yend),
               color = "grey50", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_path(data = circle, aes(x = x, y = y), color = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_path(data = trajectory, aes(x = Valence, y = Arousal, color = Time), linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_point(data = trajectory, aes(x = Valence, y = Arousal, color = Time), size = 1) +
  scale_color_viridis(option = "D", direction = 1) +
  geom_text(data = axis_labels, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label, hjust = hjust, vjust = vjust),
            size = base_font_size / .pt) +
  coord_fixed(xlim = c(0, 1), ylim = c(0, 1)) +
  theme_void(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  labs(subtitle = "A")

# # **Arrange & Display First Row of Plots**
# lay1 <- rbind(c(1,2), c(3,2))
# grid.arrange(p_emotion_cursor, p_valence_arousal, layout_matrix = lay1, heights = c(1,1), widths = c(0.5,1))
```

<!-- **Figure S10. Cursor-based mental health predictions respond to experienced emotion.** -->

<!-- **A. Continuous tracking of valence and arousal.** We applied the cursor-based mental health prediction to cursor tracking data from an independent experiment where participants provided continuous ratings of the emotion they experienced in emotionally evocative videos. They reported moment-to-moment fluctuations in valence (negative to positive) and arousal (low to high) on a 2D circular grid (axis counterbalanced across participants). Panel A shows an example trajectory from a single trial, spanning 100 time points: the participant starts in a neutral state, shifts toward high arousal and negative valence, gradually moves to low arousal, and eventually returns to baseline. Brighter colors indicate later time points. We applied the same analysis pipeline as in the main experiment. We separated the cursor cursor movements into segments, embedded them with the pretrained LSTM autoencoder, and projected them into the space of movement signatures identified byt the k-means clusters from the main experiment. We then generated predictions for overall distress and wellbeing using the pretrained SVR models from the main experiment, separately for high versus low arousal and positive versus negative valence, which were defined based on within-participant median splits. Please note that the applied clustering and SVR models had never encountered these participants or data from an experiment involving continuous emotion tracking. -->

<!-- **B. Prediction of distress and wellbeing during emotion tracking.** Predicted distress (left) and wellbeing (right) as a function of arousal and valence (median splits). A linear mixed-effects model revealed significant main effects of arousal ($\mathrm{beta}$ = $`r -STAT.Distress_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[2,1]`$ ± $`r STAT.Distress_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[2,2]`$, T($`r STAT.Distress_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[2,3]`$) = $`r -STAT.Distress_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[2,4]`$, p = $`r STAT.Distress_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[2,5]`$) and  valence ($\mathrm{beta}$ = $`r -STAT.Distress_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[3,1]`$ ± $`r STAT.Distress_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[3,2]`$, T($`r STAT.Distress_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[3,3]`$) = $`r -STAT.Distress_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[3,4]`$, p = $`r STAT.Distress_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[3,5]`$), indicating higher distress in high-arousal, negative-valence states. We observed no interaction. Predicted wellbeing also depended on both arousal and valence, but in opposite directions depending on their combination. A significant interaction between arousal and valence revealed that positive valence increased predicted wellbeing under low arousal, but reduced it under high arousal ($\mathrm{beta}$ = $`r STAT.Wellbeing_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[3,1]`$ ± $`r STAT.Wellbeing_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[3,2]`$, T($`r STAT.Wellbeing_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[3,3]`$) = $`r STAT.Wellbeing_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[3,4]`$, p = $`r STAT.Wellbeing_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[3,5]`$). Main effects of Arousal and Valence were also present (arousal: p = $`r STAT.Wellbeing_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[2,5]`$, valence: p = $`r STAT.Wellbeing_Arousal_Valence$coefficients[3,5]`\$). -->

```{r}
#| label: SF11_real_permuted_change_vectors_done
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| cache: true
#| 
# Plot density of permuted correlations with observed correlation marked
p_density_analysis <- ggplot(permuted_vector, aes(x = Permutation_Mean_Rho)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "black", alpha = 0.5, color = "white", bins = 100) +
  geom_vline(data = observed_vector, aes(xintercept = Mean_Spearman_rho), color = my_red, 
             linetype = "solid", size = 0.8, alpha = 0.75) +
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  labs(x = "Empirical (red) and permuted correlation (grey) between actual and predicted change vectors", y = "Density")

# Show the plot
#p_density_analysis
```

<!-- **Figure S12. Per-participant correlation between** $\Delta_M$ and $\Delta_C$. Distribution of permuted correlations (gray) between actual ($\Delta_M$) and predicted change vectors $\Delta_C$, with the average observed correlation marked in red. The observed correlation exceeds the null distribution (permutations across participants), indicating that cursor-based predictions reliably track multi-dimensional mental health changes. -->

```{r}
#| label: SF12_identification_versus_regression
#| echo: false
#| include: true
#| cache: true
#| out.width: "100%"
#| out.height: "100%"

dot_size    <- 1
line_size   <- 1.0
dodge_width <- 0.1
p_weeks_acc <- ggplot(summary_ident_combined, aes(x = weeks, y = av_acc)) +
  geom_point(size = dot_size) +
  geom_line(size = dot_size/2) +
  
  # Use log-scale for y
  scale_y_log10() +
  
  # Add horizontal dashed line at chance=1/4000
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1/4000, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.25) +
  
  # A classic theme
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  
  # Axis labels, title, etc.
  labs(
    x = "Weeks",
    y = "Identification accuracy (log)",
    subtitle = "A"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none"
  )


p_mse_time <- ggplot(summary_mse_combined, aes(x = weeks, y = av_mse)) +
  # Error bars: av_mse ± sd_mse
  geom_errorbar(aes(
    ymin = av_mse - sem_mse,
    ymax = av_mse + sem_mse
  ), width = 0.2) +
  # Points
  geom_point(size = dot_size) +
  # Optional connecting line
  geom_line(size = dot_size/2) +
  # A simple theme
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  labs(
    x = "Weeks",
    y = "mse",
    subtitle = "B"
  )

p_ident_mse <- ggplot(data = summary_ident_vs_regression, aes(x = mean_accuracy, y = mean_MSE, ymin = mean_MSE - sem_MSE,  ymax = mean_MSE + sem_MSE)) +
  # 1) Violin plot for each mean_accuracy level
  geom_errorbar(width = 0.2) +
  geom_point(size = dot_size) +
  # 5) A classic theme
  theme_classic(base_size = base_font_size) +
  # 6) Axis labels, title, etc.
  labs(
    x = "Identification accuracy",
    y = "mse",
    subtitle = "C"
  )

# lay1 <- rbind(c(2,1), c(3,1))
# grid.arrange(p_ident_mse, p_weeks_acc, p_mse_time, layout_matrix = lay1, heights = c(1,1), widths = c(0.5,0.25))
```

<!-- **Figure S15. Identification performance and mental health prediction.** -->

<!-- **A. Identification performance over time.** The proportion of accurately identified individuals (y-axis, log scale) is plotted against time (x-axis). For week 0, we measured identification performance using consecutive, non-overlapping movement embeddings within a single session. For weeks 1-8, we trained on week-0 data and tested on the subset of participants who returned after 1-8 weeks. The dashed line at 1/4000 denotes chance-level performance. -->

<!-- **B. Mental health prediction over time.** The mean squared error (y-axis) in mental health predictions is plotted against time (x-axis). For week 0, we computed MSE using 5-fold cross-validation across participants in the same session. For weeks 1-8, we used a model trained on all participants’ week-0 data and applied it to those who returned after 1-8 weeks. -->

<!-- **C. Identification versus mental health prediction.** Linear mixed effects modeling indicated no significant effect of identification accuracy on mse ($\mathrm{beta}$ = $`r STAT.identification_to_regression$coefficients[2,1]`$ ± $`r STAT.identification_to_regression$coefficients[2,2]`$, T($`r STAT.identification_to_regression$coefficients[2,3]`$) = $`r STAT.identification_to_regression$coefficients[2,4]`$, p = $`r STAT.identification_to_regression$coefficients[2,5]`$). These findings suggest that cursor movement features encode two distinct types of information: identity and mental health. While identification relies on idiosyncratic movement patterns that differentiate individuals, mental health prediction captures generalizable internal states that reflect psychological distress and wellbeing. -->
